topics.title: Reliability in Terms of
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT754 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Reliability in Terms of

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

**Reliability** is formally defined as the *probability that a system, component, or item will perform its intended function satisfactorily under stated operating conditions, for a specified period of time*.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$R(t) = P(T \geq t)$$

where $T$ is the random variable representing the time-to-failure of the component, and $t$ is the specified mission time. The complementary quantity is the **Unreliability** or **Probability of Failure**:

$$F(t) = P(T < t) = 1 - R(t)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:** Reliability is a *probability*, hence it is a *dimensionless quantity* bounded between **0** and **1** (i.e., $0 \leq R(t) \leq 1$). This is a frequently tested point in KTU examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you buy a **smartphone battery** advertised with a "2-year warranty." The manufacturer's claim is essentially a reliability statement. **Quality** is about *how well* the battery is manufactured (no defects, premium materials). **Reliability** is about *how long* that high-quality battery will keep working in your daily usage. 

- A *high-quality* battery has few manufacturing defects.
- A *high-reliability* battery will not only start with quality but will *survive* 2 years of charge-discharge cycles without significant capacity loss.

So when we say "Reliability in terms of," we are asking: **"Through which lens or mathematical quantity can we best measure, express, and predict this 'time-survival' capability?"**

The most common lenses are:
1. **Reliability in terms of Quality** (inherent capability)
2. **Reliability in terms of Failure Rate / Hazard Rate** (instantaneous risk of failure)
3. **Reliability in terms of MTTF / MTBF** (expected lifetime)
4. **Reliability in terms of Probability** ($R(t)$ function itself)
5. **Reliability in terms of Availability** (operational readiness over time)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Perspective:** Module 1 lays the foundation. The "terms of" perspective reframes the single concept of reliability into multiple *measurable engineering metrics* that can be tracked, tested, and improved.

## 1.3 Standard Reliability Metrics in Industry

| Metric (Abbreviation) | Physical Meaning | Unit |
|------------------------|------------------|------|
| $R(t)$ | Reliability function | Dimensionless |
| $F(t)$ | Cumulative failure distribution | Dimensionless |
| $\lambda(t)$ | Failure (hazard) rate | Failures / hour |
| MTTF | Mean Time To Failure | Hours |
| MTBF | Mean Time Between Failures | Hours |
| MTTR | Mean Time To Repair | Hours |
| A(t) | Steady-state availability | Dimensionless |

> [!TIP]
> For **non-repairable** systems (e.g., a bulb, a fuse, a rocket component), we use **MTTF**. For **repairable** systems (e.g., a car engine, a CNC machine, a server), we use **MTBF**. This is a classic KTU 2024 question trap.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Reliability in Terms of Quality

**Quality** and **Reliability** are *related but distinct* concepts:

- **Quality** is a *static* measure of conformance to specifications at the time of manufacture (Time = 0). It is concerned with *defect prevention* during production.
- **Reliability** is a *dynamic* measure of performance *over time*. It is concerned with *failure prevention* during the operational life.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A product can be of **high quality** but **low reliability** if it is built perfectly but degrades quickly. Conversely, a product can be of **low quality** (some initial defects) but **high reliability** if the defects do not lead to early failure.

## 2.2 Reliability in Terms of the Reliability Function $R(t)$

Starting from the failure distribution function $F(t)$:

$$F(t) = P(T \leq t)$$

The reliability function is the survival function:

$$R(t) = P(T > t) = 1 - F(t)$$

**Properties of $R(t)$:**
- $R(0) = 1$ (a brand-new component is initially reliable with probability 1)
- $R(\infty) = 0$ (eventually, with enough time, everything fails)
- $R(t)$ is a **monotonically non-increasing function** of $t$
- $\frac{dR(t)}{dt} \leq 0$ for all $t \geq 0$

## 2.3 Reliability in Terms of the Probability Density Function (PDF)

The **probability density function of failure** is:

$$f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

Therefore, we can express reliability in terms of the PDF:

$$R(t) = P(T > t) = \int_{t}^{\infty} f(\tau) \, d\tau$$

The fraction of components surviving up to time $t$ equals the area under the PDF curve from $t$ to $\infty$.

## 2.4 Reliability in Terms of Failure Rate (Hazard Rate)

The **instantaneous failure rate** $\lambda(t)$ is the most important reliability metric in engineering practice. It is defined as the *conditional probability of failure in the next infinitesimal interval, given survival up to time $t$*:

$$\lambda(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t < T \leq t + \Delta t \mid T > t)}{\Delta t} = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

This gives us the **fundamental reliability differential equation**:

$$\lambda(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)} = -\lambda(t) \, dt$$

Integrating from $0$ to $t$ with $R(0) = 1$:

$$\int_{0}^{t} \frac{dR(\tau)}{R(\tau)} = -\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(\tau) \, d\tau$$

$$\ln R(t) - \ln R(0) = -\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(\tau) \, d\tau$$

$$\boxed{R(t) = \exp\left(-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(\tau) \, d\tau\right)}$$

For the special case of **constant failure rate** $\lambda(t) = \lambda$ (valid during the useful life phase of the Bathtub Curve):

$$\boxed{R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the famous **Exponential Reliability Law** — the most-tested formula in KTU Module 1.

## 2.5 Reliability in Terms of MTTF / MTBF

The **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** is the expected value of the time-to-failure random variable $T$:

$$\text{MTTF} = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt$$

Using integration by parts ($u = t$, $dv = f(t)dt$, with $R(0)=1$ and $R(\infty)=0$):

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t) \, dt$$

For the exponential distribution with constant failure rate $\lambda$:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt = \left[ -\frac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

Therefore, for the exponential case:

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} \quad \text{and} \quad R(t) = e^{-t/\text{MTTF}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Repairable systems:** $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$. The mean *operating* time between failures is the mean time *to* failure plus the mean time *spent in* repair. Availability is then: $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \dfrac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}}$.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Expression | Equation | Engineering Utility |
|---|------------|----------|--------------------|
| 1 | Reliability Function | $R(t) = 1 - F(t)$ | Defines survival probability |
| 2 | Failure Density | $f(t) = \dfrac{dF(t)}{dt} = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$ | Frequency of failure occurrences |
| 3 | General Reliability | $R(t) = \exp\left(-\int_0^t \lambda(\tau) d\tau\right)$ | Most general form — valid for *any* failure rate profile |
| 4 | Exponential Reliability | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Useful-life phase, memoryless property |
| 5 | Failure Rate | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | Instantaneous risk; core of reliability block diagrams |
| 6 | MTTF (General) | $\text{MTTF} = \int_0^\infty R(t) \, dt$ | Average life of non-repairable item |
| 7 | MTTF (Exponential) | $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Direct link between rate and average life |
| 8 | MTBF | $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$ | Repairable system metric |
| 9 | Steady-State Availability | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Operational readiness fraction |
| 10 | Hazard Relationship | $R(t) = e^{-\int_0^t \lambda(\tau) d\tau}$ | The "umbrella" formula linking all the above |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Writing $R(t) = e^{\lambda t}$. The sign is *negative* because reliability *decreases* with time. Always verify the sign in the exponent.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Mission reliability of rocket engines for a 600-second burn uses $R(600) = e^{-\lambda \cdot 600}$.
- **Electronics:** Manufacturers of electrolytic capacitors specify reliability at rated temperature via the Arrhenius-modified failure rate.
- **Power Systems:** MTBF of transformers is used in grid reliability indices like SAIDI (System Average Interruption Duration Index).
- **Automotive:** MTTF of brake pads is used for warranty period determination and recall planning.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Failure Rate to Reliability Function

We start from the *definition* of conditional probability for survival and failure in an infinitesimal window.

**Step 1: Conditional probability of failure in $[t, t+\Delta t]$, given survival up to $t$:**

$$P(\text{fail in } [t, t+\Delta t] \mid \text{alive at } t) = \frac{P(t < T \leq t + \Delta t)}{P(T > t)} = \frac{F(t+\Delta t) - F(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 2: Divide by $\Delta t$ and take the limit as $\Delta t \to 0$:**

$$\lambda(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{1}{\Delta t} \cdot \frac{F(t+\Delta t) - F(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

where $f(t) = \dfrac{dF(t)}{dt}$.

**Step 3: Substitute $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$:**

Since $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$, we have $\dfrac{dF}{dt} = -\dfrac{dR}{dt}$, so $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR}{dt}$.

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{-dR(t)/dt}{R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 4: Separate variables:**

$$-R(t) \cdot \lambda(t) \, dt = dR(t)$$

$$\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)} = -\lambda(t) \, dt$$

**Step 5: Integrate both sides from $0$ to $t$:**

Applying the boundary condition $R(0) = 1$ (a new unit is reliable with probability 1):

$$\int_{0}^{t} \frac{1}{R(\tau)} dR(\tau) = -\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(\tau) \, d\tau$$

$$\ln R(t) - \ln R(0) = -\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(\tau) \, d\tau$$

**Step 6: Apply $R(0) = 1 \Rightarrow \ln R(0) = 0$ and exponentiate:**

$$\ln R(t) = -\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(\tau) \, d\tau$$

$$\boxed{R(t) = \exp\left(-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(\tau) \, d\tau\right)}$$

**Step 7: For constant failure rate $\lambda(t) = \lambda$:**

$$R(t) = \exp\left(-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda \, d\tau\right) = \exp(-\lambda t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

> This is the most-cited derivation in KTU 2024 Module 1. Marks are awarded for the boundary condition $R(0)=1$ being explicitly stated.

## 3.2 Derivation: MTTF from the Reliability Function

**Step 1: Definition of expected value of a non-negative random variable $T$:**

$$\text{MTTF} = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt$$

**Step 2: Apply integration by parts.**

Let $u = t \Rightarrow du = dt$ and $dv = f(t)dt \Rightarrow v = \int f(t)dt = F(t)$.

But we want to express in terms of $R(t)$. Use the alternative: $v = F(t) = 1 - R(t)$ (since $R(0)=1$, the integration constant is zero).

$$\text{MTTF} = \big[ t \cdot (1 - R(t)) \big]_{0}^{\infty} - \int_{0}^{\infty} (1 - R(t)) \, dt$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the boundary term.**

- At $t = \infty$: $R(\infty) = 0 \Rightarrow (1 - R(\infty)) = 1$, so $t \cdot (1 - R(t)) \to \infty \cdot 1 = \infty$.

We need a more careful integration-by-parts using $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR}{dt}$:

$$\text{MTTF} = -\int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \frac{dR}{dt} \, dt$$

Integration by parts with $u = t$, $du = dt$, $dv = -dR$, $v = -R(t)$:

$$\text{MTTF} = \big[ -t \cdot R(t) \big]_{0}^{\infty} + \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t) \, dt$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the boundary term again.**

- At $t = 0$: $R(0) = 1 \Rightarrow -0 \cdot 1 = 0$.
- At $t = \infty$: For finite-MTTF distributions, $t \cdot R(t) \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$ (this requires $\lambda(t)$ to be non-zero for large $t$).

Therefore the boundary term vanishes, giving:

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t) \, dt}$$

**Step 5: Specialize to the exponential distribution:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt = \left[ \frac{-1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - \left( \frac{-1}{\lambda} \right) = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style)

> **Problem:** A non-repairable electronic component has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ failures/hour. Compute:
> (a) The reliability for a mission time of $t = 1000$ hours.
> (b) The MTTF.
> (c) The reliability for a mission time of $t = 5000$ hours.

**Solution:**

**(a) Reliability at 1000 hours:**

$$R(1000) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(5 \times 10^{-5})(1000)} = e^{-0.05}$$

$$R(1000) = 0.9512 \; \text{(or 95.12\%)}$$

**(b) MTTF:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-5}} = 20{,}000 \; \text{hours}$$

**(c) Reliability at 5000 hours:**

$$R(5000) = e^{-(5 \times 10^{-5})(5000)} = e^{-0.25} = 0.7788 \; \text{(or 77.88\%)}$$

> **Insight:** Notice that at $t = $ MTTF, $R(\text{MTTF}) = e^{-1} \approx 0.3679 \approx 36.8\%$. **Only about 37% of components survive up to their mean life**. This is a counterintuitive but heavily tested KTU result.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Concept Map: "Reliability in Terms of" — The Five Perspectives

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["RELIABILITY R of t"]:::root
    A --> B["In Terms of QUALITY"]
    A --> C["In Terms of FAILURE RATE"]
    A --> D["In Terms of MTTF / MTBF"]
    A --> E["In Terms of PROBABILITY"]
    A --> F["In Terms of AVAILABILITY"]

    B --> B1["Conformance at t = 0"]
    B --> B2["Defect prevention"]
    B --> B3["Static measure"]

    C --> C1["Hazard rate lambda of t"]
    C --> C2["Instantaneous risk"]
    C --> C3["Bathtub curve phases"]

    D --> D1["Average life"]
    D --> D2["1 by lambda for exponential"]
    D --> D3["MTBF = MTTF + MTTR"]

    E --> E1["R of t = 1 minus F of t"]
    E --> E2["exp of minus integral of lambda"]
    E --> E3["Monotonic decreasing"]

    F --> F1["Operational readiness"]
    F --> F2["MTBF by MTBF plus MTTR"]
    F --> F3["Repairable systems"]

    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Bathtub Curve — The Three Failure-Rate Regimes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X["Time t"]:::axis
    X --> Y["lambda of t"]:::axis
    subgraph phases [BATHTUB CURVE REGIMES]
        direction LR
        P1["INFANT MORTALITY"]:::phase1
        P2["USEFUL LIFE"]:::phase2
        P3["WEAR OUT"]:::phase3
    end
    P1 -->|lambda decreasing| P2
    P2 -->|lambda constant| P3
    P3 -->|lambda increasing| END1["FAILURE"]:::end1

    classDef axis fill:#f4f4f4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef phase1 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef phase2 fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    classDef phase3 fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000

```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Reliability Function Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN1["Failure Rate lambda of t"]:::input
    IN2["Boundary R of 0 = 1"]:::input

    IN1 --> M1["Differential Equation dR by dt = minus lambda times R"]:::math
    IN2 --> M1
    M1 --> M2["Integrate 0 to t"]:::math
    M2 --> M3["ln R of t = minus integral of lambda d tau"]:::math
    M3 --> M4["R of t = exp minus integral of lambda d tau"]:::math
    M4 --> OUT1["GENERAL RELIABILITY FUNCTION"]:::output
    M4 --> M5["Special case lambda is constant"]:::math
    M5 --> OUT2["EXPONENTIAL R of t = e to power minus lambda t"]:::output
    OUT2 --> M6["Integrate R of t from 0 to infinity"]:::math
    M6 --> OUT3["MTTF = 1 by lambda"]:::output

    classDef input fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef math fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806600,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define reliability in terms of quality. How is quality different from reliability?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Reliability in terms of quality:** A product is considered *reliable* if it consistently performs its intended function over time, with quality serving as the foundation. Quality refers to the inherent manufacturing excellence — freedom from defects and conformance to design specifications at the time of production (i.e., at $t = 0$).

**Difference between Quality and Reliability:**

| Aspect | Quality | Reliability |
|--------|---------|-------------|
| Time frame | Static, at $t = 0$ | Dynamic, over the life cycle |
| Focus | Defect prevention during manufacture | Failure prevention during operation |
| Measure | Number of defects per million (DPMO) | Probability of survival $R(t)$, MTTF, $\lambda(t)$ |
| Concern | Conformance to specification | Sustained performance over time |

A product can be of *high quality* (no defects) but of *low reliability* (degrades quickly). Likewise, a *low-quality* product can be reliable if its defects are non-critical for survival.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define the term "Failure Rate" $\lambda(t)$ and explain its significance in reliability engineering.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **failure rate** (also called *hazard rate* or *instantaneous failure rate*) is defined as the *conditional probability density of failure at time $t$, given that the component has survived up to time $t$*:

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

**Significance:**
1. It quantifies the *instantaneous risk* of failure at any moment during the component's life.
2. It forms the *bathtub curve* — decreasing in infant mortality, constant in useful life, and increasing in wear-out.
3. It is the primary input for system reliability block diagrams and for computing MTTF, MTBF, and availability.
4. It allows prediction of $R(t) = \exp\left(-\int_0^t \lambda(\tau) d\tau\right)$ for any failure-rate profile.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the general expression for reliability $R(t)$ in terms of the failure rate $\lambda(t)$, starting from the definition of the failure rate. State clearly the boundary condition used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A non-repairable system has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 0.001$ failures/hour. Calculate:
- (i) The reliability for a mission time of 500 hours.
- (ii) The Mean Time To Failure (MTTF).
- (iii) The probability that the system will fail before completing 2000 hours of operation. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the failure rate using conditional probability.

The probability that a component fails in the interval $[t, t+\Delta t]$, given that it has survived up to time $t$:

$$P(t < T \leq t + \Delta t \mid T > t) = \frac{F(t + \Delta t) - F(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Divide by $\Delta t$ and take the limit:

$$\lambda(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{1}{\Delta t} \cdot \frac{F(t + \Delta t) - F(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Substitute $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$:

$$\lambda(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Separate variables and integrate:

$$\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)} = -\lambda(t) \, dt \quad \Rightarrow \quad \int_0^t \frac{dR(\tau)}{R(\tau)} = -\int_0^t \lambda(\tau) \, d\tau$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Apply boundary condition $R(0) = 1$:

$$\ln R(t) - \ln R(0) = -\int_0^t \lambda(\tau) \, d\tau \quad \Rightarrow \quad \ln R(t) = -\int_0^t \lambda(\tau) \, d\tau$$

**Step 6 (2 marks):** Exponentiate to obtain:

$$\boxed{R(t) = \exp\left(-\int_0^t \lambda(\tau) \, d\tau\right)}$$

For constant failure rate $\lambda(t) = \lambda$, this reduces to $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

Given: $\lambda = 0.001$ failures/hour, non-repairable system.

**(i) Reliability at $t = 500$ hours (2 marks):**

$$R(500) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(0.001)(500)} = e^{-0.5} = 0.6065$$

**Reliability = 60.65%.**

**(ii) Mean Time To Failure (2 marks):**

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.001} = 1000 \; \text{hours}$$

**MTTF = 1000 hours.**

**(iii) Probability of failure before 2000 hours (3 marks):**

The probability of failure before time $t$ is $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$:

$$F(2000) = 1 - e^{-(0.001)(2000)} = 1 - e^{-2} = 1 - 0.1353 = 0.8647$$

**Probability of failure = 86.47%.**

Note that this is *not* $1 - 0.5 = 0.5$, because at $t = 2 \times \text{MTTF}$, $R = e^{-2} \approx 0.135$, not $0.5$. A common student error is to assume that at twice the MTTF, the reliability is $50\%$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** What is MTTF? Derive the relationship MTTF $= \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ for a component following the exponential reliability law. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a repairable system, the MTTF is 4000 hours and the MTTR is 50 hours. Find:
- (i) The MTBF.
- (ii) The steady-state availability.
- (iii) The reliability for a 100-hour mission, assuming constant failure rate. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) MTTF Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Definition. The Mean Time To Failure is the expected (average) time of operation before failure of a non-repairable component:

$$\text{MTTF} = E[T] = \int_0^\infty t \cdot f(t) \, dt$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Substitute $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$:

$$\text{MTTF} = -\int_0^\infty t \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt} \, dt$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Use integration by parts: $u = t$, $du = dt$, $dv = -dR$, $v = -R$:

$$\text{MTTF} = \left[ -t \cdot R(t) \right]_0^\infty + \int_0^\infty R(t) \, dt$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Boundary term vanishes since $R(\infty) = 0$ (and $t \cdot R(t) \to 0$ for distributions with finite MTTF) and $t = 0$ at the lower limit:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_0^\infty R(t) \, dt$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** For exponential law, $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_0^\infty e^{-\lambda t} \, dt = \left[ -\frac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t} \right]_0^\infty$$

**Step 6 (2 marks):** Evaluate:

$$\text{MTTF} = 0 - \left( -\frac{1}{\lambda} \right) = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}}$$

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

Given: MTTF = 4000 hours, MTTR = 50 hours.

**(i) MTBF (2 marks):**

$$\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR} = 4000 + 50 = 4050 \; \text{hours}$$

**(ii) Steady-State Availability (2 marks):**

$$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{4050}{4050 + 50} = \frac{4050}{4100} = 0.9878$$

**Availability = 98.78%.**

**(iii) Reliability for 100-hour mission (3 marks):**

Since MTTF = 4000 hours, for exponential distribution $\lambda = 1/\text{MTTF}$:

$$\lambda = \frac{1}{4000} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{failures/hour}$$

$$R(100) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(2.5 \times 10^{-4})(100)} = e^{-0.025} = 0.9753$$

**Reliability = 97.53%.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Zones (verified from KTU 2024 valuation keys):**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in Exponent:** Writing $R(t) = e^{\lambda t}$ instead of $e^{-\lambda t}$ — *loses 1–2 marks instantly*.
> 2. **Forgetting the Boundary Condition $R(0) = 1$:** Examiners specifically test this in derivations — *loses 1 mark* if not stated.
> 3. **Confusing MTTF with MTBF:** MTTF applies to **non-repairable** systems; MTBF to **repairable** systems. Mixing these is a *2-mark penalty* in descriptive questions.
> 4. **Assuming $R(\text{MTTF}) = 0.5$:** For exponential, $R(\text{MTTF}) = e^{-1} \approx 0.368$, NOT $0.5$. This is a *favourite trick question*.
> 5. **Skipping the "Given that..." statement** in failure-rate definition: Always write it as a *conditional probability*. Lose 1 mark for a bare equation.
> 6. **Units:** Failure rate is in *failures per hour* (or per cycle, per mission, per km). MTTF is in *hours*. Reliability is *dimensionless*.
> 7. **In numerical problems:** Not showing the intermediate step $\lambda t$ value before exponentiation loses partial marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 1 — "Reliability in Terms of":**
>
> - **Reliability $R(t)$ is a probability**, always in the interval $[0, 1]$, and **monotonically non-increasing** in $t$.
> - **Boundary conditions:** $R(0) = 1$ and $R(\infty) = 0$.
> - **Quality $\neq$ Reliability.** Quality is *static* (at $t=0$); reliability is *dynamic* (over the mission time).
> - **The five "terms of" perspectives** are: Quality, Failure Rate $\lambda(t)$, MTTF/MTBF, Probability $R(t)$, and Availability $A$.
> - **Master equation:** $R(t) = \exp\left(-\int_0^t \lambda(\tau) \, d\tau\right)$. This single equation connects *all* the metrics.
> - **Exponential Law:** Valid during the *useful life* phase of the bathtub curve where $\lambda$ is constant: $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.
> - **MTTF (Exponential)** $= 1/\lambda$. **MTBF** (repairable) $= \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$.
> - **Availability:** $A = \text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$.
> - **MTTF and the 37% rule:** At $t = \text{MTTF}$, only $\approx 36.8\%$ of units survive — a classic KTU trick.
> - **The failure rate $\lambda(t)$ is the conditional probability of failure** in the next instant, given survival — always state it as a *conditional probability*, not a bare ratio.
> - **Units discipline:** $\lambda$ in failures/hour, MTTF/MTBF/MTTR in hours, $R(t)$ and $A$ dimensionless.
> - **For any KTU 2024 derivation question**, always write: (1) the starting definition, (2) the mathematical manipulation, (3) the boundary condition, and (4) the final boxed result with units where applicable.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Failure Rate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT754 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Failure Rate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Reliability Engineering**, the **Failure Rate** $\lambda(t)$ of a non-repairable component or system is formally defined as the **instantaneous rate of failure at time $t$**, conditional upon the fact that the component has survived up to that time $t$.

Mathematically, it is the limit of the conditional probability of failure in a small time interval $\Delta t$ as $\Delta t \to 0$:

$$\lambda(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t < T \le t + \Delta t \mid T > t)}{\Delta t}$$

where $T$ is the random variable denoting the **Time-To-Failure (TTF)** of the component.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> The failure rate at time $t$ is the ratio of the probability density function of failure $f(t)$ to the reliability function $R(t)$ at that instant. It represents the **risk of failure** for a component that has already survived until time $t$.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Car Tire" Analogy

Imagine driving a **car tire** (non-repairable component):

- When the tire is **brand new** (just installed), it has a *very low* probability of bursting in the next second because manufacturing defects are rare.
- After the tire has been running for **30,000 km** (useful life), the failure rate is roughly **constant** — the risk per km driven doesn't change much.
- After **60,000 km** (wear-out phase), the rubber degrades, and the chance of blowout in the next km **skyrockets**.

This is exactly what the **Bathtub Curve** captures for failure rate $\lambda(t)$ over the life of a system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Units of Failure Rate:**
> The failure rate $\lambda(t)$ is measured in **failures per unit time**, commonly expressed as:
> - **failures/hour**
> - **fit** (failures in time, where 1 fit = $10^{-9}$ failures/hour — used in electronics)
> - **% per 1000 hours** (used in mechanical components)

## 1.3 Three Philosophical Regions of Failure

| Region | Name | Behavior of $\lambda(t)$ | Engineering Cause |
|---|---|---|---|
| I | **Infant Mortality** (Burn-in) | Decreasing | Manufacturing defects, poor quality control |
| II | **Useful Life** (Random Failures) | Approximately **Constant** | Random external shocks, environmental stress |
| III | **Wear-Out** | Increasing | Aging, corrosion, fatigue, material degradation |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logical Chain — Deriving the Failure Rate from First Principles

Let us build the failure rate definition step-by-step, which is a favorite KTU derivation:

**Step 1 — Unconditional Probability of Failure in $(t, t+\Delta t]$:**
$$P(t < T \le t + \Delta t) = F(t+\Delta t) - F(t) = f(t) \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 2 — Conditional Probability Given Survival Up To $t$:**
$$P(t < T \le t+\Delta t \mid T > t) = \frac{P(t < T \le t+\Delta t)}{P(T > t)} = \frac{f(t) \cdot \Delta t}{R(t)}$$

**Step 3 — Take the Limit as $\Delta t \to 0$:**
$$\lambda(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{f(t) \cdot \Delta t}{R(t) \cdot \Delta t} = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Master Equation of Failure Rate:**
> $$\boxed{\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}}$$
> This is the single most important formula for this module.

**Step 4 — Express in Terms of Reliability Function Only:**

Since $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$, we substitute:

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{-\frac{dR(t)}{dt}}{R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

This is recognized as the **negative logarithmic derivative**:

$$\boxed{\lambda(t) = -\frac{d}{dt} \left[ \ln R(t) \right]}$$

**Step 5 — Invert to Find Reliability from Failure Rate:**

Integrating both sides from $0$ to $t$:

$$\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(u) \, du = -\ln R(t) + \ln R(0)$$

Since $R(0) = 1$, we obtain the **fundamental reliability integral**:

$$\boxed{R(t) = \exp\left(-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(u) \, du\right)}$$

For a **constant failure rate** $\lambda(t) = \lambda$ (useful life region):

$$\boxed{R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}}$$

This is the **Exponential Distribution**, central to all of reliability engineering.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Use $\vert$ for absolute value in math, NEVER the pipe character in tables.**

| \# | Formula | Description / Conditions | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | Instantaneous failure rate (general) | failures/time |
| 2 | $\lambda(t) = -\dfrac{d}{dt}\left[\ln R(t)\right]$ | Failure rate via reliability function | failures/time |
| 3 | $R(t) = \exp\left(-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(u)\, du\right)$ | Reliability from failure rate (general) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Reliability under **constant** $\lambda$ (exponential) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $f(t) = \lambda(t) \cdot R(t)$ | Failure density via failure rate | 1/time |
| 6 | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Mean Time Between Failures (constant $\lambda$ only) | time |
| 7 | $R(t) = e^{-t/\text{MTBF}}$ | Reliability expressed via MTBF | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\lambda(t) = \beta \eta^{\beta} t^{\beta-1}$ | Weibull failure rate (generalized) | 1/time |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Electronics Industry**: A microprocessor with $\lambda = 500$ fit means we expect $500 \times 10^{-9}$ failures per hour. Over 10,000 units running for 1 year (≈ 8,760 hrs), expected failures ≈ 0.00438 per unit.
- **Aerospace**: Failure rate of critical components is modeled to schedule preventive replacements **before** Region III.
- **Power Systems**: Circuit breakers with constant failure rates during useful life allow simple series/parallel reliability block diagram analysis.
- **Automotive**: Brake pad failure rate follows Weibull with $\beta > 1$ (wear-out dominated), guiding replacement intervals.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Failure Rate to the Exponential Distribution

We are given a **constant failure rate**: $\lambda(t) = \lambda$ (valid in Region II of the bathtub curve).

Starting with the fundamental relationship:
$$\lambda(t) = -\frac{d}{dt}\left[\ln R(t)\right]$$

Substitute $\lambda(t) = \lambda$:
$$\lambda = -\frac{d}{dt}\left[\ln R(t)\right]$$

Rearrange:
$$\frac{d}{dt}\left[\ln R(t)\right] = -\lambda$$

Integrate both sides with respect to $t$:
$$\int \frac{d}{dt}\left[\ln R(t)\right] dt = \int -\lambda \, dt$$

$$\ln R(t) = -\lambda t + C$$

Apply the **boundary condition** at $t = 0$: We know $R(0) = 1$ (a new component is certainly reliable at time zero).

$$\ln R(0) = \ln(1) = 0 = -\lambda(0) + C \implies C = 0$$

Substitute back:
$$\ln R(t) = -\lambda t$$

Exponentiate both sides:
$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

This is the **Exponential Reliability Function**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Failure Density from Exponential Reliability

Given:
$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

The cumulative distribution function of failure is:
$$F(t) = 1 - R(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$$

The probability density function of failure is the derivative of $F(t)$:
$$f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\left[1 - e^{-\lambda t}\right] = 0 - (-\lambda) e^{-\lambda t} = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Verification using the master equation** $\lambda(t) = f(t)/R(t)$:
$$\lambda(t) = \frac{\lambda e^{-\lambda t}}{e^{-\lambda t}} = \lambda \quad \checkmark$$

The constant cancels out, confirming the constant failure rate property. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) for Constant Failure Rate

The MTTF is the expected value of the time-to-failure random variable:
$$\text{MTTF} = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt$$

Substitute $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$:
$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

This integral is solved using **integration by parts**. Let $u = t$ and $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda t} dt$, so $du = dt$ and $v = -e^{-\lambda t}$.

$$\text{MTTF} = \left[-t e^{-\lambda t}\right]_{0}^{\infty} - \int_{0}^{\infty} \left(-e^{-\lambda t}\right) dt$$

Evaluate the boundary term:
$$\lim_{t \to \infty} t e^{-\lambda t} = 0 \quad \text{(exponential dominates polynomial)}$$
$$\text{At } t=0: \quad 0 \cdot e^{0} = 0$$

So the first term vanishes:
$$\text{MTTF} = 0 + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} dt = \left[-\frac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t}\right]_{0}^{\infty}$$

$$\text{MTTF} = \left(0 - \left(-\frac{1}{\lambda}\right)\right) = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

Therefore, the **MTBF = MTTF = $\dfrac{1}{\lambda}$** for a constant failure rate system. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:** A non-repairable system has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 0.0002$ failures/hour. Compute (a) the reliability for 1000 hours, (b) the MTTF, and (c) the failure density at $t = 1000$ hours.

**Solution:**

**(a)** Reliability at $t = 1000$ hours:
$$R(1000) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(0.0002)(1000)} = e^{-0.2} \approx 0.8187$$

**(b)** Mean Time To Failure:
$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.0002} = 5000 \text{ hours}$$

**(c)** Failure density at $t = 1000$ hours:
$$f(1000) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t} = 0.0002 \times e^{-0.2} = 0.0002 \times 0.8187 \approx 1.637 \times 10^{-4} \text{ per hour}$$

**Verification using master equation:**
$$\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{1.637 \times 10^{-4}}{0.8187} = 2.0 \times 10^{-4} = 0.0002 \text{ failures/hour} \quad \checkmark$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Quantity | Symbol | Constant $\lambda$ Case | General Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reliability | $R(t)$ | $e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\exp\left(-\int_0^t \lambda(u)\,du\right)$ |
| Failure Density | $f(t)$ | $\lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda(t) \cdot R(t)$ |
| CDF | $F(t)$ | $1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | $1 - R(t)$ |
| Hazard (Failure) Rate | $\lambda(t)$ | $\lambda$ (constant) | $\dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ |
| MTTF | $E[T]$ | $1/\lambda$ | $\int_0^\infty R(t)\,dt$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bathtub Curve — Failure Rate vs Time

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["INFANT MORTALITY"] --> B["USEFUL LIFE"]
    B --> C["WEAR OUT REGION"]
    A1["Lambda decreases from high value"] -.-> A
    B1["Lambda is approximately constant"] -.-> B
    C1["Lambda increases sharply"] -.-> C
    A --> A2["Cause: Manufacturing defects, poor QC, debugging"]
    B --> B2["Cause: Random shocks, environmental stress, overload"]
    C --> C2["Cause: Aging, corrosion, fatigue, wear"]
    style A fill:#ffe5e5
    style B fill:#e5ffe5
    style C fill:#fff5e5
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Relationships Between R(t), F(t), f(t), and λ(t)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Reliability Function R(t)"] --> B["Failure Density f(t) = -dR/dt"]
    A --> C["Failure Rate lambda t = f/R"]
    B --> C
    C --> D["Integrated Hazard Function H(t)"]
    D --> A
    A --> E["CDF F(t) = 1 - R(t)"]
    A --> F["MTTF = integral of R(t) dt from 0 to infinity"]
    style A fill:#cce5ff
    style B fill:#e5ccff
    style C fill:#ffccff
    style D fill:#ffffcc
    style E fill:#ccffe5
    style F fill:#ffe5cc
```

## 4.3 Hazard Function Behavior Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start["Is lambda t constant?"] --> Q1{"Yes, lambda is constant"}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Exp["Use Exponential Distribution: R t = e to power minus lambda t"]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{"Is lambda t increasing?"}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Wei["Use Weibull Distribution with beta greater than 1"]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{"Is lambda t decreasing?"}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Wei2["Use Weibull Distribution with beta less than 1"]
    Q3 -->|No| Gen["Use General Non-Parametric Model"]
    style Start fill:#ffcccc
    style Exp fill:#ccffcc
    style Wei fill:#ccccff
    style Wei2 fill:#ccccff
    style Gen fill:#ffffcc
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define the term **failure rate** of a component. State its units.

**Model Answer:**
The failure rate $\lambda(t)$ of a non-repairable component is defined as the **conditional probability density of failure at time $t$**, given that the component has survived up to time $t$. Mathematically:

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

It represents the **instantaneous risk of failure** at age $t$.

**Units:** Failures per unit time. Common practical units are **% per 1000 hours** (mechanical) or **fit** (failures in $10^9$ hours) for electronic components.

*(3 marks: 2 for definition + 1 for units)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Distinguish between the **three regions** of the bathtub curve with respect to failure rate.

**Model Answer:**

| Region | Failure Rate Behavior | Cause |
|---|---|---|
| I — Infant Mortality | $\lambda(t)$ **decreases** with time | Manufacturing defects, debugging |
| II — Useful Life | $\lambda(t)$ **constant** (lowest value) | Random external shocks, environmental stress |
| III — Wear-out | $\lambda(t)$ **increases** rapidly | Aging, corrosion, material fatigue |

The component is most reliable during **Region II** and most failure-prone in **Region III**.

*(3 marks: 1 per region)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Students often confuse **failure rate** with **probability of failure**. Failure rate is a *density* conditional on survival, not a probability.
> - Always carry the **units** in numerical answers. Marks are cut if the MTTF answer omits "hours" or "years".
> - In Weibull problems, do **not** assume $\beta = 1$ unless explicitly stated. Identify the regime from the problem text.

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** The reliability of a mechanical sub-system is given by $R(t) = e^{-0.001 t^2}$ for $t \ge 0$.

**(a)** Determine the failure rate $\lambda(t)$ of the sub-system and identify which bathtub curve region it represents. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Calculate the reliability, failure density, and MTTF of the system. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a):

**Step 1** — Apply the failure rate formula:
$$\lambda(t) = -\frac{d}{dt}\left[\ln R(t)\right]$$

**Step 2** — Compute the logarithm:
$$\ln R(t) = \ln\left(e^{-0.001 t^2}\right) = -0.001 t^2$$

**Step 3** — Differentiate:
$$\frac{d}{dt}\left[\ln R(t)\right] = -0.002 t$$

**Step 4** — Apply the negative sign:
$$\lambda(t) = -(-0.002 t) = 0.002 t \quad \text{failures/hour}$$

**Step 5** — Identify the region:
Since $\lambda(t) = 0.002 t$ is **linearly increasing** with $t$, this represents the **Wear-out Region (Region III)** of the bathtub curve.

*(7 marks: 2 for formula, 3 for computation, 2 for region identification)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b):

**Step 1** — Reliability at any $t$:
$$\boxed{R(t) = e^{-0.001 t^2}}$$

**Step 2** — Failure density:
$$f(t) = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt} = -\frac{d}{dt}\left[e^{-0.001 t^2}\right] = 0.002 t \cdot e^{-0.001 t^2}$$

**Step 3** — Verification using master equation:
$$\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{0.002 t \cdot e^{-0.001 t^2}}{e^{-0.001 t^2}} = 0.002 t \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4** — MTTF by integration:
$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-0.001 t^2} \, dt$$

**Step 5** — Use the standard Gaussian integral:
$$\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-a t^2} dt = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{\pi}{a}}$$

With $a = 0.001$:
$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{\pi}{0.001}} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{3141.59} \approx \frac{1}{2}(56.05) \approx 28.03 \text{ hours}$$

*(7 marks: 2 for reliability, 2 for failure density, 3 for MTTF)*

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** A batch of 1000 identical bulbs is put on life test. The following data is observed:

| Interval (hours) | Failures during interval |
|---|---|
| 0 – 100 | 50 |
| 100 – 200 | 30 |
| 200 – 300 | 20 |
| 300 – 400 | 25 |
| 400 – 500 | 40 |
| 500 – 600 | 65 |

**(a)** Calculate the failure rate for each interval and sketch the failure rate curve. Identify the bathtub region. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Estimate the average failure rate and the MTTF for the useful life region. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a):

**Step 1** — Use the empirical failure rate formula:
$$\lambda_i = \frac{n_i}{N_{s,i} \cdot \Delta t}$$

where $n_i$ = failures in interval, $N_{s,i}$ = survivors at start of interval, $\Delta t = 100$ hrs.

| Interval | Survivors at start $N_{s,i}$ | Failures $n_i$ | Failure rate $\lambda_i$ (per hr) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 – 100 | 1000 | 50 | $50/(1000 \times 100) = 5.0 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| 100 – 200 | 950 | 30 | $30/(950 \times 100) = 3.16 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| 200 – 300 | 920 | 20 | $20/(920 \times 100) = 2.17 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| 300 – 400 | 900 | 25 | $25/(900 \times 100) = 2.78 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| 400 – 500 | 875 | 40 | $40/(875 \times 100) = 4.57 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| 500 – 600 | 835 | 65 | $65/(835 \times 100) = 7.78 \times 10^{-4}$ |

**Step 2** — Behavior:
- 0 – 300 hrs: Failure rate **decreases** → **Infant Mortality Region**
- 300 – 400 hrs: Failure rate **lowest and stable** → **Useful Life Region (constant $\lambda$)**
- 400 – 600 hrs: Failure rate **increases** → **Wear-out Region**

*(7 marks: 4 for table, 2 for sketch description, 1 for region identification)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b):

**Step 1** — Useful life region: 300 – 400 hrs with $\lambda \approx 2.78 \times 10^{-4}$ /hr.

**Step 2** — Average failure rate (centered on useful life region):
$$\lambda_{\text{avg}} \approx 2.78 \times 10^{-4} \text{ failures/hour}$$

**Step 3** — MTTF for constant failure rate:
$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda_{\text{avg}}} = \frac{1}{2.78 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 3597 \text{ hours}$$

This means the average bulb lasts approximately **3600 hours** during its useful operating life.

*(7 marks: 2 for avg lambda, 2 for MTTF formula, 3 for final numerical value with units)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Failure Rate Module**

- ☐ **Definition**: $\lambda(t) = f(t)/R(t)$ — the *conditional* failure density at age $t$.
- ☐ **Logarithmic form**: $\lambda(t) = -\dfrac{d}{dt}\left[\ln R(t)\right]$.
- ☐ **Inversion formula**: $R(t) = \exp\left(-\int_0^t \lambda(u)\,du\right)$.
- ☐ **Constant $\lambda$** $\Rightarrow$ **Exponential distribution**: $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.
- ☐ **MTTF for constant $\lambda$**: $\dfrac{1}{\lambda}$.
- ☐ **Three bathtub regions**: Infant Mortality (decreasing $\lambda$) → Useful Life (constant $\lambda$) → Wear-out (increasing $\lambda$).
- ☐ **Units to remember**: fit ($10^{-9}$/hr), %/1000 hrs, failures/hour.
- ☐ **Master equation to verify**: $\lambda(t) = f(t)/R(t)$ must always hold.
- ☐ **Weibull failure rate** form: $\lambda(t) = \beta \eta^{\beta} t^{\beta-1}$ — useful for non-constant $\lambda$ problems.
- ☐ **Numerical pitfall**: Always compute survivors $N_{s,i}$ at the *start* of the interval, not the end.
- ☐ **Probability vs Rate**: $\lambda(t)$ is a *rate* (1/time), **not** a dimensionless probability.
- ☐ **Boundary condition**: $R(0) = 1$ (certain reliability at $t=0$).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Failure Density - Relation Between Reliability, Failure Density and Hazard Rate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT754 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Failure Density - Relation Between Reliability, Failure Density and Hazard Rate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Failure Density Function $f(t)$

The **Failure Density Function (FDF)**, denoted $f(t)$, is defined as the probability that a component, system, or item fails within an infinitesimally small time interval $[t, t+\Delta t]$, normalized by the length of that interval, in the limit as $\Delta t \to 0$.

Mathematically, it is the probability density function (PDF) of the **time-to-failure** random variable $T$:

$$f(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t \le T \le t+\Delta t)}{\Delta t} = \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

where $F(t)$ is the Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) and $R(t)$ is the Reliability function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> $f(t)$ is **NOT a probability** in itself — it is a probability *density*. The actual probability of failure in interval $[t_1, t_2]$ is given by $\int_{t_1}^{t_2} f(t)\,dt$.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a busy city intersection over 24 hours.
- The **number of accidents per hour** is highest during morning rush (8–10 AM) and evening rush (5–7 PM).
- The **accident rate** at any given hour is analogous to the **Failure Density** $f(t)$.
- If you integrate the rate over the entire day, you get the total probability/expected number of accidents.

Similarly, $f(t)$ tells you **"how likely is failure at exactly time $t$"** — it is the instantaneous rate of failure occurrence at time $t$. It is analogous to the **velocity** of failure accumulation.

---

## 1.2 Reliability Function $R(t)$

The **Reliability Function** $R(t)$ is the probability that a component performs its intended function **without failure for a specified duration** $[0, t]$.

$$R(t) = P(T > t) = \int_{t}^{\infty} f(x)\,dx$$

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a brand-new car.
- $R(0) = 1$ → on day zero, the car is guaranteed to work (100% reliable).
- $R(5\,\text{years}) = 0.7$ → after 5 years, there is a 70% chance the car is still operational without major failure.

Reliability answers the question: **"What is the probability that this item will survive beyond time $t$?"**

---

## 1.3 Hazard Rate (Failure Rate) $h(t)$ or $\lambda(t)$

The **Hazard Rate** $h(t)$ (also called the **Instantaneous Failure Rate**) is the conditional probability density that a component fails at time $t$, **given that it has survived up to time $t$**.

$$h(t) = \lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

A useful analogy: consider a group of **100 light bulbs** placed in service at $t = 0$.
- At time $t$, suppose **80 bulbs** are still working.
- In the next tiny interval $dt$, suppose **5 bulbs** burn out.
- The hazard rate is essentially: *"Out of the 80 that survived until time $t$, what fraction fails in the next instant?"*

So $h(t)$ is **conditional on survival** — it does not include items that have already failed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:**
> - $f(t)$ = unconditional failure density (includes already-failed items in the denominator concept).
> - $h(t)$ = conditional failure density (denominator is only the survivors).

---

## 1.4 Properties of the Four Fundamental Functions

For a non-negative time-to-failure random variable $T$ with PDF $f(t)$:

| Property | Mathematical Form | Meaning |
|----------|-------------------|---------|
| Total Probability | $\int_0^\infty f(t)\,dt = 1$ | All units will fail eventually (with probability 1) |
| Reliability Bound | $R(0) = 1$ | New unit is 100% reliable |
| Failure Bound | $R(\infty) = 0$ | Eventually every unit fails |
| Monotonicity | $R(t)$ is non-increasing | Reliability decreases (or stays same) over time |
| Density Positivity | $f(t) \ge 0$ | Probabilities are non-negative |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Foundations

### 2.1.1 Unreliability (CDF) $F(t)$

The **Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF)**, also called the **Unreliability Function**, is the probability that the time-to-failure $T$ is less than or equal to $t$:

$$F(t) = P(T \le t) = \int_0^t f(x)\,dx$$

Since the unit either survives beyond $t$ or fails by $t$, we have the **complement rule**:

$$R(t) + F(t) = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad F(t) = 1 - R(t)$$

### 2.1.2 Relationship Between $f(t)$ and $R(t)$

Differentiating $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$ with respect to $t$:

$$\frac{dF(t)}{dt} = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

Since by definition $f(t) = \dfrac{dF(t)}{dt}$, we obtain:

$$\boxed{\,f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}\,}$$

This is one of the **most heavily tested KTU relations**.

### 2.1.3 Relationship Between $h(t)$, $f(t)$, and $R(t)$

By the definition of conditional probability density:

$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} \quad \text{(provided } R(t) > 0\text{)}$$

Substituting $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$:

$$h(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt} = -\frac{d}{dt}\bigl[\ln R(t)\bigr]$$

### 2.1.4 Reliability Expressed via Hazard Rate

Rearranging and integrating both sides from $0$ to $t$:

$$-\int_0^t \frac{1}{R(x)} \cdot \frac{dR(x)}{dx}\,dx = \int_0^t h(x)\,dx$$

$$\int_0^t d\bigl[-\ln R(x)\bigr] = \int_0^t h(x)\,dx$$

$$-\ln R(t) + \ln R(0) = \int_0^t h(x)\,dx$$

Since $R(0) = 1$, $\ln R(0) = 0$:

$$\ln R(t) = -\int_0^t h(x)\,dx$$

$$\boxed{\,R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_0^t h(x)\,dx\right)\,}$$

### 2.1.5 Unreliability via Hazard Rate

$$F(t) = 1 - R(t) = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\int_0^t h(x)\,dx\right)$$

---

## 2.2 The Four Key Functions — Visual Summary

| Function | Symbol | Probabilistic Meaning | Mathematical Form |
|----------|--------|------------------------|-------------------|
| Failure Density | $f(t)$ | Density of failure at exactly $t$ | $f(t) \ge 0,\ \int_0^\infty f(t)\,dt = 1$ |
| Reliability | $R(t)$ | Survival beyond $t$ | $R(t) = \int_t^\infty f(x)\,dx$ |
| Unreliability (CDF) | $F(t)$ | Failure by time $t$ | $F(t) = \int_0^t f(x)\,dx = 1 - R(t)$ |
| Hazard Rate | $h(t)$ | Conditional failure rate at $t$ | $h(t) = f(t)/R(t)$ |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Maintenance scheduling:** $h(t)$ determines whether to schedule preventive replacement (wear-out region) or run-to-failure (constant failure rate region).
- **Warranty design:** Manufacturers use $R(t)$ to determine warranty period (often $R(t) \ge 0.9$ for warranty duration).
- **Component derating:** Engineers derate components to push operation into the useful-life region where $h(t) \approx \text{constant}$.
- **System reliability prediction:** $R(t)$ of individual components feeds into series/parallel system reliability models.
- **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF):** Computed as $\int_0^\infty R(t)\,dt$ — directly uses reliability function.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to "show the relation between $R(t)$, $f(t)$, and $h(t)$", always begin with the **defining relation** $h(t) = f(t)/R(t)$ and then substitute. Marks are awarded for the *starting point*, not just the final expression.

---

## 2.4 Special Case: Exponential Distribution

If $h(t) = \lambda = \text{constant}$ (memoryless, useful-life region), then:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}, \quad f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}, \quad F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$$

The **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** becomes:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_0^\infty R(t)\,dt = \int_0^\infty e^{-\lambda t}\,dt = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

This is the most commonly tested distribution in KTU exams for the first module.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: $R(t)$ in terms of $f(t)$

**Statement:** $R(t) = \int_t^\infty f(x)\,dx$

**Proof:**

By definition, the CDF is:

$$F(t) = P(T \le t) = \int_0^t f(x)\,dx$$

Since the total probability over $[0, \infty)$ is unity:

$$\int_0^\infty f(x)\,dx = 1$$

We can split the integral:

$$\int_0^\infty f(x)\,dx = \int_0^t f(x)\,dx + \int_t^\infty f(x)\,dx$$

$$1 = F(t) + \int_t^\infty f(x)\,dx$$

But $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$, so:

$$1 = (1 - R(t)) + \int_t^\infty f(x)\,dx$$

$$R(t) = \int_t^\infty f(x)\,dx \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$

**Statement:** The failure density is the negative derivative of reliability.

**Proof:**

Starting from the result of Derivation 1:

$$R(t) = \int_t^\infty f(x)\,dx$$

Differentiate both sides with respect to $t$ using the Leibniz rule for differentiating an integral with variable lower limit:

$$\frac{dR(t)}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\int_t^\infty f(x)\,dx = -f(t)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}\,} \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: $h(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$

**Statement:** Hazard rate equals failure density divided by reliability.

**Proof:**

By the definition of **conditional probability density**:

$$h(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t \le T \le t+\Delta t \mid T > t)}{\Delta t}$$

Using the definition of conditional probability, $P(A\mid B) = \dfrac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$:

$$h(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{1}{\Delta t} \cdot \frac{P(t \le T \le t+\Delta t)}{P(T > t)}$$

$$h(t) = \frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t \le T \le t+\Delta t)}{\Delta t}$$

$$h(t) = \frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot f(t)$$

$$\boxed{\,h(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}\,} \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: $R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\displaystyle\int_0^t h(x)\,dx\right)$

**Statement:** Reliability expressed purely in terms of hazard rate.

**Proof:**

From Derivation 3:

$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$h(t)\,R(t) = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

$$h(t)\,dt = -\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)}$$

Integrate both sides from $0$ to $t$:

$$\int_0^t h(x)\,dx = -\int_0^t \frac{dR(x)}{R(x)}$$

$$\int_0^t h(x)\,dx = -\bigl[\ln R(x)\bigr]_0^t$$

$$\int_0^t h(x)\,dx = -\ln R(t) + \ln R(0)$$

Since $R(0) = 1$, $\ln R(0) = 0$:

$$\int_0^t h(x)\,dx = -\ln R(t)$$

$$\ln R(t) = -\int_0^t h(x)\,dx$$

Exponentiating both sides:

$$\boxed{\,R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_0^t h(x)\,dx\right)\,} \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Verification on Exponential Distribution

**Given:** $h(t) = \lambda$ (constant). Find $R(t)$, $f(t)$, and $F(t)$.

**Step 1 — Compute $R(t)$:**

$$R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_0^t \lambda\,dx\right) = \exp(-\lambda t)$$

**Step 2 — Compute $f(t)$:**

$$f(t) = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt} = -\frac{d}{dt}e^{-\lambda t} = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $F(t)$:**

$$F(t) = 1 - R(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 4 — Verify the relation $h(t) = f(t)/R(t)$:**

$$h(t) = \frac{\lambda e^{-\lambda t}}{e^{-\lambda t}} = \lambda \quad \checkmark$$

This confirms internal consistency of the three relations.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example: Weibull Distribution Verification

**Given:** Hazard rate $h(t) = \alpha \beta t^{\beta - 1}$ where $\alpha > 0$, $\beta > 0$.

**Step 1 — Compute $R(t)$:**

$$R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_0^t \alpha \beta x^{\beta - 1}\,dx\right)$$

$$R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\alpha \beta \cdot \frac{x^{\beta}}{\beta}\bigg|_0^t\right)$$

$$R(t) = \exp(-\alpha t^{\beta})$$

**Step 2 — Compute $f(t)$:**

$$f(t) = h(t) \cdot R(t) = \alpha \beta t^{\beta - 1} \cdot e^{-\alpha t^{\beta}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $F(t)$:**

$$F(t) = 1 - e^{-\alpha t^{\beta}}$$

All three relations are **mutually consistent**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

**Reading the Bathtub Curve:**

| Region | Time Period | Hazard Rate Behaviour | Engineering Action |
|--------|-------------|------------------------|-------------------|
| Infant Mortality | Early life | Decreasing | Burn-in / debugging |
| Useful Life | Middle | Constant (lowest) | Normal operation |
| Wear-Out | Late life | Increasing | Preventive replacement |

```mermaid

    F1 -- "Divide by R t" --> H1["h t = Hazard Rate"]
    H1 -- "Multiply by R t" --> F1
    H1 -- "Integral exponent form" --> R1

```

   R(t) and f(t) vs Time

    |  *                              f(t) = Failure Density
    |   *  . . . . . . . . . .

- $R(t)$ **monotonically decreases** from 1 toward 0.
- $f(t)$ typically rises, peaks, then falls (unimodal, non-negative).
