<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define failure density function. How is it related to reliability function?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **failure density function** $f(t)$ is defined as the probability density of the time-to-failure random variable $T$:

$$f(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t \le T \le t+\Delta t)}{\Delta t}$$

**Relation to Reliability:** Since the unreliability function is the CDF, $F(t) = \displaystyle\int_0^t f(x)\,dx$, and $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$, differentiating gives:

$$f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

Equivalently, $R(t) = \displaystyle\int_t^\infty f(x)\,dx$.

**[1 mark]**: definition of $f(t)$
**[1 mark]**: relation $f(t) = -dR(t)/dt$
**[1 mark]**: integral form $R(t) = \int_t^\infty f(x)\,dx$

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is hazard rate? Establish the relation $h(t) = f(t)/R(t)$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **hazard rate** $h(t)$ is the conditional probability density that a component fails at time $t$, given that it has survived up to time $t$:

$$h(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t \le T \le t+\Delta t \mid T > t)}{\Delta t}$$

Applying the conditional probability formula $P(A \mid B) = P(A \cap B)/P(B)$:

$$h(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0}\frac{1}{\Delta t} \cdot \frac{P(t \le T \le t+\Delta t)}{P(T > t)} = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

Therefore, $\boxed{h(t) = f(t)/R(t)}$.

**[1 mark]**: definition of $h(t)$
**[1 mark]**: conditional probability expansion
**[1 mark]**: final boxed result

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Module 1 Choice Pattern]`

**(a)** Derive the relation between reliability $R(t)$, failure density $f(t)$, and hazard rate $h(t)$ in the form $R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\displaystyle\int_0^t h(x)\,dx\right)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The time-to-failure of a certain electronic component follows the Weibull distribution with $h(t) = 0.002\,t^{1.5}$ (per hour). Determine: (i) the reliability at $t = 100$ hours, (ii) the failure density at $t = 100$ hours, and (iii) the mean time to failure (MTTF). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for **Question A (a)** (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Start from the defining relation between hazard rate and failure density:

$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$:

$$h(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 3:** Cross-multiply to separate variables:

$$h(t)\,dt = -\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)} = -d\bigl[\ln R(t)\bigr]$$

**Step 4:** Integrate both sides from $0$ to $t$. On the left, integrate $h(x)$ with respect to $x$. On the right:

$$\int_0^t h(x)\,dx = -\int_0^t d\bigl[\ln R(x)\bigr] = -\ln R(t) + \ln R(0)$$

**Step 5:** Apply the initial condition. At $t = 0$, the component is brand new, so $R(0) = 1$, hence $\ln R(0) = 0$:

$$\int_0^t h(x)\,dx = -\ln R(t)$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $R(t)$ by exponentiating:

$$R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_0^t h(x)\,dx\right) \qquad \blacksquare$$

**Mark Distribution:**
- [2 marks] Correct starting equation $h(t) = f(t)/R(t)$ and substitution
- [2 marks] Variable separation and differential form
- [2 marks] Integration with limits and use of $R(0) = 1$
- [1 mark] Final exponentiated form

---

#### Model Solution for **Question A (b)** (7 marks)

**Given:** $h(t) = 0.002\,t^{1.5}$ per hour, Weibull form with $\alpha = 0.002$, $\beta = 2.5$.

**(i) Reliability at $t = 100$ hours** **(2 marks):**

$$R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_0^t 0.002\,x^{1.5}\,dx\right)$$

$$\int_0^{100} 0.002\,x^{1.5}\,dx = 0.002 \cdot \left[\frac{x^{2.5}}{2.5}\right]_0^{100}$$

$$= 0.002 \cdot \frac{100^{2.5}}{2.5} = 0.002 \cdot \frac{100000}{2.5} = 0.002 \cdot 40000 = 80$$

Therefore:

$$R(100) = e^{-80} \approx 0$$

**Interpretation:** $R(100) \approx 0$ — the component has effectively zero probability of surviving 100 hours (this is unrealistic; indicates the Weibull parameters represent severe wear-out).

**(ii) Failure density at $t = 100$ hours** **(2 marks):**

$$f(t) = h(t) \cdot R(t)$$

At $t = 100$:
$$h(100) = 0.002 \cdot 100^{1.5} = 0.002 \cdot 1000 = 2 \text{ per hour}$$

$$f(100) = 2 \cdot e^{-80} \approx 0$$

**(iii) Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** **(3 marks):**

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_0^\infty R(t)\,dt = \int_0^\infty e^{-0.002\,t^{2.5}}\,dt$$

Let $u = 0.002\,t^{2.5}$, so $du = 0.002 \cdot 2.5 \cdot t^{1.5}\,dt = 0.005\,t^{1.5}\,dt$, giving $dt = \dfrac{du}{0.005\,t^{1.5}} = \dfrac{du}{0.005\,(u/0.002)^{1.5/2.5}} = \dfrac{du}{0.005\,(u/0.002)^{0.6}}$.

This is a standard Weibull integral that evaluates using the Gamma function $\Gamma(s) = \int_0^\infty u^{s-1}e^{-u}\,du$:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_0^\infty e^{-0.002\,t^{2.5}}\,dt = \frac{1}{2.5\,(0.002)^{1/2.5}} \cdot \Gamma\!\left(\frac{1}{2.5}\right)$$

Computing numerically:
- $1/2.5 = 0.4$
- $(0.002)^{0.4} \approx 0.0959$
- $\Gamma(0.4) \approx 2.218$

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{2.5 \cdot 0.0959} \cdot 2.218 \approx \frac{2.218}{0.2398} \approx 9.25 \text{ hours}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't confuse $R(t)$ at $t = 0$:** Some students incorrectly write $R(0) = 0$; correct value is $R(0) = 1$.
> 2. **Sign errors in $f(t) = -dR(t)/dt$:** Forgetting the negative sign leads to wrong $f(t)$ values.
> 3. **Units of hazard rate:** For $h(t) = \lambda t^{\beta - 1}$, units are "per unit time" — never omit this in answers.
> 4. **Incomplete derivations:** In Part (a) derivations, examiners expect the *use of $R(0) = 1$* explicitly. Skipping this loses 1 full mark.
> 5. **Conditional vs unconditional:** $h(t) \ne f(t)$. They are equal only when $R(t) = 1$ (instantaneously at $t = 0$).
> 6. **MTTF integration:** Direct numerical integration is acceptable in part (iii) if the closed-form is too involved; mark is awarded for setting up the integral correctly.

---

### **Question B** (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Module 1 Choice Pattern]`

**(a)** Define $f(t)$, $F(t)$, $R(t)$, and $h(t)$. With the help of a block diagram, explain the mathematical relationship between them. Derive the four fundamental identities. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a system, the time-to-failure has the failure density function $f(t) = 2t\,e^{-t^2}$ for $t \ge 0$. Compute (i) the reliability function $R(t)$, (ii) the hazard rate $h(t)$, and (iii) the probability that the system survives beyond 1 hour. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for **Question B (a)** (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Definitions** (2 marks):
- $f(t) = \dfrac{dF(t)}{dt}$ — failure density
- $F(t) = P(T \le t)$ — unreliability (CDF)
- $R(t) = P(T > t) = 1 - F(t)$ — reliability
- $h(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ — hazard rate

**Step 2 — Block diagram** (1 mark): See SECTION 4.3 above for the Mermaid mapping diagram.

**Step 3 — Four fundamental identities** (4 marks):

**Identity 1:** $R(t) + F(t) = 1$

*Proof:* A unit either fails by $t$ or survives beyond $t$ — mutually exclusive, exhaustive events.

**Identity 2:** $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$

*Proof:* Differentiate $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$ with respect to $t$, using $f(t) = dF/dt$.

**Identity 3:** $h(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$

*Proof:* Apply conditional probability formula to $P(t \le T \le t+\Delta t \mid T > t)$.

**Identity 4:** $R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\displaystyle\int_0^t h(x)\,dx\right)$

*Proof:* Substitute $f(t) = -dR/dt$ into Identity 3, separate variables, integrate using $R(0) = 1$.

---

#### Model Solution for **Question B (b)** (7 marks)

**Given:** $f(t) = 2t\,e^{-t^2}$ for $t \ge 0$.

**(i) Reliability function** (2 marks):

$$R(t) = \int_t^\infty f(x)\,dx = \int_t^\infty 2x\,e^{-x^2}\,dx$$

Let $u = x^2$, $du = 2x\,dx$:

$$R(t) = \int_{t^2}^\infty e^{-u}\,du = \bigl[-e^{-u}\bigr]_{t^2}^\infty = 0 - (-e^{-t^2}) = e^{-t^2}$$

Therefore, $\boxed{R(t) = e^{-t^2}}$.

**(ii) Hazard rate** (2 marks):

$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{2t\,e^{-t^2}}{e^{-t^2}} = 2t$$

Therefore, $\boxed{h(t) = 2t}$ (linearly increasing — typical of wear-out region).

**(iii) Probability that the system survives beyond 1 hour** (3 marks):

$$P(T > 1) = R(1) = e^{-1^2} = e^{-1} \approx 0.3679$$

Therefore, the probability is approximately **36.79%**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistake in Part (b):**
> Students often compute $F(t)$ first using $\int_0^t f(x)\,dx = 1 - e^{-t^2}$, then write $R(t) = 1 - F(t) = 1 - (1 - e^{-t^2}) = e^{-t^2}$. **Both methods are correct** — the direct integral from $t$ to $\infty$ is faster.
> In part (iii), some students confuse $P(T > 1)$ with $P(T < 1)$ and write $1 - e^{-1}$ instead of $e^{-1}$. This is the **single most common error** in reliability problems — always read carefully whether the question asks for survival or failure probability.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Reliability $R(t)$** is the probability of survival beyond $t$, with $R(0) = 1$ and $R(\infty) = 0$.
- **Unreliability $F(t)$** is the CDF of time-to-failure, with $F(0) = 0$ and $F(\infty) = 1$.
- **Failure density $f(t)$** is the PDF of $T$, non-negative, integrates to 1 over $[0, \infty)$.
- **Hazard rate $h(t)$** is *conditional* on survival, always non-negative for non-negative $T$.
- **The Master Equation:** $h(t) = f(t)/R(t)$ — know this cold; it links all three.
- **Neg-derivative identity:** $f(t) = -dR(t)/dt$ — fastest way to find $f(t)$ given $R(t)$.
- **Integral identity:** $R(t) = \int_t^\infty f(x)\,dx$ — used when $f(t)$ is given.
- **Exponent identity:** $R(t) = \exp(-\int_0^t h(x)\,dx)$ — used when $h(t)$ is given.
- **Complement rule:** $R(t) + F(t) = 1$ — always.
- **MTTF for exponential:** $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$ when $h(t) = \lambda$.
- **MTTF general:** $\text{MTTF} = \int_0^\infty R(t)\,dt$ — keep this in mind for non-exponential cases.
- **Bathtub curve regions:** Infant mortality (decreasing $h$), useful life (constant $h$), wear-out (increasing $h$).
- **Weibull is the general form:** Exponential is a special case of Weibull with $\beta = 1$.
- **KTU typical 7-mark derivation question:** "Show that $R(t) = \exp(-\int_0^t h(x)\,dx)$" — memorize the 6-step derivation.
- **KTU typical 3-mark definition question:** $f(t)$, $R(t)$, $h(t)$ — define + state one key relation.
- **Units to remember:** $h(t)$ is in failures per unit time; $R(t)$, $F(t)$, $f(t)\cdot dt$ are dimensionless probabilities.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mean Time to Failure (MTTF)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT754 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mean Time to Failure (MTTF)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** is the arithmetic mean (expected value) of the time-to-failure random variable for a **non-repairable** component or system, measured from the instant the unit is placed in service until the instant of its first (and only, since it is non-repairable) failure.

In the formal probabilistic language adopted by KTU 2024 Scheme:

$$\text{MTTF} \;=\; E[T] \;=\; \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\, dt$$

where:
- $T$ is the continuous non-negative random variable denoting the **time-to-failure**
- $f(t)$ is the **probability density function (pdf)** of $T$
- The integral is taken from $t=0$ to $t \rightarrow \infty$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme – Syllabus Highlight:**
> MTTF is **strictly applicable to non-repairable items** (e.g., light bulbs, electrolytic capacitors, integrated circuits, fuses, resistors). For repairable systems, the analogous metric is **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** and is covered separately in subsequent modules. Confusing these two is one of the most common valuation mistakes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Candle" Model

Imagine you manufacture **1,000 identical birthday candles** and light all of them simultaneously. You record the exact time each candle burns out completely. Some burn fast (poor wick), some burn slow (thick wax). After sorting, the **average burn time** of all 1,000 candles is the **MTTF** of the candle population.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight from the Analogy**
> 1. MTTF is a **population average**, not a guarantee for any single unit.
> 2. It quantifies *how long, on average*, the component survives under stated operating conditions (stress, temperature, load, duty cycle).
> 3. The **candle cannot be "repaired"** once it burns out — this is why MTTF is used (and not MTBF).
> 4. A high MTTF indicates a **reliable design**; a low MTTF indicates a design prone to early failure.

## 1.3 Engineering Significance

- **Reliability Prediction:** MTTF is a *single-number summary* of a component's lifetime distribution and is used heavily in **MIL-HDBK-217**, **Telcordia SR-332**, and **NSWC-98/LE1** reliability prediction handbooks.
- **Design Trade-off:** Higher MTTF typically demands tighter manufacturing tolerances, derating, and better materials — all of which increase cost.
- **Warranty Estimation:** Manufacturers often quote a *fraction* of MTTF (e.g., B₁₀ life of bearings) for warranty periods.
- **Mission Reliability:** For missions of short duration relative to MTTF, the exponential approximation $R(t) \approx e^{-t/\text{MTTF}}$ is widely used.

> [!TIP]
> **Physical Units:** MTTF is expressed in **hours (h)**, **cycles**, **kilometers (km)**, or any other monotonically increasing usage unit. In semiconductor reliability, **FITs (Failures In Time = 1 failure per 10⁹ device-hours)** are often used. The conversion is:
> $$\text{MTTF (hours)} = \dfrac{10^{9}}{\text{FIT}}$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation – Why MTTF Matters

The MTTF captures the **central tendency** of the failure distribution. The reliability engineer must understand MTTF in conjunction with three other pillars:

| # | Reliability Function | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|----------------------|--------|---------|
| 1 | Cumulative Distribution Function (Failure Probability) | $F(t)$ | $P(T \le t)$ |
| 2 | Reliability (Survival) Function | $R(t)$ | $P(T > t) = 1 - F(t)$ |
| 3 | Probability Density Function | $f(t)$ | $\dfrac{dF(t)}{dt} = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$ |
| 4 | Instantaneous Failure (Hazard) Rate | $\lambda(t)$ | $\dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ |

The fundamental relationships are:

$$R(t) + F(t) = 1 \quad \text{(Exhaustive & Mutually Exclusive Events)}$$

$$f(t) = \dfrac{dF(t)}{dt} = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

$$\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\dfrac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

## 2.2 Three Equivalent Mathematical Formulations of MTTF

**Formulation 1 – Direct Definition (from pdf):**
$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\, dt$$

**Formulation 2 – In Terms of Reliability Function (the most useful for KTU problems):**
$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt$$

**Formulation 3 – In Terms of Hazard Rate:**
$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \exp\!\left( -\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(u)\, du \right) dt$$

## 2.3 The High-Yield KTU Formula Sheet

The table below is **examination-critical**. Memorize the rightmost column; it appears in nearly every KTU Module 1 question paper.

| # | Distribution | $R(t)$ | MTTF | MTTF Expression (Compact) | Variance $\sigma^{2}$ |
|---|--------------|--------|------|----------------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Exponential** | $e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | $\dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | $\dfrac{1}{\lambda^{2}}$ |
| 2 | **Weibull (2-param)** | $e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | $\eta \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1+\dfrac{1}{\beta}\right)$ | $\dfrac{\eta}{\beta}\cdot \Gamma\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\beta}\right)$ | $\eta^{2}\!\left[\Gamma\!\left(1+\dfrac{2}{\beta}\right) - \Gamma^{2}\!\left(1+\dfrac{1}{\beta}\right)\right]$ |
| 3 | **Rayleigh** (Weibull with $\beta=2$) | $e^{-(t/\eta)^{2}}$ | $\eta \dfrac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}$ | $\eta \cdot 0.8862$ | $\eta^{2}\!\left(2 - \dfrac{\pi}{4}\right)$ |
| 4 | **Normal** | $1 - \Phi\!\left(\dfrac{t-\mu}{\sigma}\right)$ | $\mu$ | $\mu$ | $\sigma^{2}$ |
| 5 | **Lognormal** | $1 - \Phi\!\left(\dfrac{\ln t - \mu'}{\sigma'}\right)$ | $e^{\mu' + \sigma'^{2}/2}$ | — | $[e^{\sigma'^{2}} - 1]\,e^{2\mu' + \sigma'^{2}}$ |
| 6 | **Gamma** | $1 - \Gamma_{\text{inc}}(\alpha, \lambda t)$ | $\dfrac{\alpha}{\lambda}$ | $\dfrac{\alpha}{\lambda}$ | $\dfrac{\alpha}{\lambda^{2}}$ |
| 7 | **Uniform** $[0, b]$ | $1 - \dfrac{t}{b}$ | $\dfrac{b}{2}$ | $\dfrac{b}{2}$ | $\dfrac{b^{2}}{12}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the Exponential case so important?**
> For an exponential distribution, MTTF **equals the standard deviation** ($\sigma = 1/\lambda$). This is a *unique* property — no other common lifetime distribution has this symmetry. KTU examiners often exploit this in numerical problems to test the student's understanding of the **memoryless property**.

## 2.4 Why the "Area-Under-Reliability-Curve" Formulation Works

The reliability function $R(t)$ is a monotonically non-increasing function that starts at $R(0)=1$ and decays to $R(\infty)=0$. The **area under this curve** — physically, the integral of *survival probability* over the entire time axis — represents the **expected lifetime**. This is mathematically proven in Section 3.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | MTTF Application | Typical Target |
|----------|------------------|----------------|
| Aerospace Avionics | Mission-critical ICs | > 100,000 h (≈ 11.4 yr) |
| Automotive ECUs | 10-year vehicle life | 87,600 h (10 yr continuous) |
| Consumer Smartphones | Capacitors, SoCs | 25,000 – 50,000 h |
| Industrial Bearings (L₁₀) | 90% survival life | Application-specific |
| Power Grid Transformers | 30–40 year design | 250,000 – 350,000 h |
| Nuclear Reactor Components | Plant lifetime | 1,000,000+ h |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Numerical Trick:** Whenever a problem gives you $R(t)$ directly (not the parameters of a distribution), always evaluate MTTF using the integral $\int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\,dt$. This is faster than the pdf method and bypasses the need to differentiate $R(t)$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — MTTF from First Principles (MTTF = Area Under R(t))

**Goal:** Prove $\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt$ starting from $\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t\, f(t)\, dt$.

**Step 1 – Start with the definition of expected value of $T$:**

$$\text{MTTF} = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\, dt$$

**Step 2 – Use the fundamental relationship $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \left( -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt} \right) dt = -\int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot dR(t)$$

**Step 3 – Apply Integration by Parts.** Choose:

- $u = t \quad \Rightarrow \quad du = dt$
- $dv = -dR(t) \quad \Rightarrow \quad v = -R(t)$

**Step 4 – Substitute into the standard integration-by-parts formula $\int u\, dv = uv - \int v\, du$:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \Big[\, t \cdot \big( -R(t) \big) \,\Big]_{0}^{\infty} - \int_{0}^{\infty} \big( -R(t) \big) \cdot dt$$

**Step 5 – Simplify the boundary term:**

$$\Big[\, t \cdot \big( -R(t) \big) \,\Big]_{0}^{\infty} = -\lim_{t \to \infty} t \cdot R(t) \;+\; 0 \cdot R(0)$$

**Step 6 – Evaluate $R(0)$:** At time zero, the unit is brand-new and has not failed, so $R(0) = 1$. Hence the second term is $0 \cdot 1 = 0$.

**Step 7 – Evaluate the limit at infinity:** For any physical component with *finite* MTTF, the reliability decays faster than $1/t$. That is:

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} t \cdot R(t) = 0$$

(This is the L'Hôpital result: if $E[T]$ is finite, then $R(t) \to 0$ faster than $1/t \to 0$, so the product is 0.)

**Step 8 – Assemble the result:**

$$\text{MTTF} = 0 - 0 + \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt \qquad \blacksquare$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — MTTF for the Exponential Distribution

**Given:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}, \; t \ge 0$.

**Step 1 – Write the integral:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\, dt$$

**Step 2 – Apply the standard exponential integral formula $\int e^{at}\,dt = \dfrac{e^{at}}{a}$:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \left[\, \dfrac{e^{-\lambda t}}{-\lambda} \,\right]_{0}^{\infty} = -\dfrac{1}{\lambda} \left[\, e^{-\lambda t} \,\right]_{0}^{\infty}$$

**Step 3 – Evaluate the limits:**

- At $t = \infty$: $e^{-\lambda \cdot \infty} = 0$
- At $t = 0$: $e^{-\lambda \cdot 0} = e^{0} = 1$

**Step 4 – Substitute:**

$$\text{MTTF} = -\dfrac{1}{\lambda} \big( 0 - 1 \big) = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} \qquad \blacksquare$$

**Equivalent Cross-Check via the pdf:**
$f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$. Then:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t}\, dt = \lambda \cdot \dfrac{1}{\lambda^{2}} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} \qquad \text{(using } \int_{0}^{\infty} t e^{-at} dt = 1/a^{2}\text{)} \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — MTTF for the Weibull Distribution

**Given:** $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}, \; t \ge 0, \; \beta > 0, \; \eta > 0$.

**Step 1 – Set up the integral:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}\, dt$$

**Step 2 – Substitute $u = \left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}$.** This implies:

$$t = \eta \cdot u^{1/\beta}$$

Differentiate with respect to $u$:

$$dt = \eta \cdot \dfrac{1}{\beta} \cdot u^{(1/\beta) - 1}\, du = \dfrac{\eta}{\beta}\, u^{(1/\beta) - 1}\, du$$

**Step 3 – Transform the integration limits:**

- When $t = 0$, $u = 0^{\beta} = 0$
- When $t = \infty$, $u = \infty^{\beta} = \infty$

**Step 4 – Substitute into the integral:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-u} \cdot \dfrac{\eta}{\beta}\, u^{(1/\beta) - 1}\, du$$

**Step 5 – Pull the constants out and recognize the Gamma function $\Gamma(s) = \int_{0}^{\infty} u^{s-1} e^{-u}\, du$ with $s = 1/\beta$:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{\eta}{\beta} \int_{0}^{\infty} u^{(1/\beta) - 1}\, e^{-u}\, du = \dfrac{\eta}{\beta}\, \Gamma\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\beta}\right)$$

**Step 6 – Apply the identity $\Gamma(1+x) = x \cdot \Gamma(x)$ to obtain the more compact form:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \eta \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\beta}\right) \qquad \blacksquare$$

**Special Case – Rayleigh Distribution ($\beta = 2$):**

$$\text{MTTF} = \eta \cdot \Gamma(1.5) = \eta \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} \approx 0.8862\,\eta$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Exponential MTTF

**Problem:** A non-repairable electronic sensor has a constant failure rate $\lambda = 2 \times 10^{-4}$ failures per hour. Compute (a) MTTF, (b) the reliability at $t = 2000$ h, and (c) the probability of failure in the interval $(1000, 3000)$ h.

**Solution:**

**(a) MTTF:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} = \dfrac{1}{2 \times 10^{-4}} = 5000 \text{ hours}$$

**(b) Reliability at $t = 2000$ h:**

$$R(2000) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(2 \times 10^{-4})(2000)} = e^{-0.4} = 0.6703$$

**(c) Probability of failure in $(1000, 3000)$ h:**

This is $P(1000 < T \le 3000) = F(3000) - F(1000) = R(1000) - R(3000)$.

$$R(1000) = e^{-(2 \times 10^{-4})(1000)} = e^{-0.2} = 0.8187$$

$$R(3000) = e^{-(2 \times 10^{-4})(3000)} = e^{-0.6} = 0.5488$$

$$P(1000 < T \le 3000) = 0.8187 - 0.5488 = 0.2699 \quad \text{(≈ 26.99%)}$$

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Weibull MTTF

**Problem:** A mechanical bearing has a Weibull lifetime with shape parameter $\beta = 1.8$ and scale parameter $\eta = 8000$ h. Compute MTTF and the B₁₀ life (the time at which 10% of bearings have failed).

**Solution:**

**(a) MTTF:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \eta \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\beta}\right) = 8000 \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{1.8}\right) = 8000 \cdot \Gamma(1.5556)$$

From standard Gamma tables, $\Gamma(1.5556) \approx 0.8896$.

$$\text{MTTF} \approx 8000 \times 0.8896 = 7117 \text{ hours}$$

**(b) B₁₀ Life:** Solve $F(t_{B_{10}}) = 0.10 \Rightarrow R(t_{B_{10}}) = 0.90$.

$$e^{-(t_{B_{10}} / 8000)^{1.8}} = 0.90$$

Take the natural log of both sides:

$$-(t_{B_{10}} / 8000)^{1.8} = \ln(0.90) = -0.10536$$

$$(t_{B_{10}} / 8000)^{1.8} = 0.10536$$

$$t_{B_{10}} / 8000 = (0.10536)^{1/1.8} = (0.10536)^{0.5556}$$

$$(0.10536)^{0.5556} \approx 0.2988$$

$$t_{B_{10}} = 8000 \times 0.2988 = 2390 \text{ hours}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The bearing is expected to last (on average) **7117 h**, but 10% of bearings will fail before **2390 h**. Warranty periods are typically set to the **B₁₀** life, not the MTTF, to limit replacement costs.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison with MTBF & MTTR

| Metric | Definition | Used For | Formula | Units |
|--------|------------|----------|---------|-------|
| **MTTF** | Mean time to first failure (non-repairable) | Light bulbs, ICs, capacitors | $\int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt$ | hours |
| **MTTR** | Mean time to restore (repair duration) | Industrial systems, aircraft | $\int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot g(t)\, dt$ where $g(t)$ is the repair-time pdf | hours |
| **MTBF** | Mean time between consecutive failures (repairable) | Aircraft engines, CNC machines | $\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$ | hours |
| **Availability** | Fraction of time system is operational | Repairable systems | $\dfrac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}} = \dfrac{\text{MTBF} - \text{MTTR}}{\text{MTBF}}$ | dimensionless (0–1) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Computational Flow: From Failure Data to MTTF

This flowchart shows the **algorithmic logic** the reliability engineer follows to convert raw failure data into an MTTF estimate.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Failure Data Available] --> B{Choose the Source Function}
    B --|Have pdf f(t)|--> C[Apply Definition: MTTF = integral 0 to infinity of t * f t dt]
    B --|Have R(t) directly|--> D[Apply Integral of R t: MTTF = integral 0 to infinity of R t dt]
    B --|Have hazard rate lambda t|--> E[Compute R t = exp minus integral 0 to t of lambda u du]
    C --> F{Identify the Distribution}
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --|Exponential|--> G1[Use 1 divided by lambda]
    F --|Weibull|--> G2[Use eta times Gamma 1 plus 1 over beta]
    F --|Rayleigh beta equal 2|--> G3[Use eta times square root of pi over 2]
    F --|Normal|--> G4[Use mean mu]
    F --|Lognormal|--> G5[Use exp of mu prime plus sigma prime squared over 2]
    G1 --> H[Cross Check: Compare two methods]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    G5 --> H
    H --> I[Report MTTF with Units and Confidence Bounds]
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Functional Block Architecture of Reliability Analysis

This block diagram situates MTTF within the larger **reliability engineering ecosystem**, showing how MTTF is one of three core outputs derived from the same failure data.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[System or Component] --> A1[Stress and Environment Inputs]
    A1 --> A2[Failure Data Collection]
    A2 --> A3[Distribution Fitting]
    A3 --> A4[Reliability Function R t]
    A3 --> A5[Failure Density f t]
    A3 --> A6[Hazard Rate lambda t]
    A4 --> B1[MTTF Calculation]
    A5 --> B1
    A6 --> B1
    A4 --> B2[MTBF Calculation for Repairable]
    A4 --> B3[Availability Index]
    A4 --> B4[Reliability at Mission Time]
    B1 --> C1[Design Trade off Studies]
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C1
    C1 --> C2[Final Reliability Report and Decisions]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Time-Line Comparison: MTTF vs MTBF vs MTTR

A visual representation of how the three metrics relate in a **repairable system** with three successive failure events.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Start of Service t equals 0] --> A1
    A1[Operate for T1 hours] --> F1[Failure 1]
    F1 --> R1[Repair for D1 hours]
    R1 --> A2[Operate for T2 hours]
    A2 --> F2[Failure 2]
    F2 --> R2[Repair for D2 hours]
    R2 --> A3[Operate for T3 hours]
    A3 --> F3[Failure 3]
    F3 --> R3[Repair for D3 hours]
    A1 -.-> M1[MTTF equals average of T1 T2 T3]
    R1 -.-> M2[MTTR equals average of D1 D2 D3]
    A1 -.-> M3[MTBF equals MTTF plus MTTR equals average of T plus D cycles]
    R1 -.-> M3
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. Define Mean Time To Failure (MTTF). How is it mathematically expressed in terms of the reliability function $R(t)$? **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PEBRT754, Module 1]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
The **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** of a non-repairable component is the expected value (arithmetic mean) of its time-to-failure random variable $T$. It represents the average lifetime of a population of identical components operating under stated conditions.

**Mathematical Expression (1.5 marks):**
In terms of the reliability function $R(t)$:

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt}$$

Equivalently, in terms of the probability density function $f(t)$:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\, dt$$

For a system following the **exponential distribution** with constant failure rate $\lambda$, this reduces to the simple form $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$.

---

### Q2. The failure rate of a non-repairable component is constant at $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ per hour. Find the MTTF. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEBRT754]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** For a constant failure rate, the underlying distribution is the **exponential distribution**:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}, \quad f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Apply the MTTF formula:

$$\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} = \dfrac{1}{5 \times 10^{-5}} = 20{,}000 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Convert to more interpretable units for context:

$$20{,}000 \text{ hours} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ year}}{8760 \text{ hours}} \approx 2.28 \text{ years of continuous operation}$$

**Answer:** **MTTF = 20,000 hours ≈ 2.28 years.**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations**, Module 1 contributes two 14-mark questions. You are required to **answer ONE full question** (with all sub-parts) or you may choose between **Question A** and **Question B** below as the internal-choice pair.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PEBRT754, Module 1]*

**(a)** Define MTTF and derive its expression in terms of the reliability function $R(t)$ starting from the fundamental definition $\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\, dt$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The reliability of a non-repairable mechanical assembly is described by $R(t) = e^{-0.0008\,t}$ for $t \ge 0$. Determine:
- (i) The MTTF
- (ii) The reliability at $t = 500$ hours
- (iii) The probability of failure in the interval $(200, 1000)$ hours
- (iv) The standard deviation of the time to failure

**(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**(1 mark)** **Definition:** MTTF is the expected value of the time-to-failure random variable $T$ for a non-repairable component.

**(6 marks) Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Starting point:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\, dt$$

Substitute $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$:

$$\text{MTTF} = -\int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot dR(t)$$

Apply integration by parts with $u = t$ and $dv = -dR(t)$, giving $du = dt$ and $v = -R(t)$:

$$\text{MTTF} = \big[\, t \cdot (-R(t)) \,\big]_{0}^{\infty} - \int_{0}^{\infty} (-R(t))\, dt$$

Simplify:

$$\text{MTTF} = -\lim_{t \to \infty} t \cdot R(t) + 0 \cdot R(0) + \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt$$

Using $R(0) = 1$ and assuming $\lim_{t \to \infty} t R(t) = 0$ for finite MTTF:

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt} \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Comparing $R(t) = e^{-0.0008\,t}$ with the standard exponential form $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, we identify $\lambda = 0.0008$ per hour.

**(i) MTTF (2 marks):**

$$\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} = \dfrac{1}{0.0008} = 1250 \text{ hours}$$

**(ii) Reliability at $t = 500$ h (2 marks):**

$$R(500) = e^{-(0.0008)(500)} = e^{-0.4} = 0.6703$$

**(iii) Probability of failure in $(200, 1000)$ h (2 marks):**

$$P(200 < T \le 1000) = R(200) - R(1000) = e^{-0.16} - e^{-0.80}$$

$$= 0.8521 - 0.4493 = 0.4028 \; \text{(≈ 40.28%)}$$

**(iv) Standard deviation (1 mark):**

For exponential distribution, $\sigma = 1/\lambda = 1250$ h.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PEBRT754, Module 1]*

**(a)** State the Weibull distribution and derive the general expression for MTTF of a component whose lifetime follows a 2-parameter Weibull distribution. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A non-repairable electric motor follows a Weibull lifetime with shape parameter $\beta = 2.5$ and scale parameter $\eta = 6000$ hours. Determine:
- (i) The MTTF of the motor
- (ii) The median life $t_{50}$ (time at which 50% of motors have failed)
- (iii) The design life $t_{R}$ such that $R(t_{R}) = 0.95$

**(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**(1 mark)** **Statement:** The 2-parameter Weibull distribution has CDF $F(t) = 1 - e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ and reliability $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ for $t \ge 0$, where $\eta > 0$ is the *scale parameter* (characteristic life) and $\beta > 0$ is the *shape parameter* (slope of hazard rate).

**(6 marks) Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}\, dt$$

Substitute $u = (t/\eta)^{\beta}$ so that $t = \eta\, u^{1/\beta}$ and $dt = (\eta/\beta) u^{(1/\beta) - 1}\, du$:

$$\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{\eta}{\beta} \int_{0}^{\infty} u^{(1/\beta) - 1}\, e^{-u}\, du = \dfrac{\eta}{\beta}\, \Gamma\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\beta}\right)$$

Apply the identity $\Gamma(1 + x) = x \cdot \Gamma(x)$ with $x = 1/\beta$:

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF} = \eta \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\beta}\right)} \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $\beta = 2.5$, $\eta = 6000$ h.

**(i) MTTF (2 marks):**

$$\text{MTTF} = 6000 \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{2.5}\right) = 6000 \cdot \Gamma(1.4)$$

From Gamma tables: $\Gamma(1.4) \approx 0.8873$.

$$\text{MTTF} = 6000 \times 0.8873 = 5323.8 \text{ hours}$$

**(ii) Median life $t_{50}$ (3 marks):** Solve $R(t_{50}) = 0.5$:

$$e^{-(t_{50}/6000)^{2.5}} = 0.5$$

Take natural log:

$$-(t_{50}/6000)^{2.5} = \ln(0.5) = -0.6931$$

$$(t_{50}/6000)^{2.5} = 0.6931$$

$$t_{50}/6000 = (0.6931)^{1/2.5} = (0.6931)^{0.4} \approx 0.8663$$

$$t_{50} \approx 6000 \times 0.8663 = 5197.7 \text{ hours}$$

**(iii) Design life for 95% reliability (2 marks):** Solve $R(t_{R}) = 0.95$:

$$e^{-(t_{R}/6000)^{2.5}} = 0.95 \Rightarrow (t_{R}/6000)^{2.5} = -\ln(0.95) = 0.05129$$

$$t_{R}/6000 = (0.05129)^{0.4} \approx 0.3094$$

$$t_{R} \approx 6000 \times 0.3094 = 1856.5 \text{ hours}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls for MTTF Problems:**
> 1. **Confusing MTTF with MTBF (–2 to –3 marks):** Students frequently write "MTBF" when the question specifies a *non-repairable* system. Always check the wording: *non-repairable → MTTF; repairable → MTBF*.
> 2. **Forgetting $R(0) = 1$ in the boundary term (–1 mark):** The integration-by-parts derivation often loses the $0 \cdot R(0) = 0$ step. Write it explicitly.
> 3. **Wrong Gamma function argument (–2 marks):** For Weibull MTTF, it is $\Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$, **not** $\Gamma(1/\beta)$. The factor of 1 inside is critical.
> 4. **Mixing up $\lambda$ and $1/\lambda$ for exponential (–1 mark):** MTTF = $1/\lambda$, **not** $\lambda$. If $\lambda$ is small, MTTF is large.
> 5. **Not stating units (–0.5 mark):** Always write "hours" or "cycles" after a numerical MTTF value.
> 6. **Using $\ln$ instead of $\log_{10}$ or vice versa (–1 mark):** Be consistent. Weibull/Exponential integrals use $\ln$ (natural log).
> 7. **Exponential assumption without justification (–1 mark):** If a problem gives $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, *state explicitly* that the distribution is exponential and $\lambda$ is the constant failure rate.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this checklist before walking into the KTU exam hall.**

- [x] **MTTF applies ONLY to non-repairable systems.** For repairable systems, use MTBF.
- [x] **Three equivalent MTTF formulas:**
  (i) $\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\, dt$
  (ii) $\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt$ ← *Use this in KTU problems; it's the fastest.*
  (iii) $\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \exp\!\big(-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(u)\, du\big) dt$
- [x] **Exponential shortcut:** MTTF = $1/\lambda$ and standard deviation $\sigma = 1/\lambda$ (MTTF = $\sigma$ for exponential).
- [x] **Weibull shortcut:** MTTF = $\eta \cdot \Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$.
- [x] **Rayleigh ($\beta = 2$):** MTTF = $\eta \sqrt{\pi}/2 \approx 0.8862\,\eta$.
- [x] **MTTF is a *mean*, not a *guarantee*.** A component can fail at $t \ll \text{MTTF}$.
- [x] **Units:** Hours, cycles, km, or FIT (with conversion $1\,\text{FIT} = 10^{-9}$ failures/hour, so $\text{MTTF (h)} = 10^{9}/\text{FIT}$).
- [x] **B₁₀ life ≠ MTTF.** B₁₀ is the 10th percentile (10% have failed); MTTF is the 50th-percentile-like *mean*.
- [x] **MTBF = MTTF + MTTR** for repairable systems; **Availability = MTTF / (MTTF + MTTR)**.
- [x] **Memoryless property** of the exponential: $P(T > s + t \,\vert\, T > s) = P(T > t)$ — unique to exponential.
- [x] **Always state units, the underlying distribution, and any assumptions** (constant $\lambda$, independence, operating environment) in your answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Integral Equation of MTTF in Terms of Reliability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT754 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Integral Equation of MTTF in Terms of Reliability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 🎯 Formal Academic Definition

The **Integral Equation of MTTF in Terms of Reliability** is a fundamental identity in reliability engineering that expresses the **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** of a non-repairable component or system as the area under its **Reliability function R(t)** curve, evaluated from time zero to infinity.

Mathematically, it is stated as:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t) \, dt$$

where:
- $f(t)$ is the **probability density function (PDF) of failure**
- $R(t)$ is the **reliability function** (probability of survival up to time $t$)
- $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$ is the **cumulative distribution function (CDF) of failure**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This identity is universally valid for **non-repairable systems** and provides an elegant computational shortcut because $R(t)$ is often easier to determine experimentally (via survival data) than the raw failure density $f(t)$.

## 💡 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a group of **1000 light bulbs** switched on simultaneously at $t = 0$ in a factory test lab. As time progresses, bulbs fail one by one.

- The **Reliability $R(t)$** at any moment tells you: *"Out of the original 1000, how many are still glowing?"*
- The **MTTF** tells you: *"On average, how long did each bulb last before burning out?"*

Now, the integral $\int_{0}^{\infty} R(t) \, dt$ geometrically represents the **total "bulb-hours" of survival accumulated** by the original 1000 bulbs over the entire life of the test. Dividing by the initial population (or simply summing up) gives the **average lifetime per bulb** — which is exactly MTTF.

**Think of $R(t)$ as a "survival curve" and MTTF as the total area beneath it.** This is the geometric heart of the integral equation.

## 📌 Key Metrics to Remember

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **MTTF** | Mean Time To Failure | hours / cycles |
| **R(t)** | Reliability at time $t$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| **f(t)** | Failure density | per hour |
| **F(t)** | Cumulative failure probability | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> For **repairable systems**, the analogous concept is **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)**, which equals MTTF + MTTR (Mean Time To Repair). The integral identity $\text{MTBF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t) \, dt$ holds only when MTTR $\approx 0$ or under specific renewal assumptions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🧠 Foundational Logic — The Building Blocks

The derivation rests on three pillars:

1. **Definition of MTTF** (a statistical expected value):
   $$\text{MTTF} = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt$$

2. **Definition of Reliability** (survival probability):
   $$R(t) = P(T > t) = 1 - F(t) = \int_{t}^{\infty} f(x) \, dx$$

3. **Differential relationship** between R(t) and f(t):
   $$f(t) = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

### 🔁 The "Why" Behind the Conversion

Directly computing MTTF using $\int t \cdot f(t) \, dt$ requires knowing the complete failure density, which is often noisy from experimental data. In contrast, **R(t) is monotonically decreasing and smoother**, and can be estimated non-parametrically (e.g., Kaplan–Meier survival analysis). Hence, converting MTTF into an integral of R(t) is both **mathematically elegant and practically advantageous**.

## 🧾 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the following table verbatim — these are **favourite KTU examiner targets** for Part A 3-mark questions.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Context |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R(t) = 1 - F(t)$ | Reliability = 1 − CDF |
| 2 | $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$ | Failure density as negative slope of R(t) |
| 3 | $\text{MTTF} = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} t\, f(t)\, dt$ | Expectation definition |
| 4 | $\boxed{\text{MTTF} = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt}$ | **⭐ Master Identity** |
| 5 | $\text{MTTF} = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\, dt = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | For exponential distribution |
| 6 | $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\eta}\,\Gamma\!\left(1+\dfrac{1}{\beta}\right)$ | Weibull distribution |
| 7 | $\text{MTTF}_{\text{series}} = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty}\prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)\, dt$ | Series system identity |
| 8 | $\text{MTTF}_{\text{parallel}} = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty}\!\left[1-\prod_{i=1}^{n}(1-R_i(t))\right] dt$ | Parallel system identity |
| 9 | $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$ | Repairable systems |
| 10 | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\dfrac{1}{R(t)}\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$ | Hazard / failure rate |

> [!NOTE]
> **No vertical pipes used in the table** — all absolute-value and conditional notations are written as $\vert \cdot \vert$ or with explicit words to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 🏭 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Predicting mean mission duration of satellites before critical subsystem failure.
- **Power Systems:** Computing average lifetime of transformer insulation under thermal stress.
- **Electronics:** Estimating mean operational life of capacitors, ICs, and solder joints.
- **Mechanical:** Predicting bearing fatigue life under cyclic loading.
- **Software Reliability:** Calculating mean time to next defect encounter in modular code bases.

> [!TIP]
> Whenever the KTU question gives $R(t)$ explicitly and asks for MTTF — **skip the differentiation route and directly integrate R(t)**. This is the fastest, lowest-risk path to full marks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 🧮 Derivation 1: The Master Identity $\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt$

### Starting Point

By definition, MTTF is the expected value of the random lifetime $T$:

$$\text{MTTF} = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt \quad \text{...(i)}$$

### Step 1: Apply Integration by Parts (IBP)

Use the standard IBP formula: $\int u\, dv = uv - \int v\, du$

Choose:
- $u = t \quad \Rightarrow \quad du = dt$
- $dv = f(t)\, dt \quad \Rightarrow \quad v = \int f(t)\, dt = F(t)$

(Note: $v$ is an antiderivative of $f(t)$; we pick $v = F(t) = 1 - R(t)$ momentarily, but the cleaner route uses $v = -R(t)$.)

### Step 2: Cleaner IBP Using $v = -R(t)$

Since $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$, we have $\int f(t)\, dt = -R(t)$.

Choose:
- $u = t \quad \Rightarrow \quad du = dt$
- $dv = f(t)\, dt \quad \Rightarrow \quad v = -R(t)$

Then:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} t\, f(t)\, dt = \bigl[ t \cdot (-R(t)) \bigr]_{0}^{\infty} - \int_{0}^{\infty} (-R(t))\, dt$$

### Step 3: Simplify the Boundary Term

$$\bigl[ -t\, R(t) \bigr]_{0}^{\infty} = \lim_{t \to \infty}\bigl[-t\, R(t)\bigr] - \lim_{t \to 0}\bigl[-t\, R(t)\bigr]$$

**At $t = 0$:** $R(0) = 1$, so the term becomes $-0 \cdot 1 = 0$.

**At $t \to \infty$:** For any physically meaningful reliability function, $R(t)$ decays **faster than** $1/t$ (exponential or stretched-exponential decay is typical), so:

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} t\, R(t) = 0$$

Therefore, the boundary term vanishes completely.

### Step 4: Obtain the Final Identity

Substituting back:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} t\, f(t)\, dt = 0 + \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt$$

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The convergence condition $\lim_{t \to \infty} t \cdot R(t) = 0$ is satisfied by **all standard lifetime distributions** (exponential, Weibull, gamma, lognormal). It fails only for heavy-tailed distributions like Pareto with shape parameter $\leq 1$ — but these are rarely seen in KTU problems.

## 🧮 Derivation 2: MTTF for the Exponential Distribution

Given $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ for $\lambda > 0$:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\, dt$$

$$= \left[ \frac{e^{-\lambda t}}{-\lambda} \right]_{0}^{\infty}$$

$$= 0 - \left( \frac{-1}{\lambda} \right)$$

$$= \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **hallmark property of the exponential distribution**: the mean lifetime equals $1/\lambda$, and the standard deviation also equals $1/\lambda$. This **memoryless property** makes it the default model in KTU problems unless stated otherwise.

## 🧮 Derivation 3: MTTF for a Two-Component Series System

For two **independent** components with reliabilities $R_1(t)$ and $R_2(t)$:

$$R_s(t) = R_1(t) \cdot R_2(t)$$

$$\text{MTTF}_s = \int_{0}^{\infty} R_1(t) \cdot R_2(t)\, dt$$

**Example:** If $R_1(t) = e^{-\lambda_1 t}$ and $R_2(t) = e^{-\lambda_2 t}$:

$$\text{MTTF}_s = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2)t}\, dt = \frac{1}{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For exponential components in series, **MTTF adds reciprocally**, but the failure rates **add directly** — a classic KTU trap question.

## 🧮 Derivation 4: MTTF for a Two-Component Parallel System

For two **independent** components in parallel:

$$R_p(t) = 1 - (1 - R_1(t))(1 - R_2(t)) = R_1(t) + R_2(t) - R_1(t)R_2(t)$$

$$\text{MTTF}_p = \int_{0}^{\infty} \bigl[R_1(t) + R_2(t) - R_1(t)R_2(t)\bigr]\, dt$$

$$= \text{MTTF}_1 + \text{MTTF}_2 - \text{MTTF}_s$$

> [!TIP]
> This gives a memorable KTU result: $\text{MTTF}_{\text{parallel}} = \text{MTTF}_1 + \text{MTTF}_2 - \text{MTTF}_{\text{series}}$. Always decompose parallel configurations this way for clean integration.

## 📊 Analytical Matrix: Special Cases at a Glance

| Distribution | $R(t)$ | MTTF |
|---|---|---|
| Exponential($\lambda$) | $e^{-\lambda t}$ | $1/\lambda$ |
| Weibull($\eta, \beta$) | $e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | $\eta \cdot \Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$ |
| Rayleigh($\sigma$) | $e^{-t^{2}/(2\sigma^{2})}$ | $\sigma \sqrt{\pi/2}$ |
| Uniform(0, a) | $1 - t/a$ | $a/2$ |
| Gamma($k, \theta$) | $1 - \Gamma_{\text{inc}}(k, t/\theta)$ | $k\theta$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 🔄 Mermaid Diagram 1: Relationship Between Reliability Concepts

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Random Lifetime T"] --> B["Failure Density f of t"]
    A --> C["Cumulative CDF F of t"]
    A --> D["Reliability R of t"]
    B -- "integral from 0 to t" --> C
    C -- "1 minus F of t" --> D
    B -- "negative derivative of R" --> D
    D -- "integral from 0 to infinity" --> E["MTTF Area under R of t"]
    B -- "integral of t times f of t" --> E
    E --> F["Mean Time To Failure"]
    D --> G["Hazard Rate lambda of t"]
    G --> H["Failure Rate Behaviour"]
    H --> I["Useful Life Region"]
    H --> J["Wear Out Region"]
    H --> K["Infant Mortality Region"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This diagram traces the **information flow** from the underlying random lifetime $T$ down to the operational MTTF metric. The two parallel paths to MTTF — direct via $f(t)$ and integral via $R(t)$ — are visually highlighted.

## 🔄 Mermaid Diagram 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture of MTTF Computation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["INPUT BLOCK: System Definition"] --> P2["Reliability Function R of t"]
    P2 --> P3{"Is R of t Closed Form?"}
    P3 -- "Yes" --> P4["Direct Integration Path"]
    P3 -- "No" --> P5["Numerical Integration Path"]
    P4 --> P6["Trapezoidal or Simpson Rule"]
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> P7["Compute Integral from 0 to infinity"]
    P7 --> P8["Boundary Check: t times R of t at infinity"]
    P8 -- "Equals Zero" --> P9["Valid MTTF Output"]
    P8 -- "Diverges" --> P10["Heavy Tail Warning"]
    P10 --> P11["Use Conditional MTTF up to mission time"]
    P9 --> P12["OUTPUT: MTTF in hours or cycles"]
    P11 --> P12
    P2 --> P13["Alternative: Differentiate to get f of t"]
    P13 --> P14["Compute E of T directly"]
    P14 --> P12
```

> [!TIP]
> KTU students should remember: when $R(t)$ is given, **always choose the integration path** — it is one step shorter and avoids differentiation errors.

## 🔄 Mermaid Diagram 3: Series vs Parallel MTTF Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Component 1 with R1 of t"] --> J1["Joint Reliability R of t"]
    S2["Component 2 with R2 of t"] --> J1
    S3["Component N with RN of t"] --> J1
    J1 -- "Series: product of Ri" --> MS["MTTF Series equals integral of product"]
    J1 -- "Parallel: 1 minus product of 1 minus Ri" --> MP["MTTF Parallel equals integral of union"]
    MS --> OUT["System Level MTTF"]
    MP --> OUT
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### ❓ Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

> **Q:** Define Mean Time To Failure (MTTF). State the integral equation of MTTF in terms of reliability $R(t)$ and explain its significance.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** MTTF is the **expected (mean) operating time** of a non-repairable component or system before it fails for the first time.

**Integral Equation (1 mark):**

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt$$

**Significance (1 mark):** This identity allows computation of MTTF directly from the **reliability function $R(t)$**, which is easier to obtain from survival test data than the failure density $f(t)$. It also gives a geometric interpretation: MTTF is the **area under the reliability curve**.

---

### ❓ Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

> **Q:** For a system with reliability $R(t) = e^{-0.002 t}$ (where $t$ is in hours), find the MTTF.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

This is an **exponential distribution** with failure rate $\lambda = 0.002$ per hour.

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-0.002 t}\, dt = \left[ \frac{e^{-0.002 t}}{-0.002} \right]_{0}^{\infty}$$

$$= 0 - \left( \frac{-1}{0.002} \right) = \frac{1}{0.002} = \mathbf{500 \text{ hours}}$$

---

## 📝 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

### 📌 Question A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

> **(a)** Derive the integral equation of MTTF in terms of the reliability function $R(t)$, starting from the definition of MTTF as the expected lifetime. State clearly any convergence condition required. **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** The reliability of a mechanical subassembly is given by $R(t) = e^{-0.0005 t} \cdot e^{-0.0003 t^{2}}$ for $t \geq 0$ (time in hours). Compute its MTTF using appropriate integration. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Start with MTTF definition:
$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t\, f(t)\, dt$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Use $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$ and integration by parts with $u = t$, $dv = f(t)\,dt$ so that $v = -R(t)$:

$$\text{MTTF} = \bigl[-t\, R(t)\bigr]_{0}^{\infty} + \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** At $t = 0$: $R(0) = 1 \Rightarrow -0 \cdot 1 = 0$.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Convergence condition: $\lim_{t \to \infty} t \cdot R(t) = 0$. This holds for exponential, Weibull, and most standard lifetime distributions because $R(t)$ decays exponentially or faster.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Hence the boundary term vanishes, giving:
$$\boxed{\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** **Geometric interpretation:** MTTF is the area under the $R(t)$ curve from $0$ to $\infty$.

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $R(t) = e^{-0.0005 t} \cdot e^{-0.0003 t^{2}} = e^{-(0.0003 t^{2} + 0.0005 t)}$

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Apply the master identity:
$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(0.0003 t^{2} + 0.0005 t)}\, dt$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Complete the square in the exponent:
$$0.0003 t^{2} + 0.0005 t = 0.0003\!\left(t^{2} + \tfrac{0.0005}{0.0003}t\right) = 0.0003\!\left[\left(t + \tfrac{0.0005}{0.0006}\right)^{2} - \left(\tfrac{0.0005}{0.0006}\right)^{2}\right]$$

$$= 0.0003(t + 0.8333)^{2} - 0.0003(0.6944) = 0.0003(t + 0.8333)^{2} - 0.0002083$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Substituting:
$$\text{MTTF} = e^{0.0002083} \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-0.0003(t+0.8333)^{2}}\, dt$$

Let $u = \sqrt{0.0003}\,(t + 0.8333)$, so $du = \sqrt{0.0003}\, dt$:

$$= \frac{e^{0.0002083}}{\sqrt{0.0003}} \int_{0.4518}^{\infty} e^{-u^{2}}\, du$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Using the standard Gaussian integral $\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-u^{2}}\, du = \dfrac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}$ and $\text{erfc}(x) = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}}\int_{x}^{\infty} e^{-u^{2}}\, du$:

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{e^{0.0002083}}{\sqrt{0.0003}} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} \cdot \text{erfc}(0.4518)$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Numerical evaluation:
- $e^{0.0002083} \approx 1.000208$
- $\sqrt{0.0003} \approx 0.01732$
- $\text{erfc}(0.4518) \approx 0.4940$

$$\text{MTTF} \approx \frac{1.000208 \times 1.7725 \times 0.4940}{2 \times 0.01732} \approx \frac{0.8703}{0.03464} \approx \mathbf{25.12 \text{ hours}}$$

---

### 📌 Question B — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

> **(a)** For a non-repairable system with reliability $R(t) = 1 - \dfrac{t}{1000}$ for $0 \leq t \leq 1000$, find the MTTF. Justify whether this represents a realistic lifetime model. **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** Two components with reliabilities $R_1(t) = e^{-0.001 t}$ and $R_2(t) = e^{-0.002 t}$ are connected in **(i) series** and **(ii) parallel**. Compute the MTTF in each case. Comment on which configuration gives higher reliability. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Apply the master identity:
$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt = \int_{0}^{1000}\!\left(1 - \frac{t}{1000}\right) dt$$

(For $t > 1000$, $R(t) = 0$, so integration naturally truncates.)

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Evaluate:
$$= \left[ t - \frac{t^{2}}{2000} \right]_{0}^{1000} = 1000 - \frac{10^{6}}{2000} = 1000 - 500 = 500$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** MTTF = **500 hours**.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** **Reality check:**
- $R(t) = 1 - t/1000$ represents a **uniform distribution** of failure on $[0, 1000]$.
- This implies a **linearly increasing failure rate** $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{1}{1000 - t}$, which diverges at $t = 1000$ (catastrophic wear-out boundary).
- Realistic for components with **deterministic failure times** (e.g., light bulbs with no infant mortality), but **NOT realistic** for most electronic or mechanical parts.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** **Limitation:** This model has a hard cutoff at $t = 1000$ — physically, $R(t)$ should taper smoothly, not drop to exactly zero. Hence it is a **simplified pedagogical model**, not a realistic lifetime distribution for engineering components.

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) Series Configuration (3 marks):**

$$R_s(t) = R_1(t) \cdot R_2(t) = e^{-0.001 t} \cdot e^{-0.002 t} = e^{-0.003 t}$$

$$\text{MTTF}_{\text{series}} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-0.003 t}\, dt = \frac{1}{0.003} = \mathbf{333.33 \text{ hours}}$$

**(ii) Parallel Configuration (3 marks):**

$$R_p(t) = 1 - (1 - R_1(t))(1 - R_2(t)) = 1 - (1 - e^{-0.001 t})(1 - e^{-0.002 t})$$

$$= e^{-0.001 t} + e^{-0.002 t} - e^{-0.003 t}$$

Using linearity of integration:

$$\text{MTTF}_{\text{parallel}} = \frac{1}{0.001} + \frac{1}{0.002} - \frac{1}{0.003}$$

$$= 1000 + 500 - 333.33 = \mathbf{1166.67 \text{ hours}}$$

**Comment (1 mark):** The **parallel configuration** gives substantially higher MTTF (1166.67 hrs vs 333.33 hrs) — a **3.5× improvement**. This is the quantitative justification for **redundancy** in safety-critical systems (e.g., dual-channel flight control computers, parallel pump arrays in nuclear plants).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Students Lose Marks**
> 
> 1. **❌ Forgetting the convergence condition:** Many students write the master identity without checking $\lim_{t \to \infty} t R(t) = 0$. Examiners deduct **1 full mark** for this omission.
> 2. **❌ Confusing MTTF with MTBF:** For repairable systems, MTTF is **not equal** to MTBF. The integral identity only applies to MTTF, not MTBF, unless MTTR is negligible.
> 3. **❌ Skipping the integration-by-parts setup:** Even if the final answer is correct, students who jump from definition directly to the result lose **derivation marks**.
> 4. **❌ Sign errors in IBP:** A common error is writing $v = R(t)$ instead of $v = -R(t)$, which introduces a sign flip. Always verify: $dv = f(t)\,dt \Rightarrow v = \int f(t)\,dt = -R(t)$ (plus constant, which vanishes in definite integrals).
> 5. **❌ Treating parallel system MTTF additively:** A frequent blunder is writing $\text{MTTF}_p = \text{MTTF}_1 + \text{MTTF}_2$. The correct formula **subtracts the series MTTF** to account for overlap.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 🎯 **Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ⭐ The **master identity**: $\text{MTTF} = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt$ — derive it once, remember it forever.
- ⭐ MTTF applies to **non-repairable systems**; MTBF applies to **repairable systems**.
- ⭐ MTTF = $\dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ for exponential distribution with rate $\lambda$.
- ⭐ Convergence condition: $\lim_{t \to \infty} t \cdot R(t) = 0$ must hold for the identity to be valid.
- ⭐ Derivation path: start with $\int t f(t) dt \to$ IBP with $u = t$, $v = -R(t) \to$ evaluate boundary term $\to$ arrive at $\int R(t) dt$.
- ⭐ For **exponential components in series**: failure rates **add** ($\lambda_s = \sum \lambda_i$), so $\text{MTTF}_s = 1/\sum \lambda_i$.
- ⭐ For **exponential components in parallel**: $\text{MTTF}_p = \sum \text{MTTF}_i - \text{MTTF}_s$ (a powerful KTU shortcut).
- ⭐ Geometric interpretation: MTTF is the **area under the R(t) curve**.
- ⭐ Series systems have **lower MTTF** than their weakest component; parallel systems have **higher MTTF** than their strongest component.
- ⭐ KTU favourite distributions: Exponential, Weibull, Rayleigh, Uniform — memorize their MTTF formulas.
- ⭐ Hazard rate $\lambda(t) = f(t)/R(t)$ — its reciprocal integrated over the mission gives a **conditional reliability measure** when full MTTF diverges.
- ⭐ In numerical problems, **prefer integrating R(t) directly** rather than differentiating to get f(t) and then computing $\int t f(t) dt$ — fewer steps, fewer errors.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hazard Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT754 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hazard Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Hazard Models

In **Reliability Engineering**, a **Hazard Model** is a mathematical representation that describes the instantaneous rate of failure of a component, system, or device, conditional upon its survival up to a specified time. The hazard function, also widely known as the **failure rate function** or **force of mortality**, quantifies the propensity of a system to fail in a small time interval immediately following time $t$, given that the system has successfully survived until time $t$.

Mathematically, the **hazard function** $h(t)$ is defined as the ratio of the probability density function (PDF) of failure $f(t)$ to the reliability function $R(t)$:

$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{f(t)}{1 - F(t)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Hazard Model is the central descriptive tool in Module 2 of PEBRT754. Students must master the relationship between the hazard function, reliability function, failure density function, and cumulative hazard function. Exam questions frequently appear on deriving one function from another.

The **cumulative hazard function** $H(t)$ is the integral of the instantaneous hazard rate from time $0$ to time $t$:

$$H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} h(x) \, dx$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Hazard Rate Units:** The hazard rate $h(t)$ carries the unit of **failures per unit time** (e.g., failures/hour). The cumulative hazard $H(t)$ is dimensionless and represents the expected number of failures in the interval $(0, t]$.

## 1.2 The Five Fundamental Reliability Functions

For any continuous non-negative random variable $T$ representing the **Time-To-Failure (TTF)**, the following five functions completely characterize the failure behavior:

| Function | Symbol | Formal Definition |
|:---------|:------:|:------------------|
| Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) | $F(t)$ | $P(T \le t)$ |
| Reliability (Survival) Function | $R(t)$ | $P(T > t) = 1 - F(t)$ |
| Probability Density Function (PDF) | $f(t)$ | $\dfrac{dF(t)}{dt} = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$ |
| Hazard (Failure Rate) Function | $h(t)$ | $\dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ |
| Cumulative Hazard Function | $H(t)$ | $\displaystyle\int_{0}^{t} h(x) \, dx = -\ln R(t)$ |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The "Mortality" Interpretation

Imagine you are an actuary studying the **mortality of a fleet of identical electric motors** in a factory. The **hazard rate** at age $t$ answers the question: *"Out of all motors that have ALREADY survived until time $t$, what fraction will fail in the next instant?"* It is fundamentally a **conditional probability rate** — the rate is computed only on the population of survivors, not on the original population.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Intuition:** A young human adult (age 25) has a very low hazard rate (low probability of dying in the next instant). A person aged 90 has a very high hazard rate. The hazard function captures this **age-conditional risk profile** of components — exactly analogous to human mortality tables used by insurance companies.

## 1.4 The Bathtub Curve: Visualizing Hazard Behavior

The most famous visual representation in reliability engineering is the **Bathtub Curve**, which plots $h(t)$ versus $t$ and divides the operating life of a component into three distinct regions:

1. **Infant Mortality Region (Burn-In Period):** The hazard rate is **decreasing** because defective components fail early due to manufacturing defects, poor quality, or installation errors. Once these weak units are weeded out, the surviving population is healthier.

2. **Useful Life Region (Normal Operation):** The hazard rate is **approximately constant** (flat). Failures are random in nature — caused by random overloads, environmental stresses, or chance events. This region dominates the operational lifespan.

3. **Wear-Out Region (Aging Period):** The hazard rate is **increasing** due to physical degradation — metal fatigue, corrosion, insulation breakdown, bearing wear, etc. Components progressively deteriorate until catastrophic failure becomes more probable.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** The hazard rate is NOT a probability — it is a **rate** (probability per unit time). Its value can exceed 1, especially in the wear-out region, because it is not a normalized probability but a density-like conditional rate.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic of Hazard Models

A hazard model specifies a **functional form** for $h(t)$ based on physical reasoning about the failure mechanism. The choice of model depends on:
- The **physical failure mechanism** (wear, corrosion, random shock, infant mortality, etc.)
- The **shape of empirical failure data** when fitted
- The **mathematical tractability** required for system-level analysis

The reliability function can always be **recovered** from the hazard function using the fundamental identity:

$$R(t) = \exp\left(-\int_{0}^{t} h(x) \, dx\right) = e^{-H(t)}$$

This identity is the cornerstone of hazard modeling. Once $h(t)$ is known (either by assumption or by fitting data), the entire statistical life-characteristics of the component are determined.

## 2.2 Classification of Standard Hazard Models

### 2.2.1 Constant Hazard Model (Exponential Distribution)

The simplest and most analytically tractable model. The hazard rate is **independent of time**:

$$h(t) = \lambda \quad (\text{constant})$$

This is the defining characteristic of the **Exponential Distribution** of failure times. The corresponding reliability function is:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** Used for systems with **no memory** — the future failure probability of a surviving component does not depend on how long it has already operated. This is the appropriate model during the useful-life region of the bathtub curve.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memoryless Property:** $P(T > s + t \mid T > s) = P(T > t)$. A component that has survived 1000 hours is no "better" or "worse" than a brand new one. This is mathematically equivalent to a Poisson process for failure arrivals.

### 2.2.2 Linearly Increasing Hazard Model

Used to model the **wear-out phase**:

$$h(t) = \alpha + \beta t, \quad \alpha, \beta \ge 0$$

- $\alpha$ is the **initial hazard** (baseline failure rate)
- $\beta$ is the **rate of increase of hazard** (reflects degradation speed)

The reliability function is obtained by integration:

$$R(t) = \exp\left(-\alpha t - \frac{\beta t^{2}}{2}\right)$$

This is the **Rayleigh Distribution** when $\alpha = 0$. Used in practice for **ball bearings, rotating machinery, and mechanical wear-out**.

### 2.2.3 Linearly Decreasing Hazard Model

Used to model **infant mortality (burn-in)**:

$$h(t) = \alpha - \beta t, \quad 0 \le t \le \alpha/\beta$$

For this to be valid as a hazard rate, $\beta < \alpha/t$ across the operating range, so the function remains non-negative. The reliability function is:

$$R(t) = \exp\left(-\alpha t + \frac{\beta t^{2}}{2}\right)$$

### 2.2.4 Weibull Hazard Model (The Versatile Generalization)

The most widely used **general-purpose** hazard model in industry. It encompasses all three regions of the bathtub curve through a single shape parameter:

$$h(t) = \frac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta - 1}, \quad t \ge 0$$

- $\alpha > 0$ is the **scale parameter** (characteristic life)
- $\beta > 0$ is the **shape parameter** (controls curve shape)
- $\beta = 1$: Reduces to exponential (constant hazard)
- $\beta < 1$: Decreasing hazard (infant mortality)
- $\beta > 1$: Increasing hazard (wear-out)

The reliability function is:

$$R(t) = \exp\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right]$$

### 2.2.5 Power Law (Duane) Hazard Model

Used during **development testing** when collecting reliability growth data:

$$h(t) = \alpha \beta t^{\beta - 1}$$

- When $\beta < 1$, the hazard is decreasing — indicating that **reliability is growing** with time as design defects are discovered and corrected.
- Used by the **U.S. Department of Defense** and aerospace industries for reliability growth tracking.

### 2.2.6 Gamma Distribution Hazard Model

For more complex failure processes involving multiple independent shock events (e.g., multiple stress impacts):

$$f(t) = \frac{\lambda^{\alpha}}{\Gamma(\alpha)} t^{\alpha-1} e^{-\lambda t}$$

$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{\lambda^{\alpha} t^{\alpha-1} e^{-\lambda t}}{\Gamma(\alpha, \lambda t)}$$

where $\Gamma(\alpha, \lambda t)$ is the **upper incomplete gamma function**. This model is used when there are **multiple failure mechanisms** acting concurrently.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MEMORIZE THE TABLE BELOW** — these are the most frequently tested formulas in KTU PEBRT754 Module 2.

| Hazard Model | $h(t)$ | $R(t)$ | MTTF | Best Use |
|:-------------|:------:|:------:|:----:|:---------|
| Constant (Exponential) | $\lambda$ | $e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Useful life (random failures) |
| Linear Increasing | $\alpha + \beta t$ | $\exp\left(-\alpha t - \dfrac{\beta t^{2}}{2}\right)$ | $\dfrac{\sqrt{\pi}\, \text{erfc}(\alpha/\sqrt{2\beta})}{2\sqrt{\beta/2}}$ | Wear-out |
| Linear Decreasing | $\alpha - \beta t$ | $\exp\left(-\alpha t + \dfrac{\beta t^{2}}{2}\right)$ | Depends on $\alpha/\beta$ | Burn-in / infant mortality |
| Weibull | $\dfrac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\dfrac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1}$ | $\exp\left[-\left(\dfrac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right]$ | $\alpha \, \Gamma\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\beta}\right)$ | All three regions |
| Power Law (Duane) | $\alpha \beta t^{\beta-1}$ | $\exp\left(-\alpha t^{\beta}\right)$ | $\alpha^{-1/\beta} \Gamma\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\beta}\right)$ | Reliability growth |
| Gamma | $\dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | $1 - \dfrac{\gamma(\alpha, \lambda t)}{\Gamma(\alpha)}$ | $\dfrac{\alpha}{\lambda}$ | Multiple shock events |

**Key Identity Chain (KTU Exam Favorite):**

$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}[-\ln R(t)] = \frac{dH(t)}{dt}$$

$$R(t) = e^{-H(t)} = e^{-\int_0^t h(x)\, dx}$$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Engineering Field | Typical Hazard Model | Why? |
|:------------------|:---------------------|:-----|
| **Electronics (resistors, capacitors)** | Constant / Weibull with $\beta = 1$ | Stable useful-life operation |
| **Mechanical bearings** | Weibull with $\beta > 1$ (often $\beta \approx 1.5$) | Dominant wear-out mechanism |
| **Aircraft turbines** | Weibull with $\beta \approx 2$ to $3$ | Strong aging, fatigue-driven |
| **Software (bugs)** | Decreasing hazard | Bug-fix reduces hazard over releases |
| **Power grid transformers** | Weibull with $\beta \approx 4$ to $6$ | Sharp wear-out, insulation aging |
| **Aerospace development** | Duane / Power Law | Tracking reliability growth during test-fix-test cycles |
| **Semiconductors (early life)** | Decreasing linear hazard | Infant mortality, latent defects |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Fundamental Derivation: From Hazard Function to Reliability Function

**Given:** The hazard function $h(t)$ for a system.

**To Prove:** $R(t) = \exp\left(-\int_0^t h(x)\, dx\right)$

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of the hazard function:**

$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 2 — Express the PDF in terms of the reliability function.**

Since the reliability function and CDF are complementary: $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$, differentiating both sides with respect to $t$:

$$\frac{dF(t)}{dt} = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

By definition, the PDF is the derivative of the CDF: $f(t) = \dfrac{dF(t)}{dt}$. Therefore:

$$f(t) = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the hazard definition:**

$$h(t) = \frac{-dR(t)/dt}{R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange into a separable differential equation:**

$$h(t)\, dt = -\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 5 — Integrate both sides from $0$ to $t$:**

$$\int_{0}^{t} h(x)\, dx = -\int_{0}^{t} \frac{1}{R(x)} \cdot \frac{dR(x)}{dx}\, dx = -\int_{R(0)}^{R(t)} \frac{1}{R}\, dR$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the right-hand integral:**

$$\int_{0}^{t} h(x)\, dx = -[\ln R]_{R(0)}^{R(t)} = -[\ln R(t) - \ln R(0)]$$

**Step 7 — Apply the initial condition.** At $t = 0$, the system has not yet failed, so $R(0) = 1$ and $\ln R(0) = 0$:

$$\int_{0}^{t} h(x)\, dx = -\ln R(t) + 0 = -\ln R(t)$$

**Step 8 — Solve for $R(t)$:**

$$H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} h(x)\, dx = -\ln R(t) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \ln R(t) = -H(t)$$

Exponentiating both sides:

$$R(t) = e^{-H(t)} = \exp\left(-\int_{0}^{t} h(x)\, dx\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is the **single most important derivation** in KTU PEBRT754 Module 2. Examiners frequently ask students to derive the reliability function from a given hazard model, or vice-versa.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation: Linear Increasing Hazard Model

**Given:** $h(t) = \alpha + \beta t$

**Step 1 — Apply the fundamental identity:**

$$R(t) = \exp\left(-\int_{0}^{t} (\alpha + \beta x)\, dx\right)$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$\int_{0}^{t} (\alpha + \beta x)\, dx = \left[\alpha x + \frac{\beta x^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{t} = \alpha t + \frac{\beta t^{2}}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Write the reliability function:**

$$R(t) = \exp\left(-\alpha t - \frac{\beta t^{2}}{2}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Derive the PDF by differentiating $f(t) = -dR/dt$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
f(t) &= -\frac{dR(t)}{dt} = -R(t) \cdot \left(-\alpha - \beta t\right) \\
&= (\alpha + \beta t) \cdot \exp\left(-\alpha t - \frac{\beta t^{2}}{2}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Verification of correctness:** Check that $h(t) = f(t)/R(t) = (\alpha + \beta t)$. ✓ The consistency confirms the derivation.

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation: Weibull Hazard Model — Deriving the Reliability Function

**Given:** $h(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\dfrac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1}$

**Step 1 — Compute the cumulative hazard $H(t)$:**

$$H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} \frac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\frac{x}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1} dx = \frac{\beta}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{1}{\alpha^{\beta-1}} \int_{0}^{t} x^{\beta-1} dx$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the integral using the power rule:**

$$\int_{0}^{t} x^{\beta-1} dx = \frac{t^{\beta}}{\beta}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back:**

$$H(t) = \frac{\beta}{\alpha^{\beta}} \cdot \frac{t^{\beta}}{\beta} = \left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}$$

**Step 4 — Apply $R(t) = e^{-H(t)}$:**

$$R(t) = \exp\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right]$$

**Step 5 — Derive the Weibull PDF:**

$$\begin{aligned}
f(t) &= -R'(t) = -\frac{d}{dt}\left\{\exp\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right]\right\} \\
&= \frac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1} \exp\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right]
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Derivation: Constant Hazard Model — From First Principles

**Given:** $h(t) = \lambda$

**Step 1 — Compute the cumulative hazard:**

$$H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} \lambda \, dx = \lambda t$$

**Step 2 — Apply the fundamental identity:**

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 3 — Derive the PDF:**

$$f(t) = -\frac{dR}{dt} = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 4 — Derive the Mean Time To Failure (MTTF):**

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\, dt = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

**Step 5 — Derive the variance of the exponential distribution:**

$$\text{Var}(T) = \frac{1}{\lambda^{2}} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \sigma_{T} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Result for KTU:** The standard deviation equals the mean for the exponential distribution. This is a unique signature — no other common reliability distribution has $\sigma = \mu$.

---

## 3.5 Inverse Derivation: From Reliability Function to Hazard Function

**Given:** $R(t)$ is known, derive $h(t)$.

**Step 1 — Compute the PDF using $f(t) = -R'(t)$:**

$$f(t) = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the hazard formula $h(t) = f(t)/R(t)$:**

$$h(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt} = -\frac{d}{dt}\left[\ln R(t)\right]$$

**Numerical Example:** If $R(t) = e^{-0.02 t} - 0.0001 t^{3}$ for $t \in [0, 50]$, find $h(20)$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{dR}{dt} &= -0.02 \, e^{-0.02t} - 0.0003 t^{2} \\
R(20) &= e^{-0.4} - 0.0001 (8000) = 0.6703 - 0.8 \quad \text{(negative — not valid!)}
\end{aligned}$$

This negative result reminds students that **not every mathematically valid $R(t)$ is a valid reliability function** — $R(t)$ must be non-increasing and bounded in $[0,1]$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Validity Constraints on $R(t)$:** (1) $R(0) = 1$, (2) $R(\infty) = 0$, (3) $R(t)$ is monotonically non-increasing, (4) $R(t) \ge 0$ for all $t \ge 0$. Any proposed $R(t)$ violating these conditions is not a valid reliability function.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Properties Comparison

| Property | Exponential | Weibull $(\beta > 1)$ | Linear Increasing |
|:---------|:-----------:|:---------------------:|:-----------------:|
| MTTF | $1/\lambda$ | $\alpha \Gamma(1+1/\beta)$ | Finite, closed form in terms of error function |
| Variance | $1/\lambda^{2}$ | Closed form in $\Gamma$ | Function of $\alpha, \beta$ |
| Memoryless? | **Yes** | No | No |
| Used for | Useful life | Wear-out (if $\beta > 1$) | Wear-out |
| Failure rate at $t=0$ | $\lambda$ | $0$ (if $\beta > 1$) | $\alpha$ |
| Failure rate $\to \infty$? | No | Yes (if $\beta > 1$) | Yes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Bathtub Curve — Hazard Rate vs Time

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph T0["INFANT MORTALITY"]
        A0["Region I: Decreasing Hazard"]
        A1["h(t) is HIGH but FALLING"]
        A2["Cause: Manufacturing defects, latent flaws, poor installation"]
    end
    subgraph T1["USEFUL LIFE"]
        B0["Region II: Constant Hazard"]
        B1["h(t) = lambda (flat)"]
        B2["Cause: Random shocks, environmental stress"]
    end
    subgraph T2["WEAR-OUT"]
        C0["Region III: Increasing Hazard"]
        C1["h(t) is LOW but RISING"]
        C2["Cause: Fatigue, corrosion, aging, insulation breakdown"]
    end
    A0 --> B0
    B0 --> C0
    A0 --- A1
    A1 --- A2
    B0 --- B1
    B1 --- B2
    C0 --- C1
    C1 --- C2
```

```mermaid
    H0["Cumulative Hazard H(t) = integral of h"]
    MTTF0["MTTF = integral of R(t) dt"]

    R0 -->|"differentiate"| f0

```
