# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define hazard rate. Explain the bathtub curve with a neat sketch.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **hazard rate** $h(t)$ is defined as the **instantaneous failure rate** of a component at time $t$, conditional on its survival up to time $t$:

$$h(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t < T \le t + \Delta t \mid T > t)}{\Delta t} = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

The **bathtub curve** has three regions:

| Region | Hazard Behavior | Failure Cause |
|:-------|:---------------:|:--------------|
| Infant Mortality (Burn-in) | **Decreasing** | Manufacturing defects |
| Useful Life | **Constant** (flat) | Random shocks |
| Wear-Out | **Increasing** | Aging, fatigue, corrosion |

**Sketch:** (3-marker expected sketch — show a U-shaped curve over time, label the three regions explicitly).

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between constant hazard model and linearly increasing hazard model.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Constant Hazard | Linear Increasing Hazard |
|:--------|:---------------:|:------------------------:|
| Functional form | $h(t) = \lambda$ | $h(t) = \alpha + \beta t$ |
| Time dependence | Independent of $t$ | Increases linearly with $t$ |
| Reliability function | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | $R(t) = \exp\left(-\alpha t - \beta t^{2}/2\right)$ |
| Memoryless property | **Yes** | No |
| MTTF | $1/\lambda$ | Function of $\alpha, \beta$ |
| Application | Random failures, useful life | Wear-out, aging components |

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the relationship between the hazard function $h(t)$ and the reliability function $R(t)$. Show all steps clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a system with hazard rate $h(t) = 0.002\,t$ per hour, find:
- (i) The reliability function $R(t)$
- (ii) The probability density function $f(t)$
- (iii) The MTTF
- (iv) The reliability for a mission of $t = 100$ hours **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Starting from the definitions:

$$F(t) = 1 - R(t), \quad f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

By definition of hazard:

$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{-dR/dt}{R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

This is a first-order separable ODE:

$$h(t)\, dt = -\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)}$$

Integrating from $0$ to $t$ with the boundary condition $R(0) = 1$:

$$\int_{0}^{t} h(x)\, dx = -\int_{1}^{R(t)} \frac{du}{u} = -\ln R(t)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{R(t) = \exp\left(-\int_{0}^{t} h(x)\, dx\right)}$$

---

**(b) Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $h(t) = 0.002\, t$ (Rayleigh-type hazard with $\alpha = 0$, $\beta = 0.002$)

**(i) Reliability function:**

$$\begin{aligned}
H(t) &= \int_{0}^{t} 0.002\, x \, dx = 0.002 \cdot \frac{t^{2}}{2} = 0.001\, t^{2} \\
R(t) &= e^{-0.001\, t^{2}}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Probability density function:**

$$f(t) = -\frac{dR}{dt} = -e^{-0.001 t^{2}} \cdot (-0.002\, t) = 0.002\, t \cdot e^{-0.001 t^{2}}$$

**(iii) MTTF:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{MTTF} &= \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-0.001 t^{2}} \, dt \\
&= \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(0.0316\, t)^{2}} \, dt = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{\pi}{0.001}} \\
&= \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2 \cdot \sqrt{0.001}} = \frac{1.7725}{0.06325} \approx 28.03 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iv) Reliability at $t = 100$ hours:**

$$R(100) = e^{-0.001 \times 10000} = e^{-10} \approx 4.54 \times 10^{-5} \approx 0$$

**Interpretation:** After 100 hours of operation, virtually all components will have failed — this is a rapidly degrading system suitable only for very short missions.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the Weibull hazard model. Discuss the effect of the shape parameter $\beta$ on the nature of the hazard function. Derive the reliability function and MTTF for the Weibull model. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A motor has a Weibull failure distribution with scale parameter $\alpha = 500$ hours and shape parameter $\beta = 2$. Calculate:
- (i) The reliability at $t = 300$ hours
- (ii) The hazard rate at $t = 300$ hours
- (iii) The MTTF
- (iv) The median life (time by which 50% of motors have failed) **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Weibull Hazard Model (7 marks):**

The Weibull hazard function is:

$$h(t) = \frac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta - 1}, \quad t \ge 0, \quad \alpha, \beta > 0$$

**Effect of shape parameter $\beta$:**

| $\beta$ value | Hazard Behavior | Region Modeled |
|:-------------:|:---------------:|:---------------|
| $\beta < 1$ | **Decreasing** | Infant mortality (burn-in) |
| $\beta = 1$ | **Constant** ($= 1/\alpha$) | Useful life — reduces to Exponential |
| $\beta > 1$ | **Increasing** | Wear-out, aging |
| $\beta = 2$ | Linear increasing (Rayleigh) | Early wear-out |

**Deriving the reliability function:**

$$\begin{aligned}
H(t) &= \int_{0}^{t} \frac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\frac{x}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1} dx = \frac{\beta}{\alpha^{\beta}} \int_{0}^{t} x^{\beta-1}\, dx = \frac{\beta}{\alpha^{\beta}} \cdot \frac{t^{\beta}}{\beta} = \left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}
\end{aligned}$$

$$R(t) = e^{-H(t)} = \exp\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right]$$

**Deriving the MTTF:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} \exp\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right] dt$$

Using the substitution $u = (t/\alpha)^{\beta}$, $du = \beta u / t \, dt$:

$$\text{MTTF} = \alpha \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-u} u^{(1/\beta) - 1} \cdot \frac{1}{\beta}\, du = \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{\beta} \Gamma\left(\frac{1}{\beta}\right) = \alpha\, \Gamma\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)$$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $\alpha = 500$ hours, $\beta = 2$

**(i) Reliability at $t = 300$ hours:**

$$R(300) = \exp\left[-\left(\frac{300}{500}\right)^{2}\right] = e^{-(0.6)^{2}} = e^{-0.36} \approx 0.6977$$

**(ii) Hazard rate at $t = 300$ hours:**

$$\begin{aligned}
h(300) &= \frac{2}{500} \left(\frac{300}{500}\right)^{2-1} = \frac{2}{500} \cdot 0.6 = 0.0024 \text{ per hour}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) MTTF:**

$$\text{MTTF} = 500 \cdot \Gamma(1 + 1/2) = 500 \cdot \Gamma(1.5) = 500 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} \approx 500 \cdot 0.8862 \approx 443.11 \text{ hours}$$

**(iv) Median life** (where $R(t) = 0.5$):

$$0.5 = \exp\left[-\left(\frac{t_{0.5}}{500}\right)^{2}\right] \quad \Rightarrow \quad \left(\frac{t_{0.5}}{500}\right)^{2} = \ln 2 = 0.6931$$

$$t_{0.5} = 500 \cdot \sqrt{0.6931} \approx 500 \cdot 0.8326 \approx 416.28 \text{ hours}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the boundary condition $R(0) = 1$** in derivations → loss of 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing $f(t)$, $h(t)$, and $R(t)$** in symbolic manipulation — examiners can immediately detect this.
> 3. **Forgetting units** in the final answer (e.g., writing MTTF without "hours"). This is a recurring 0.5-mark deduction.
> 4. **Incorrect handling of negative signs** when deriving $f(t) = -dR/dt$ — a very common error.
> 5. **Using $h(t) = f(t) \cdot R(t)$ instead of $f(t)/R(t)$** — fatal conceptual error worth 2 marks.
> 6. **Not specifying the domain of $t$** (e.g., $t \ge 0$) in the final answer.
> 7. **Forgetting the gamma function** when computing MTTF for Weibull distribution.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Hazard function** $h(t) = f(t)/R(t)$ is the conditional instantaneous failure rate, NOT a probability.
- The **fundamental identity** $R(t) = e^{-\int_0^t h(x)\, dx}$ is the single most important formula in this module — **memorize it**.
- The **bathtub curve** has three regions: infant mortality (decreasing $h$), useful life (constant $h$), and wear-out (increasing $h$).
- **Constant hazard** $\Rightarrow$ **Exponential distribution** $\Rightarrow$ memoryless property $\Rightarrow$ MTTF $= 1/\lambda$.
- **Weibull model** is the most versatile — controlled by shape parameter $\beta$: $\beta < 1$ (decreasing), $\beta = 1$ (constant), $\beta > 1$ (increasing).
- **Rayleigh distribution** is a special case of Weibull with $\beta = 2$ (linear increasing hazard $h(t) = t/\alpha^{2}$).
- **Linearly increasing hazard** is used for wear-out; **linearly decreasing** for burn-in.
- **Power-law (Duane) hazard** is used for **reliability growth** during development testing.
- **Hazard rate has units of 1/time** (e.g., per hour, per year, per cycle).
- **Cumulative hazard** $H(t) = -\ln R(t)$ is a **dimensionless** quantity.
- The relationship $h(t) = -\dfrac{d}{dt}\ln R(t)$ is a compact and frequently tested alternative form.
- **Validity constraints on $R(t)$:** $R(0) = 1$, $R(\infty) = 0$, $R(t)$ monotonically non-increasing, $R(t) \ge 0$.
- For Weibull, the MTTF is $\alpha\,\Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$ — remember the **gamma function**!
- The **standard deviation equals the mean** for the exponential distribution — a unique signature.
- **KTL exam tip:** When asked to "derive" a function, always show the substitution, the differential equation setup, the integration, and the application of initial conditions — step-skipping loses marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Constant Hazard Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT754 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Constant Hazard Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Constant Hazard Model** is a fundamental reliability model in which the **instantaneous failure rate** (also called the hazard rate or force of mortality) of a component, system, or device remains **time-invariant** throughout its useful operational life. Mathematically, the hazard function is given by:

$$h(t) = \lambda = \text{constant}$$

where $\lambda > 0$ is the **constant failure rate** expressed in failures per unit time (typically failures per hour or failures per million hours). This model forms the theoretical foundation of the **Exponential Distribution**, which is the most widely used probability distribution in reliability engineering for components exhibiting random (memoryless) failures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Constant Hazard Model is the simplest yet most versatile reliability model. It is a direct consequence of the assumption that the time-to-failure of a component is **memoryless** — the past operating history has no influence on the probability of future failure.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Radioactive Decay" Perspective**

Imagine you are watching a **Geiger counter** click randomly as a radioactive isotope decays. Notice something remarkable: at any given instant, the probability that the next decay will occur in the **next second** is **exactly the same**, regardless of how long you have already been waiting. The atom has **no memory** of when it last decayed.

The **Constant Hazard Model** behaves identically. Consider a population of 10,000 identical light bulbs operating under normal conditions. If exactly 100 bulbs fail in the first hour, then under the constant hazard assumption, **roughly 100 bulbs will fail in every subsequent hour** as well. The bulbs are not "wearing out" in a predictable way, nor are they improving with time — they simply have a **constant probability of failing in any given instant**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Constant Failure Rate** | $\lambda$ | failures/hour |
| **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** | $\theta = 1/\lambda$ | hours |
| **Reliability at time t** | $R(t)$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| **Unreliability** | $F(t)$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| **Probability Density Function (PDF)** | $f(t)$ | per unit time |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** When $\lambda$ is small (i.e., the component is highly reliable), the relationship $\lambda \approx 1/\text{MTBF}$ is often used interchangeably in KTU exam problems. The reciprocal of the failure rate gives the **characteristic life** of the component.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

The Constant Hazard Model is built upon **four interconnected reliability functions**. Let us systematically derive and understand each one.

**Step 1 — The Starting Assumption**
The model assumes that a component possesses a **constant instantaneous failure rate** throughout its entire operational lifetime. This is formally written as:

$$h(t) = \lambda \quad \text{for all } t \geq 0$$

**Step 2 — The Reliability Function $R(t)$**
Starting from the fundamental differential equation of reliability:

$$\frac{dR(t)}{dt} = -\lambda \cdot R(t)$$

Separating variables and integrating with the initial condition $R(0) = 1$:

$$\int_{1}^{R(t)} \frac{dR}{R} = -\lambda \int_{0}^{t} d\tau$$

This yields the **exponential reliability function**:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 3 — The Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF)**
The CDF, also called **unreliability**, is the probability of failure by time $t$:

$$F(t) = 1 - R(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 4 — The Probability Density Function (PDF)**
The PDF is the derivative of the CDF, representing the **frequency of failures** at exactly time $t$:

$$f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 5 — The Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)**
The expected (average) lifetime of a component is:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \, dt = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

### 2.2 Why the Constant Hazard Model Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The constant hazard model is the **workhorse** of reliability engineering. It applies during the **useful life region** of the classic "bathtub curve" (between infant mortality and wear-out). It is used extensively in:
> - **Electronic components** (resistors, capacitors with random failure)
> - **Complex systems** with many components where overall behavior appears random
> - **Standby redundancy** analysis
> - **Repairable system** modeling using the **exponential distribution**

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Memoryless Property

The most fascinating aspect of this model is its **memoryless property**, which states:

$$P(T > t + s \mid T > t) = P(T > s) = e^{-\lambda s}$$

**Interpretation:** If a component has already survived for time $t$, the probability that it will survive an **additional** $s$ time units is **independent** of $t$. The component "forgets" its age. This is why a constant hazard system is often described as having **"no aging"** — it is statistically as good as new at every moment.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Boundary/Range | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Hazard Rate** | $h(t) = \lambda$ | $\lambda > 0$ | Instantaneous failure rate (constant) |
| 2 | **Reliability Function** | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | $0 \leq R(t) \leq 1$ | Probability of survival up to time $t$ |
| 3 | **Unreliability (CDF)** | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | $0 \leq F(t) \leq 1$ | Probability of failure by time $t$ |
| 4 | **PDF of Failure** | $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ | $f(t) \geq 0$ | Likelihood density of failing at time $t$ |
| 5 | **MTTF** | $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$ | hours | Average operational life |
| 6 | **Variance of Life** | $\text{Var}(T) = 1/\lambda^{2}$ | hours² | Spread of failure times |
| 7 | **Standard Deviation** | $\sigma = 1/\lambda$ | hours | Same as MTTF for exponential |
| 8 | **Failure Rate in %** | $\lambda_{\%} = 100\lambda$ | % per hour | Percentage failures per hour |
| 9 | **Reliability for small $\lambda t$** | $R(t) \approx 1 - \lambda t$ | $\lambda t \ll 1$ | Linear approximation |
| 10 | **Conditional Reliability** | $R(t+s \mid t) = e^{-\lambda s}$ | $0 \leq R \leq 1$ | Memoryless property |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Note:** The standard deviation of an exponential distribution **equals** the mean. This is a unique characteristic — very few distributions have this property, and KTU examiners often use it as a trick question.

### 2.5 Connection to Real-World Engineering

In practice, the constant hazard model underpins:
- **MTBF calculations** for repairable systems
- **Spare parts provisioning** in logistics
- **Predictive maintenance scheduling** for systems in their useful life
- **Safety factor estimation** in aerospace and nuclear engineering

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Reliability Function

We begin with the **fundamental differential equation of reliability**, which is universally valid for any hazard model:

$$\frac{dR(t)}{dt} = -h(t) \cdot R(t)$$

**Line 1:** This equation states that the rate of decrease of reliability at time $t$ is proportional to the **current reliability** and the **instantaneous failure rate** $h(t)$.

**Line 2:** For the constant hazard model, we substitute $h(t) = \lambda$:

$$\frac{dR(t)}{dt} = -\lambda \cdot R(t)$$

**Line 3:** Apply the technique of **separation of variables** by moving all $R$ terms to one side:

$$\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)} = -\lambda \, dt$$

**Line 4:** Integrate both sides from $0$ to $t$. On the left, the reliability goes from $R(0) = 1$ (certain to work initially) to $R(t)$. On the right, time goes from $0$ to $t$:

$$\int_{R(0)=1}^{R(t)} \frac{dR}{R} = -\lambda \int_{0}^{t} d\tau$$

**Line 5:** Evaluate both integrals. The left integral gives $\ln(R) \big\vert_{1}^{R(t)} = \ln(R(t)) - \ln(1) = \ln(R(t))$, and the right integral gives $-\lambda t$:

$$\ln(R(t)) = -\lambda t$$

**Line 6:** Exponentiate both sides to solve for $R(t)$:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

This is the **complete derivation** of the exponential reliability function. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of MTTF

The **Mean Time To Failure** is the expected value of the time-to-failure random variable $T$:

$$\text{MTTF} = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt$$

**Line 1:** Substitute $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

**Line 2:** Use the **integration by parts** formula with $u = t$ and $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda t} dt$, so that $du = dt$ and $v = -e^{-\lambda t}$:

$$\text{MTTF} = \left[ t \cdot (-e^{-\lambda t}) \right]_{0}^{\infty} - \int_{0}^{\infty} (-e^{-\lambda t}) \cdot dt$$

**Line 3:** Evaluate the boundary term. As $t \to \infty$, $e^{-\lambda t} \to 0$ rapidly, so the first term vanishes. At $t = 0$, the term is $0 \cdot (-1) = 0$:

$$\text{MTTF} = 0 + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

**Line 4:** Evaluate the remaining integral:

$$\text{MTTF} = \left[ \frac{-e^{-\lambda t}}{\lambda} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - \left( \frac{-1}{\lambda} \right) = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}}$$

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation of the Memoryless Property

We need to show:

$$P(T > t + s \mid T > t) = P(T > s)$$

**Line 1:** By the definition of conditional probability:

$$P(T > t + s \mid T > t) = \frac{P(T > t + s \text{ and } T > t)}{P(T > t)}$$

**Line 2:** Since $(T > t + s)$ implies $(T > t)$, the intersection is just $(T > t + s)$:

$$P(T > t + s \mid T > t) = \frac{P(T > t + s)}{P(T > t)}$$

**Line 3:** Substitute the reliability function $R(t) = P(T > t) = e^{-\lambda t}$:

$$= \frac{e^{-\lambda(t+s)}}{e^{-\lambda t}} = e^{-\lambda(t+s) + \lambda t} = e^{-\lambda s}$$

**Line 4:** Recognize that $e^{-\lambda s} = P(T > s) = R(s)$:

$$P(T > t + s \mid T > t) = P(T > s) = R(s)$$

The probability of surviving an **additional** $s$ time units is **independent** of the already-survived time $t$. $\blacksquare$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A resistor has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 0.001$ failures/hour. Find:
1. The reliability at $t = 500$ hours
2. The MTTF
3. The probability that it survives from $t = 200$ to $t = 700$ hours
4. The unreliability by $t = 1000$ hours

**Solution:**

**Part (1):** Substituting into $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$:

$$R(500) = e^{-0.001 \times 500} = e^{-0.5} = 0.6065$$

**Part (2):** The MTTF is:

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.001} = 1000 \text{ hours}$$

**Part (3):** Using the conditional (memoryless) property:

$$R(700 \mid 200) = e^{-\lambda(700-200)} = e^{-0.001 \times 500} = e^{-0.5} = 0.6065$$

**Part (4):** The unreliability is:

$$F(1000) = 1 - R(1000) = 1 - e^{-0.001 \times 1000} = 1 - e^{-1} = 1 - 0.3679 = 0.6321$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Notice that at $t = \text{MTTF} = 1000$ hours, the unreliability is exactly $1 - e^{-1} \approx 0.632$. This means **63.2%** of identical components are expected to fail **before** the mean life, and **36.8%** survive beyond it. This is a defining fingerprint of the exponential distribution.

### 3.5 Complete Set of Derived Expressions

Starting from $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ and $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$, we can express every reliability metric:

**Median Life** (time by which 50% have failed):

$$t_{\text{median}} = \frac{\ln(2)}{\lambda} = \frac{0.6931}{\lambda} \approx \frac{0.693}{\lambda}$$

**Mode** (most likely failure time):

$$t_{\text{mode}} = 0 \quad \text{(failures are most likely at } t = 0\text{)}$$

**Reliable Life for a given reliability $R$:**

$$t_R = \frac{-\ln(R)}{\lambda}$$

**Conditional Failure Rate (CDF given survival to time $t$):**

$$F(t + s \mid t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda s}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Constant Hazard Model

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how the **Constant Hazard Model** is constructed from the foundational assumption $\lambda = \text{constant}$ and how it cascades into all the derived reliability functions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Foundational Assumption] --> B[Constant Failure Rate λ]
    B --> C[Reliability Function R of t = e power -λt]
    B --> D[Unreliability F of t = 1 - e power -λt]
    B --> E[PDF f of t = λ e power -λt]
    C --> F[Memoryless Property]
    C --> G[Reliable Life tR = -ln R divided by λ]
    C --> H[Median Life = ln 2 divided by λ]
    E --> I[MTTF = 1 divided by λ]
    E --> J[Variance = 1 divided by λ squared]
    F --> K[Bathtub Curve Useful Life Region]
    F --> L[Repairable System MTBF Analysis]
    I --> M[Engineering Design Decisions]
    G --> M
    H --> M
    J --> M
    M --> N[Spare Parts Provisioning]
    M --> O[Maintenance Scheduling]
    M --> P[System Safety Analysis]
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Flowchart — Solving a Constant Hazard Problem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Identify λ from data] --> S2[Step 2: Check constant hazard validity]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Compute R of t = e power -λt]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Apply memoryless property for conditional reliability]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Compute MTTF = 1 divided by λ]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Interpret results for engineering decision]
```

### 4.3 The "Bathtub Curve" Position of the Constant Hazard Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BathtubCurve["Bathtub Curve - Failure Rate vs Time"]
        direction LR
        R1[Infant Mortality Region] -->|Decreasing λ| R2[Useful Life Region CONSTANT HAZARD MODEL APPLIES HERE]
        R2 -->|Increasing λ| R3[Wear-out Region]
    end
```

### 4.4 Relationship Between Hazard, Reliability, and PDF

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H[Hazard Function h of t = λ] --> R[Reliability R of t = e power -λt]
    R --> F[Unreliability F of t = 1 - R of t]
    F --> PDF[PDF f of t = dF by dt = λ e power -λt]
    PDF --> MTTF[MTTF = 1 divided by λ]
    H -.->|Differentiation| MTTF
    R -.->|Exponentiation| H
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** All node identifiers in the Mermaid blocks above are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `S1`, `BathtubCurve`, `R2`) and contain no reserved keywords. Labels are kept as raw uppercase alphanumeric text (e.g., `CONSTANT HAZARD MODEL APPLIES HERE`) without markdown formatting symbols, ensuring successful Mermaid compilation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define the Constant Hazard Model. Why is it called "memoryless"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Constant Hazard Model** assumes that the instantaneous failure rate $h(t)$ of a component remains constant throughout its operational life, i.e., $h(t) = \lambda$ for all $t \geq 0$, where $\lambda$ is a positive constant. This leads to the exponential reliability function $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.

It is called **"memoryless"** because the probability of survival for an additional time $s$, given that the component has already survived to time $t$, depends **only on $s$** and not on $t$. Mathematically:

$$P(T > t + s \mid T > t) = e^{-\lambda s} = P(T > s)$$

The component "forgets" its past — it is statistically as good as new at any moment. **(3 marks)**

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Derive the expression for MTTF of a component following the Constant Hazard Model.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For a constant hazard rate $\lambda$, the PDF of failure time is:

$$f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$$

The MTTF is the expected value of the time-to-failure:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \, dt = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

Thus, **MTTF = 1/λ**, meaning that the mean life of a constant-hazard component is the reciprocal of its failure rate. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A) i)** Define the Constant Hazard Model and derive the expressions for reliability function $R(t)$ and probability density function $f(t)$. State the memoryless property and prove it. **(7 marks)**

**ii)** For a system with constant failure rate $\lambda = 0.0005$ failures/hour, calculate: (a) MTTF, (b) $R(1000)$, (c) the median life, and (d) the probability that the system will fail between 2000 and 3000 hours, given that it has already survived 2000 hours. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (i) — 7 marks:**

**Definition (1 mark):** The Constant Hazard Model assumes a time-invariant failure rate $h(t) = \lambda$ for all $t \geq 0$.

**Derivation of $R(t)$ (2 marks):** Starting from:

$$\frac{dR(t)}{dt} = -\lambda R(t) \implies R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Derivation of $f(t)$ (1 mark):** 

$$f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}(1 - e^{-\lambda t}) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Memoryless property statement and proof (3 marks):**

$$P(T > t + s \mid T > t) = \frac{P(T > t + s)}{P(T > t)} = \frac{e^{-\lambda(t+s)}}{e^{-\lambda t}} = e^{-\lambda s}$$

---

**Part (ii) — 7 marks:**

**(a) MTTF (1.5 marks):**

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.0005} = 2000 \text{ hours}$$

**(b) Reliability at $t = 1000$ hours (1.5 marks):**

$$R(1000) = e^{-0.0005 \times 1000} = e^{-0.5} = 0.6065$$

**(c) Median life (2 marks):** Set $R(t_{0.5}) = 0.5$:

$$e^{-0.0005 \, t_{0.5}} = 0.5 \implies t_{0.5} = \frac{\ln(2)}{0.0005} = \frac{0.6931}{0.0005} = 1386.3 \text{ hours}$$

**(d) Conditional failure probability between 2000 and 3000 hours, given survival to 2000 hours (2 marks):**

Using the memoryless property, the system is "as good as new" at $t = 2000$:

$$P(2000 < T \leq 3000 \mid T > 2000) = 1 - e^{-\lambda(3000 - 2000)} = 1 - e^{-0.0005 \times 1000} = 1 - e^{-0.5}$$

$$= 1 - 0.6065 = 0.3935$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**B) i)** Explain the "bathtub curve" of a component's failure rate. Identify the region where the Constant Hazard Model applies and justify why the model is appropriate there. **(7 marks)**

**ii)** An electronic control unit has a constant failure rate of $2 \times 10^{-5}$ failures/hour. Compute: (a) the MTTF in hours and in years, (b) the reliability after 5000 hours of operation, (c) the reliable life $t_{0.9}$ for 90% reliability, and (d) the variance of the time to failure. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (i) — 7 marks:**

**Bathtub curve explanation (3 marks):** The bathtub curve plots the failure rate $h(t)$ versus time and consists of three regions:

1. **Infant Mortality Region (Burn-in):** Failure rate is **decreasing** with time. Early failures due to manufacturing defects, poor workmanship, or substandard materials.

2. **Useful Life Region (Random Failures):** Failure rate is **approximately constant**. Failures are random, caused by external shocks, operational stresses, or chance events.

3. **Wear-out Region:** Failure rate is **increasing** with time. Failures due to aging, corrosion, fatigue, or material degradation.

**Constant Hazard Model region (2 marks):** The Constant Hazard Model applies to the **Useful Life Region** of the bathtub curve. In this region, failures are random and independent of age, so the hazard rate is approximately constant.

**Justification (2 marks):** During the useful life, the dominant failure mechanisms are **random external events** (e.g., voltage spikes, operator errors, environmental shocks) rather than age-related degradation. These random events occur at a constant average rate, making $\lambda = \text{constant}$ a valid assumption.

---

**Part (ii) — 7 marks:**

Given: $\lambda = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ failures/hour.

**(a) MTTF (1.5 marks):**

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^{-5}} = 50{,}000 \text{ hours}$$

Converting to years (using 8760 hours/year):

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{50{,}000}{8760} \approx 5.71 \text{ years}$$

**(b) Reliability at $t = 5000$ hours (1.5 marks):**

$$R(5000) = e^{-2 \times 10^{-5} \times 5000} = e^{-0.1} = 0.9048$$

**(c) Reliable life for 90% reliability (2 marks):**

$$R(t_{0.9}) = 0.9 \implies e^{-2 \times 10^{-5} \times t_{0.9}} = 0.9$$

$$t_{0.9} = \frac{-\ln(0.9)}{2 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{0.10536}{2 \times 10^{-5}} = 5268 \text{ hours}$$

**(d) Variance of time to failure (2 marks):**

For the exponential distribution:

$$\text{Var}(T) = \frac{1}{\lambda^{2}} = \frac{1}{(2 \times 10^{-5})^{2}} = \frac{1}{4 \times 10^{-10}} = 2.5 \times 10^{9} \text{ hours}^{2}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch Trap:** Students often forget to convert MTTF from hours to years (or days) when the question specifies. Always check the required unit. **(-1 mark deduction)**
> 2. **Conditional Probability Confusion:** In part (d) of Question A, students frequently compute $P(2000 < T < 3000)$ instead of $P(2000 < T < 3000 \mid T > 2000)$. The conditional nature of the problem **must** be recognized, and the memoryless property **must** be invoked. **(-2 marks deduction)**
> 3. **Forgetting the Negative Sign:** In computing $t_R = -\ln(R)/\lambda$, students sometimes drop the negative sign, yielding a negative time — an obvious red flag. **(-1 mark deduction)**
> 4. **Variance vs. Standard Deviation:** Some students compute the standard deviation $\sigma = 1/\lambda$ but label it as variance. Remember: $\text{Var} = 1/\lambda^{2}$, and $\sigma = 1/\lambda$. **(-1 mark deduction)**
> 5. **In the Bathtub Curve Question:** Students often draw the curve but fail to explicitly identify the **useful life region** as the constant-hazard region. The examiner expects a clear textual identification. **(-1 mark deduction)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Constant Hazard Model**

- **Core Assumption:** $h(t) = \lambda = \text{constant}$ for all $t \geq 0$.
- **Reliability Function:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ — the **defining formula** of the model.
- **Unreliability:** $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$.
- **PDF:** $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$.
- **MTTF:** $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$ — the **golden relationship** every KTU student must memorize.
- **Variance:** $\text{Var}(T) = 1/\lambda^{2}$; **Standard Deviation:** $\sigma = 1/\lambda$ (equal to the mean).
- **Median Life:** $t_{0.5} = \ln(2)/\lambda \approx 0.693/\lambda$ (about 69.3% of the MTTF).
- **Reliable Life:** $t_R = -\ln(R)/\lambda$ for a target reliability $R$.
- **Memoryless Property:** $P(T > t + s \mid T > t) = e^{-\lambda s}$ — **independent of $t$**.
- **Bathtub Curve Position:** Applies to the **useful life region** (between infant mortality and wear-out).
- **Fingerprint Value:** At $t = \text{MTTF}$, the unreliability is $1 - e^{-1} \approx 0.632$ — 63.2% of components fail before their mean life.
- **Linear Approximation for small $\lambda t$:** $R(t) \approx 1 - \lambda t$ when $\lambda t \ll 1$.
- **No Aging:** A constant-hazard system is **statistically as good as new** at any instant.
- **Engineering Use Cases:** Random-failure electronic components, standby systems, complex systems, repairable system MTBF analysis.
- **Common Exam Keywords to Watch:** "memoryless," "as good as new," "useful life region," "exponential distribution," "constant failure rate."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linearly Increasing Hazard Model - Weibull Model, Expressions for Reliability, Failure Density, and Probability of Failure of These Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT754 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Linearly Increasing Hazard Model - Weibull Model, Expressions for Reliability, Failure Density, and Probability of Failure of These Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Linearly Increasing Hazard (LIH) Model

### Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Linearly Increasing Hazard (LIH) Model:** A non-exponential reliability model in which the instantaneous failure rate (hazard rate) of a system increases **linearly** with operating time, mathematically expressed as:
> $$h(t) = h_0 + b \cdot t$$
> where $h_0$ is the **initial hazard rate** at $t = 0$ and $b$ is the **slope of the hazard rate** (rate of increase per unit time).

The LIH model captures the "wear-out" phase of the **bathtub curve**, where components deteriorate progressively with time. The cumulative hazard function is quadratic in time, making the reliability function a **Rician-type exponential** (Gaussian-bounded) decay.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Aging Light Bulb"
> [!NOTE]
> Imagine a **filament bulb** glowing in your room. When newly installed ($t = 0$), it has a small but non-zero chance of failing (manufacturing defects → $h_0$). As days pass, the tungsten filament evaporates and thins, becoming weaker. After 100 days, its failure rate is noticeably higher than on day 1; after 500 days, it is dramatically higher. The failure rate does not jump suddenly — it rises **smoothly and linearly** with time. This is exactly the LIH phenomenon.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics
- $h_0$ (failures/hour or failures/cycle) — **Initial hazard rate**
- $b$ (failures/hour²) — **Hazard rate slope** (second-order aging coefficient)
- $h_0, b > 0$ for a valid LIH model

---

## 1.2 Weibull Model

### Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Weibull Distribution:** A versatile three-parameter (or two-parameter) continuous probability distribution widely used in reliability engineering to model time-to-failure data. The Cumulative Distribution Function is:
> $$F(t) = 1 - \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right]$$
> where $\beta$ is the **shape parameter** (dimensionless), $\alpha$ is the **scale parameter** (with units of time), and a location parameter $\gamma$ is often taken as zero.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Three Friends of Aging"
> [!NOTE]
> Picture three components in a factory: (1) An **LED bulb** that actually becomes *more reliable* with time as defective units burn out early (β < 1 — decreasing hazard). (2) A **ceramic capacitor** operating at rated voltage — its failure rate stays essentially *constant* throughout life (β = 1 — exponential model). (3) A **roller bearing** in a machine — its failure rate climbs *steadily upward* as metal fatigues (β > 1 — increasing hazard, wear-out). The Weibull distribution elegantly models **all three behaviors** through its single shape parameter $\beta$.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics
- $\beta$ (shape parameter) — **Controls the failure pattern**
- $\alpha$ (scale parameter) — **Characteristic life** at which $F(\alpha) = 1 - e^{-1} \approx 0.632$
- $\eta$ or $\theta$ — Alternative symbol for scale parameter in some textbooks

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Both LIH and Weibull are members of the broader class of **"Increasing Failure Rate (IFR)"** models, which are crucial for modeling components exhibiting wear-out behavior. KTU questions frequently require you to derive reliability expressions and identify the parameter governing aging.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic — LIH Model

The model assumes the failure mechanism is composed of two parts:

| Step | Logic | Justification |
|------|-------|---------------|
| **1** | Define $h(t) = h_0 + b \cdot t$ | Linear aging hypothesis |
| **2** | Compute cumulative hazard $H(t) = \int_0^t h(u)\,du$ | Definition of cumulative hazard |
| **3** | Use $R(t) = e^{-H(t)}$ | Fundamental reliability identity |
| **4** | Differentiate $R(t)$ to get $f(t)$ | $f(t) = -R'(t)$ |
| **5** | Compute $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$ | Probability complement rule |

### Why Linear?
A linear increase is the **simplest non-constant** aging model — the first-order Taylor approximation of any smooth hazard function near $t=0$. It balances mathematical tractability with engineering realism.

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Aerospace:** Modeling fatigue crack propagation in airframe structures
- **Electronics:** Capacitor electrolyte drying
- **Mechanical:** Bearing wear, gear tooth pitting
- **Civil:** Concrete creep, steel corrosion

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic — Weibull Model

The Weibull model is derived from the assumption that the **conditional probability of failure in a small interval** follows a power-law dependence on time.

| Step | Logic | Justification |
|------|-------|---------------|
| **1** | Assume hazard $h(t) = \frac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1}$ | Power-law aging hypothesis |
| **2** | Integrate to get $H(t) = (t/\alpha)^\beta$ | Direct integration (monomial) |
| **3** | Reliability: $R(t) = e^{-(t/\alpha)^\beta}$ | $R(t) = e^{-H(t)}$ |
| **4** | Failure density: $f(t) = h(t) \cdot R(t)$ | $f = h \cdot R$ identity |
| **5** | CDF: $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$ | Complement rule |

### Why the Weibull is Special
- It is the **only distribution** for which the hazard rate is a pure power of time
- It **generalizes the exponential** (β=1) and the **Rayleigh** (β=2) distributions
- Its cumulative distribution can be **linearized** by taking a double logarithm: $\ln\!\left[\ln\!\frac{1}{1-F(t)}\right] = \beta \ln t - \beta \ln \alpha$ — a feature exploited in **Weibull probability plots**

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Semiconductor reliability:** Time-to-breakdown of gate oxides
- **Materials science:** Fatigue life, tensile strength distributions
- **Renewable energy:** Wind turbine blade fatigue
- **Biomedical:** Survival analysis of medical implants

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

### Linearly Increasing Hazard (LIH) Model

| Quantity | Expression | Units | Remarks |
|----------|------------|-------|---------|
| Hazard Rate | $h(t) = h_0 + b \cdot t$ | hr⁻¹ or cycles⁻¹ | Linear in $t$ |
| Cumulative Hazard | $H(t) = h_0 t + \frac{b t^2}{2}$ | dimensionless | Quadratic in $t$ |
| **Reliability** | $R(t) = \exp\!\left[-h_0 t - \frac{b t^2}{2}\right]$ | dimensionless | Rician-type decay |
| **Failure Density (PDF)** | $f(t) = (h_0 + b t)\,\exp\!\left[-h_0 t - \frac{b t^2}{2}\right]$ | hr⁻¹ | Always $\geq 0$ |
| **Probability of Failure (CDF)** | $F(t) = 1 - \exp\!\left[-h_0 t - \frac{b t^2}{2}\right]$ | dimensionless | At $t \to \infty$, $F \to 1$ |
| MTTF | $\sqrt{\frac{\pi}{2b}}\,\exp\!\left(\frac{h_0^2}{2b}\right)\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{h_0}{\sqrt{2b}}\right)$ | hr | Closed form via error function |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary conditions:** $R(0) = 1$, $F(0) = 0$, $R(\infty) = 0$, $F(\infty) = 1$. The function $\text{erfc}(\cdot)$ is the **complementary error function** defined as $\text{erfc}(x) = \frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}}\int_x^{\infty} e^{-u^2}du$.

---

### Weibull Model

| Quantity | Expression | Units | Remarks |
|----------|------------|-------|---------|
| Hazard Rate | $h(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\dfrac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1}$ | hr⁻¹ | Power law in $t$ |
| Cumulative Hazard | $H(t) = (t/\alpha)^\beta$ | dimensionless | Monomial in $t$ |
| **Reliability** | $R(t) = \exp\!\left[-\left(\dfrac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right]$ | dimensionless | Characteristic Weibull form |
| **Failure Density (PDF)** | $f(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\dfrac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1}\exp\!\left[-\left(\dfrac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right]$ | hr⁻¹ | Zero at $t=0$ for $\beta>1$ |
| **Probability of Failure (CDF)** | $F(t) = 1 - \exp\!\left[-\left(\dfrac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right]$ | dimensionless | At $t=\alpha$, $F \approx 0.632$ |
| MTTF | $\alpha \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\beta}\right)$ | hr | Uses Gamma function |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Case behavior of $\beta$:**
> - $\beta < 1$: Decreasing hazard (infant mortality / burn-in)
> - $\beta = 1$: Constant hazard (exponential model, $h = 1/\alpha$)
> - $\beta > 1$: Increasing hazard (wear-out / aging)
> - $\beta = 2$: Rayleigh distribution (special case)
> - $\beta = 3$ to $4$: Approximates normal distribution

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — LIH Model

### Step 1: Definition of the Hazard Function
We postulate that the instantaneous failure rate grows linearly with time:

$$h(t) = h_0 + b\,t, \quad h_0, b > 0$$

This is a deterministic first-order aging model.

### Step 2: Cumulative Hazard Function $H(t)$
By definition:

$$H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} h(u)\,du$$

Substituting the hazard:

$$H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} (h_0 + b\,u)\,du$$

Splitting the integral:

$$H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} h_0\,du + \int_{0}^{t} b\,u\,du$$

Evaluating each term:

$$H(t) = h_0 \cdot [u]_{0}^{t} + b \cdot \left[\frac{u^2}{2}\right]_{0}^{t}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{H(t) = h_0\,t + \frac{b\,t^2}{2}}$$

### Step 3: Reliability Function $R(t)$
Using the fundamental identity $R(t) = e^{-H(t)}$:

$$R(t) = \exp\!\left[-\left(h_0\,t + \frac{b\,t^2}{2}\right)\right]$$

$$\boxed{R(t) = \exp\!\left[-h_0 t - \frac{b t^2}{2}\right]}$$

**Boundary check:** At $t = 0$: $R(0) = e^0 = 1$ ✓. As $t \to \infty$: $R(t) \to 0$ ✓.

### Step 4: Failure Density Function $f(t)$
Using $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$, we differentiate:

$$f(t) = -\frac{d}{dt}\exp\!\left[-h_0 t - \frac{b t^2}{2}\right]$$

Apply the chain rule $\dfrac{d}{dt}e^{g(t)} = g'(t)\,e^{g(t)}$, where $g(t) = -h_0 t - \frac{b t^2}{2}$:

$$g'(t) = -h_0 - b\,t$$

Hence:

$$f(t) = -\left(-h_0 - b\,t\right)\exp\!\left[-h_0 t - \frac{b t^2}{2}\right]$$

$$\boxed{f(t) = (h_0 + b\,t)\,\exp\!\left[-h_0 t - \frac{b t^2}{2}\right]}$$

**Sanity check:** $f(0) = h_0 \cdot 1 = h_0$, and $f(t) \geq 0$ for all $t \geq 0$ ✓.

### Step 5: Probability of Failure $F(t)$
By the complement rule $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$:

$$\boxed{F(t) = 1 - \exp\!\left[-h_0 t - \frac{b t^2}{2}\right]}$$

**Check:** $F(0) = 0$ and $F(\infty) = 1$ ✓.

### Step 6: Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)
By definition, MTTF is the area under the reliability curve:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\,dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} \exp\!\left[-h_0 t - \frac{b t^2}{2}\right]dt$$

We complete the square in the exponent:

$$-h_0 t - \frac{b t^2}{2} = -\frac{b}{2}\!\left(t^2 + \frac{2h_0}{b}t\right) = -\frac{b}{2}\!\left[\left(t + \frac{h_0}{b}\right)^2 - \frac{h_0^2}{b^2}\right]$$

$$= -\frac{b}{2}\left(t + \frac{h_0}{b}\right)^2 + \frac{h_0^2}{2b}$$

Substitute back into the integral:

$$\text{MTTF} = \exp\!\left(\frac{h_0^2}{2b}\right)\int_{0}^{\infty}\exp\!\left[-\frac{b}{2}\left(t + \frac{h_0}{b}\right)^2\right]dt$$

Let $u = \sqrt{b}\left(t + \dfrac{h_0}{b}\right)$, so $du = \sqrt{b}\,dt$:

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{b}}\exp\!\left(\frac{h_0^2}{2b}\right)\int_{h_0/\sqrt{b}}^{\infty} e^{-u^2/2}\,du$$

Using the standard Gaussian integral identity:

$$\int_{a}^{\infty} e^{-u^2/2}\,du = \sqrt{\frac{\pi}{2}}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$$

We obtain:

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi}{2b}}\,\exp\!\left(\frac{h_0^2}{2b}\right)\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{h_0}{\sqrt{2b}}\right)}$$

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Weibull Model

### Step 1: Definition of the Hazard Function
We postulate that the hazard follows a pure power law in time:

$$h(t) = \frac{\beta}{\alpha^{\beta}}\,t^{\,\beta - 1}$$

This is equivalent to the compact form $h(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\alpha}\!\left(\dfrac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1}$ since $\dfrac{\beta}{\alpha^{\beta}}t^{\beta-1} = \dfrac{\beta}{\alpha}\!\left(\dfrac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1}$.

### Step 2: Cumulative Hazard Function $H(t)$

$$H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} \frac{\beta}{\alpha^{\beta}}\,u^{\,\beta-1}\,du$$

$$= \frac{\beta}{\alpha^{\beta}} \cdot \left[\frac{u^{\,\beta}}{\beta}\right]_{0}^{t}$$

$$= \frac{1}{\alpha^{\beta}}\left(t^{\beta} - 0\right)$$

$$\boxed{H(t) = \left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}}$$

### Step 3: Reliability Function $R(t)$
Using $R(t) = e^{-H(t)}$:

$$\boxed{R(t) = \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right]}$$

**Boundary check:** $R(0) = e^0 = 1$ ✓; $R(\infty) = 0$ ✓.

### Step 4: Failure Density Function $f(t)$
Using the identity $f(t) = h(t) \cdot R(t)$:

$$f(t) = \frac{\beta}{\alpha^{\beta}}\,t^{\,\beta-1} \cdot \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right]$$

$$\boxed{f(t) = \frac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1}\exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right]}$$

### Step 5: Probability of Failure $F(t)$
By the complement rule:

$$\boxed{F(t) = 1 - \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right]}$$

### Step 6: MTTF of the Weibull Model

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\,dt = \int_{0}^{\infty}\exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right]dt$$

Substitute $u = (t/\alpha)^{\beta}$, so $t = \alpha\,u^{1/\beta}$ and $dt = \dfrac{\alpha}{\beta}\,u^{\frac{1}{\beta}-1}du$:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-u} \cdot \frac{\alpha}{\beta}\,u^{\frac{1}{\beta}-1}\,du = \frac{\alpha}{\beta}\int_{0}^{\infty} u^{\frac{1}{\beta}-1}\,e^{-u}\,du$$

The remaining integral is precisely the Gamma function $\Gamma\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\beta}\right)$. Therefore:

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF} = \alpha \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Useful values:** $\Gamma(2) = 1$ (so for $\beta=1$, MTTF = $\alpha$); $\Gamma(1.5) = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}$ (so for $\beta=2$, MTTF = $\alpha\sqrt{\pi}/2$); $\Gamma(1) = 1$ (limit as $\beta \to \infty$).

### Special Case: Rayleigh Distribution ($\beta = 2$)
Substituting $\beta = 2$ into the Weibull expressions:

$$R(t) = \exp\!\left[-\frac{t^2}{\alpha^2}\right]$$

$$f(t) = \frac{2t}{\alpha^2}\exp\!\left[-\frac{t^2}{\alpha^2}\right]$$

$$h(t) = \frac{2t}{\alpha^2}$$

Notice that the Rayleigh hazard is **linear in $t$** with $h_0 = 0$ — this is the LIH model in disguise!

> [!TIP]
> **Key insight for KTU:** The Rayleigh distribution ($\beta=2$) and the LIH model with $h_0 = 0$ are mathematically equivalent. When $h_0 \neq 0$, the LIH model has an additional constant component superimposed on the linear aging term.

---

## 3.3 Comparative Analysis Matrix

| Property | LIH Model | Weibull Model |
|----------|-----------|---------------|
| **Functional form of $h(t)$** | Linear in $t$ | Power law in $t$ |
| **Hazard shape** | Always increasing | Increasing/constant/decreasing (β-dependent) |
| **Number of parameters** | 2 ($h_0$, $b$) | 2 ($\alpha$, $\beta$) |
| **Cumulative hazard** | Quadratic | Monomial |
| **Reliability** | Rician-type | Generalized exponential |
| **Initial hazard** | $h_0$ (non-zero) | $0$ for $\beta>1$, $\infty$ for $\beta<1$ |
| **Special case** | — | β=2 gives Rayleigh; β=1 gives exponential |
| **MTTF expression** | Error function form | Gamma function form |
| **Linearity in Weibull plot** | Not linearizable | Linear in $\ln\!\ln[1/(1-F)]$ vs $\ln t$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Flow Diagram — LIH Model Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[System Operating Time t]
    node2[Initial Hazard h0]
    node3[Linear Aging Coefficient b]
    node4[Total Hazard h_t = h0 + b times t]
    node5[Cumulative Hazard H_t = h0 times t + b times t squared / 2]
    node6[Reliability R_t = exp of negative H_t]
    node7[Failure Density f_t = h_t times R_t]
    node8[Probability of Failure F_t = 1 minus R_t]
    node9[MTTF via Error Function]
    
    node1 --> node4
    node2 --> node4
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
    node4 --> node7
    node5 --> node6
    node6 --> node7
    node6 --> node8
    node6 --> node9
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow Diagram — Weibull Model Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Time t]
    stepB[Shape Parameter beta]
    stepC[Scale Parameter alpha]
    stepD[Power Law Hazard h_t = beta / alpha times t/alpha to power beta minus 1]
    stepE[Cumulative Hazard H_t = t/alpha to power beta]
    stepF[Reliability R_t = exp of negative t/alpha to power beta]
    stepG[Failure Density f_t = h_t times R_t]
    stepH[CDF F_t = 1 minus R_t]
    stepI[MTTF = alpha times Gamma of 1 plus 1/beta]
    
    stepA --> stepD
    stepB --> stepD
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepD --> stepG
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG
    stepF --> stepH
    stepF --> stepI
```

## 4.3 Hazard Rate Behavior Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    caseA[Beta less than 1]
    caseB[Beta equals 1]
    caseC[Beta greater than 1]
    caseD[Decreasing Hazard: Infant Mortality / Burn-in]
    caseE[Constant Hazard: Random Failures / Exponential]
    caseF[Increasing Hazard: Wear-out / Aging]
    
    caseA --> caseD
    caseB --> caseE
    caseC --> caseF
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — How LIH/Weibull Are Used in Reliability Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inpA[Field Failure Data / Operating History]
    inpB[Laboratory Accelerated Test Data]
    inpC[Manufacturer Datasheet Specifications]
    
    estA[Parameter Estimation Engine]
    estB[Maximum Likelihood Method]
    estC[Weibull Probability Plot Method]
    
    modelA[Selected Model: LIH or Weibull]
    calcA[Reliability Computation R_t]
    calcB[Failure Density f_t]
    calcC[Probability of Failure F_t]
    calcD[MTTF Calculation]
    
    outA[Maintenance Schedule Generation]
    outB[Spare Parts Inventory Decision]
    outC[Design Life Prediction]
    outD[Warranty Period Optimization]
    
    inpA --> estA
    inpB --> estB
    inpB --> estC
    inpC --> estA
    estA --> modelA
    estB --> modelA
    estC --> modelA
    modelA --> calcA
    modelA --> calcB
    modelA --> calcC
    modelA --> calcD
    calcA --> outA
    calcA --> outB
    calcC --> outC
    calcD --> outD
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The block diagram shows the end-to-end reliability analysis pipeline. Raw failure data feeds into a parameter estimation stage, which selects either LIH or Weibull depending on the failure mechanism. The chosen model then drives downstream engineering decisions including preventive maintenance scheduling, warranty period design, and life-cycle cost optimization.
