topics.title: Reliability Block Diagrams (RBD), Components Connected in Series
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT754 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Reliability Block Diagrams (RBD), Components Connected in Series

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition — Reliability Block Diagram (RBD)

> [!NOTE]
> **Reliability Block Diagram (RBD):** A graphical, block-level schematic representation of a system's logical architecture, in which each block represents a component (or sub-system) and the interconnections between blocks represent the functional reliability relationships (series, parallel, k-out-of-n, bridge, etc.) required for the overall system to successfully perform its intended function.

An RBD is **NOT** a physical circuit diagram or a wiring schematic. It is a **logical / functional model** showing the *minimum set of components* whose simultaneous successful operation is required for the system to be considered "UP" (i.e., functional).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** RBDs are the foundational tool in Module 3 of Reliability Engineering. They form the prerequisite for understanding redundancy, parallel systems, and complex configurations (standby, k-out-of-n, bridge networks) covered later in the module.

## 1.2 Fundamental Assumptions in RBD Analysis (KTU Board Standard)

For all KTU exam problems, the following assumptions are taken as **default** unless otherwise stated:

- **Independence**: The reliability of each component is statistically independent of the others.
- **Two-State Components**: Every component is either perfectly working (state 1) or completely failed (state 0). No partial states.
- **Series Logic**: The system functions **only if every component in the block diagram functions**.
- **Non-Repairable Assumption** (during the analysis interval): Unless specifically asked, the system is considered non-repairable over the mission time *t*.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Old Christmas Lights" 💡

Imagine a string of **50 old-style incandescent Christmas lights** connected in a single line. The moment **even one bulb fuses (fails)**, the *entire* string goes dark. This is exactly how a **series RBD** works:

- Each light bulb = one **block** in the RBD.
- The wire connecting them in a line = the **series connection**.
- For the whole string to glow (system success) → **every single bulb** must glow.
- One fuse → total blackout → **system failure**.

This single-bulb-takes-down-the-whole-string behavior is the hallmark of **series configuration**, and it is the *weakest-link* philosophy of system design.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Examples of Series Systems:**
> - A satellite launch vehicle: every stage (booster, sustainer, guidance, payload fairing) must work for mission success.
> - A water pumping pipeline: pump → pipe → valve → filter must all function.
> - A computer boot sequence: SMPS → Motherboard → RAM → CPU → Storage (all must initialize).

## 1.4 Key Metrics Used in Series RBD Analysis

- **R(t)** — Reliability function (probability of survival up to time *t*).
- **F(t)** — Unreliability / CDF of failure: $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$.
- **λ (lambda)** — Failure rate (constant or time-dependent, in **failures per hour**).
- **MTBF / MTTF** — Mean Time Between Failures / Mean Time To Failure (in **hours**).
- **f(t)** — Probability Density Function (PDF) of time-to-failure.
- **h(t)** — Hazard / Failure rate function.

These are universal reliability metrics and all apply directly to series-connected components.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Series Configuration — The Logic of "ALL must work"

A system (or sub-system) is modeled as a **series configuration** in an RBD when the system performs its intended function **if and only if every single component performs its function**. If *any one* component fails, the entire system fails.

### 2.1.1 Logical Statement

Let the system success event be $S$ and the success of component $i$ be $S_i$. For a series system:

$$S = S_1 \cap S_2 \cap S_3 \cap \cdots \cap S_n$$

Failure of the system is the complement:

$$\bar{S} = \bar{S_1} \cup \bar{S_2} \cup \bar{S_3} \cup \cdots \cup \bar{S_n}$$

(i.e., the system fails if **at least one** component fails — the *OR* condition on failures).

### 2.1.2 Why "Series" Means "Weakest-Link"

Since the overall reliability is the **product** of individual reliabilities (derived below), and every factor $R_i(t) \leq 1$, the resulting system reliability is always **less than or equal to the reliability of the least reliable component**:

$$R_S(t) \leq \min\{R_1(t), R_2(t), \ldots, R_n(t)\}$$

This is the **weakest-link principle** — the chain is only as strong as its weakest link.

## 2.2 Derivation Logic — Step by Step Intuition

**Step 1:** Probability of success of component *i* over time *t* is $R_i(t)$.

**Step 2:** Because of the *statistical independence* of component failures, the joint probability of *all* components succeeding simultaneously is the **product** of their individual probabilities.

**Step 3:** This product — by the series logic definition — is the system reliability $R_S(t)$.

**Step 4:** If the components follow the **exponential distribution** (constant failure rate), then $R_i(t) = e^{-\lambda_i t}$ and the products collapse into a single exponential with summed failure rate.

**Step 5:** The reciprocal of the effective failure rate gives the **MTBF of the system**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master These 6 Formulas — They Appear in 90% of KTU Series-RBD Problems.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $R_S(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)$ | Series system reliability (general) | Dimensionless probability, $0 \leq R_S \leq 1$ |
| 2 | $R_S(t) = R^n$ | Series of *n* **identical** components, each with reliability $R$ | Quick formula for identical blocks |
| 3 | $R_S(t) = e^{-\lambda_S t} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{-\lambda_i t}$ | Series system under exponential distribution | $e^{-\lambda_S t} = e^{-(\lambda_1+\lambda_2+\cdots+\lambda_n)t}$ |
| 4 | $\lambda_S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$ | **Effective failure rate of series system** (constant) | Failures per hour |
| 5 | $\text{MTBF}_S = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_S} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i}$ | Mean Time Between Failures of series system | Hours (or whichever time unit) |
| 6 | $F_S(t) = 1 - R_S(t) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)$ | Unreliability of series system | Dimensionless probability |

### 2.3.1 Critical Boundary Conditions & Properties

- **Lower Bound**: $R_S(t) \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$ (system reliability collapses as more components are added in series).
- **Upper Bound**: $R_S(t) \leq \min_i R_i(t)$.
- **Time-Invariance Under Constant Rate**: The series system itself, when all components have exponential life distributions, **also follows an exponential distribution** with rate $\lambda_S$.
- **Order Insensitivity**: The product of reliabilities is **commutative** — physical placement in the RBD does not affect the result.
- **MTBF of Identical Series**: $\text{MTBF}_S = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}_{\text{comp}}}{n}$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why Is This Important in Practice?**

- **Aerospace**: Reliability engineers calculate the probability of mission success by multiplying the reliabilities of every critical subsystem (propulsion, avionics, structure, payload).
- **Manufacturing**: A production line with 10 stations in sequence, each with 99% uptime, has a line efficiency of $0.99^{10} \approx 0.904$ (90.4%) — known as the **Six-Nines Problem** in industry.
- **Quality Engineering**: Used in **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)** to identify the weakest component in a chain and prioritize reliability improvement budgets.
- **Telecommunications & IT**: Series reliability models the **end-to-end uptime** of dependent services in a workflow (e.g., API → service → database).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation — Series System Reliability

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Definition of Series System

A system of *n* components is in **series** (in the reliability sense) if the system operates successfully **only when all components operate successfully**. The reliability function of each component *i* is:

$$R_i(t) = P(T_i > t) = \int_{t}^{\infty} f_i(\tau)\, d\tau$$

### 3.1.2 Applying Statistical Independence

Because the failure times $T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n$ are statistically independent random variables, the joint probability factorizes:

$$P(T_1 > t \cap T_2 > t \cap \cdots \cap T_n > t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(T_i > t)$$

### 3.1.3 Combining with Series Logic

By the series definition, the system survives time *t* if and only if *all* components survive time *t*. Therefore:

$$R_S(t) = P(T_S > t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)$$

**This is the master equation for series system reliability.** It is the **first formula** every KTU student must memorize.

### 3.1.4 Specialization to Exponential Distribution

The exponential reliability function for component *i* with constant failure rate $\lambda_i$ is:

$$R_i(t) = e^{-\lambda_i t}$$

Substituting into the master equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_S(t) &= \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{-\lambda_i t} \\
       &= e^{-\lambda_1 t} \cdot e^{-\lambda_2 t} \cdots e^{-\lambda_n t} \\
       &= e^{-(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \cdots + \lambda_n)\, t}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.5 Identifying the Effective Failure Rate

Comparing with the standard exponential form $R_S(t) = e^{-\lambda_S t}$:

$$\lambda_S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$$

**Key insight:** For exponential components, the system's failure rate is the **arithmetic sum** of the component failure rates.

### 3.1.6 Deriving MTBF of the Series System

The mean life of an exponentially distributed random variable with rate $\lambda_S$ is the reciprocal of the rate:

$$\text{MTBF}_S = \int_0^{\infty} R_S(t)\, dt = \int_0^{\infty} e^{-\lambda_S t}\, dt = \frac{1}{\lambda_S}$$

Substituting $\lambda_S$:

$$\text{MTBF}_S = \frac{1}{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \cdots + \lambda_n}$$

### 3.1.7 Special Case — n Identical Components

If all *n* components are identical with reliability $R(t)$ and constant rate $\lambda$:

$$R_S(t) = [R(t)]^n = e^{-n\lambda t}, \qquad \text{MTBF}_S = \frac{\text{MTBF}_{\text{single}}}{n} = \frac{1}{n\lambda}$$

### 3.1.8 Unreliability (CDF) of the Series System

Using the complement rule $F_S(t) = 1 - R_S(t)$:

$$F_S(t) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{-\lambda_i t}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Demonstrations

### Worked Example 1 — Three Non-Identical Components

> **Problem:** A system has three components in series with reliabilities over 1000 hours: $R_1 = 0.95$, $R_2 = 0.92$, $R_3 = 0.98$. Find (a) system reliability at 1000 hours, (b) system unreliability, (c) the probability that **exactly component 2 fails** while 1 and 3 survive (assuming independence).

**Solution:**

**(a) System Reliability:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_S &= R_1 \times R_2 \times R_3 \\
    &= 0.95 \times 0.92 \times 0.98 \\
    &= 0.85628
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) System Unreliability:**

$$F_S = 1 - R_S = 1 - 0.85628 = 0.14372$$

**(c) Probability that only component 2 fails:**

The probability component 2 fails is $F_2 = 1 - R_2 = 0.08$. Components 1 and 3 must succeed:

$$P(\text{only 2 fails}) = R_1 \times F_2 \times R_3 = 0.95 \times 0.08 \times 0.98 = 0.07448$$

### Worked Example 2 — Exponential Series with MTBF

> **Problem:** Three components in series have constant failure rates $\lambda_1 = 0.002$/hr, $\lambda_2 = 0.005$/hr, $\lambda_3 = 0.001$/hr. Compute (a) system reliability at $t = 100$ hours, (b) MTBF of the system.

**Solution:**

**(a) System reliability at t = 100 hr:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_S &= \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \lambda_3 \\
          &= 0.002 + 0.005 + 0.001 \\
          &= 0.008 \text{ failures/hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_S(100) &= e^{-\lambda_S \times 100} \\
         &= e^{-0.008 \times 100} \\
         &= e^{-0.8} \\
         &\approx 0.4493
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) MTBF of the system:**

$$\text{MTBF}_S = \frac{1}{\lambda_S} = \frac{1}{0.008} = 125 \text{ hours}$$

### Worked Example 3 — Identical Components (Six-Nines Concept)

> **Problem:** A safety-critical system has 5 identical components in series, each with reliability $R = 0.999$ over the mission. Find the system reliability.

**Solution:**

$$R_S = R^5 = (0.999)^5 = 0.99501$$

So even with components at **"Three-Nines"** reliability (99.9%), chaining 5 in series drops system reliability to ~**99.5% (Two-and-a-Half Nines)**. This is the **"Reliability Multiplication Penalty"** in series design.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Each Formula

| Problem Type | Given | To Find | Formula to Apply |
|--------------|-------|---------|------------------|
| Direct series reliability | $R_i(t)$ values | $R_S(t)$ | $R_S = \prod R_i$ |
| Identical components | $R$, $n$ | $R_S$ | $R_S = R^n$ |
| Exponential, find $R_S(t)$ | $\lambda_i$, $t$ | $R_S(t)$ | $R_S = e^{-\sum \lambda_i \cdot t}$ |
| Find system MTBF | $\lambda_i$ (constant) | $\text{MTBF}_S$ | $\text{MTBF}_S = 1/\sum \lambda_i$ |
| Find effective failure rate | $\lambda_i$ | $\lambda_S$ | $\lambda_S = \sum \lambda_i$ |
| Find system unreliability | $R_S$ | $F_S$ | $F_S = 1 - R_S$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Generic Series RBD

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **logical flow** of a series RBD with 4 components. A signal/power must pass through **every block** sequentially for the system output to be delivered.

```mermaid
graph LR
    input[System Input] --> block1[Block 1: Component 1]
    block1 --> block2[Block 2: Component 2]
    block2 --> block3[Block 3: Component 3]
    block3 --> block4[Block 4: Component 4]
    block4 --> output[System Output]

    input -.->|R sub 1| block1
    block1 -.->|R sub 2| block2
    block2 -.->|R sub 3| block3
    block3 -.->|R sub 4| block4

    classDef compStyle fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A56DB,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef ioStyle fill:#D1FAE5,stroke:#059669,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    class input,output ioStyle
    class block1,block2,block3,block4 compStyle
```

**Interpretation:** System success requires that the path from **System Input → System Output** remains intact through all 4 blocks. Failure of **any single block** breaks the entire path → **system failure**.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture Flow (n Components)

This second diagram shows the **generalized functional flow** of an n-component series system, emphasizing the reliability dependence structure.

```mermaid
graph TB
    startNode([Mission Start t equals 0]) --> comp1Node
    comp1Node[Component 1: R1 of t] -->|AND| comp2Node[Component 2: R2 of t]
    comp2Node -->|AND| comp3Node[Component 3: R3 of t]
    comp3Node -->|AND| compNNode[Component N: RN of t]
    compNNode --> successNode([System Operational: RS of t])
    comp1Node -.->|F1 = 1 minus R1| failNode1
    comp2Node -.->|F2 = 1 minus R2| failNode2
    comp3Node -.->|F3 = 1 minus R3| failNode3
    compNNode -.->|FN = 1 minus RN| failNodeN
    failNode1 --> systemDown([System FAILED])
    failNode2 --> systemDown
    failNode3 --> systemDown
    failNodeN --> systemDown

    classDef okStyle fill:#D1FAE5,stroke:#059669,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef failStyle fill:#FEE2E2,stroke:#DC2626,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef compStyle fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1D4ED8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    class startNode,successNode okStyle
    class failNode1,failNode2,failNode3,failNodeN,systemDown failStyle
    class comp1Node,comp2Node,comp3Node,compNNode compStyle
```

**Architectural Logic:**

- The **AND-gate chain** (between consecutive components) reflects series logic: ALL must succeed.
- The **red dashed branches** show the failure paths — any single failure routes to the **System FAILED** terminal state.
- This is the **logical equivalent** of the Boolean expression: $S = S_1 \cdot S_2 \cdot S_3 \cdots S_n$.

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Reliability Decay Curve (Series vs Single)

```mermaid
graph LR
    axisX[Time t in hours] --> origin[Origin]
    origin --> axisY[Reliability R of t]
    singleCurve[Single Component Curve: R = e to power negative lambda t] -.->|Decreases| singleEnd[Lower Reliability Floor]
    seriesCurve[Series of N Components: RS = e to power negative N lambda t] -.->|Decreases FASTER| seriesEnd[Much Lower Reliability Floor]
    seriesCurve -->|Penalty| gap[Reliability Gap Widens with Time]

    classDef curveStyle fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#D97706,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef penaltyStyle fill:#FEE2E2,stroke:#DC2626,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    class singleCurve,seriesCurve curveStyle
    class seriesEnd,gap,penaltyStyle penaltyStyle
```

**Reading the Diagram:** The series reliability curve **decays faster** than the single-component curve — a visual representation of the **"Reliability Multiplication Penalty"**. The longer the mission time, the greater the gap.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Q1, 3 Marks]**
> **Q: Define a Reliability Block Diagram. How is a series configuration represented and what does it signify?**

**Model Answer (3 marks breakdown):**

- **1 mark** — Definition: An RBD is a logical block diagram showing how component reliabilities combine to deliver system reliability.
- **1 mark** — Series representation: Components are drawn as blocks connected in a single line (in series); system succeeds only when all blocks succeed.
- **1 mark** — Significance: $R_S(t) = \prod R_i(t)$; weakest-link principle; used to compute mission success probability for non-redundant systems.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Q2, 3 Marks]**
> **Q: A system consists of 4 components in series, each with reliability 0.95. Compute the system reliability and MTBF if each component has constant failure rate.**

**Model Answer:**

- $R_S = (0.95)^4 = 0.8145$ (1.5 marks)
- $\lambda = -\ln(0.95) = 0.0513$ /hr → MTBF = $1/\lambda = 19.49$ hr per component.
- $\text{MTBF}_S = \text{MTBF}/n = 19.49/4 = 4.87$ hr. (1.5 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In series RBD numericals, students **commonly lose 2-3 marks** for:
> 1. Forgetting to **convert units consistently** (e.g., failure rates in /hr but time given in minutes).
> 2. Applying $R_S = R^n$ when components are **non-identical** — that formula is for identical blocks only.
> 3. Not stating the **assumptions of independence and exponential distribution** explicitly — KTU examiners award marks for this.
> 4. Skipping the **final numerical verification** (plugging answer back into the formula).
> 5. Writing the **MTBF formula** as $1/\lambda$ without justifying the constant-rate assumption.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — Series RBD with Exponential Components

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted, 14 Marks]**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the reliability of a series system consisting of *n* independent components. State all assumptions clearly.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A system has 3 components in series with constant failure rates $\lambda_1 = 0.001$/hr, $\lambda_2 = 0.002$/hr, $\lambda_3 = 0.0015$/hr. Calculate (i) the system reliability at $t = 200$ hours, (ii) the MTBF of the system, and (iii) the probability that the system will fail in the first 200 hours.

### ✅ Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Components are statistically independent.
2. Each component is two-state (working or failed).
3. System is in series logic: ALL components must work for system success.

**Derivation (6 marks):**

The reliability of component $i$ at time $t$ is $R_i(t) = P(T_i > t)$. The system reliability is the probability that *all* components survive beyond time $t$:

$$R_S(t) = P(T_1 > t \cap T_2 > t \cap \cdots \cap T_n > t)$$

By statistical independence, the joint probability factorizes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_S(t) &= \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(T_i > t) \\
       &= \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

For exponential distribution with rate $\lambda_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_S(t) &= \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{-\lambda_i t} = e^{-(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \cdots + \lambda_n)t} = e^{-\lambda_S t}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\lambda_S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$. Hence:

$$R_S(t) = e^{-\lambda_S t}$$

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Step 1: Effective system failure rate (1 mark):**

$$\lambda_S = 0.001 + 0.002 + 0.0015 = 0.0045 \text{ failures/hr}$$

**Step 2: System reliability at t = 200 hr (2 marks):**

$$R_S(200) = e^{-0.0045 \times 200} = e^{-0.9} = 0.4066$$

**Step 3: System MTBF (2 marks):**

$$\text{MTBF}_S = \frac{1}{\lambda_S} = \frac{1}{0.0045} = 222.22 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 4: Probability of failure in first 200 hours (2 marks):**

$$F_S(200) = 1 - R_S(200) = 1 - 0.4066 = 0.5934$$

**Final Answer:** $R_S(200) = 0.4066$, MTBF = **222.22 hr**, $F_S(200) = 0.5934$.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Identical Series Components (Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted, 14 Marks]**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the "weakest-link" principle in series systems. Show mathematically that $R_S(t) \leq \min_i R_i(t)$.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A safety system in a nuclear plant has 5 identical sensors connected in series. Each sensor has an exponential failure distribution with MTBF of 10,000 hours. Compute (i) the failure rate of each sensor, (ii) the system MTBF, (iii) the probability of system survival for a mission time of 2,000 hours, and (iv) the unreliability over the same mission.

### ✅ Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Weakest Link (7 marks)

**Explanation (3 marks):** In a series RBD, the system is only as reliable as its weakest (least reliable) component. Any single component failure brings the system down, just as a single broken link breaks the chain. This is the **weakest-link principle** of series configurations.

**Mathematical Proof (4 marks):**

Since each $R_i(t) \leq 1$, for any specific index $k$:

$$R_k(t) \leq 1 \implies R_k(t) \cdot R_j(t) \leq R_j(t) \quad \text{for all } j \neq k$$

Therefore, multiplying across all *n* components:

$$R_S(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t) = R_k(t) \cdot \prod_{i \neq k} R_i(t) \leq R_k(t)$$

Since this holds for *any* $k$:

$$R_S(t) \leq \min_{i} R_i(t)$$

#### Part (b) — Identical Sensor Array (7 marks)

**Step 1: Failure rate per sensor (1 mark):**

$$\lambda = \frac{1}{\text{MTBF}} = \frac{1}{10{,}000} = 10^{-4} \text{ failures/hr}$$

**Step 2: System MTBF (2 marks):**

$$\text{MTBF}_S = \frac{\text{MTBF}_{\text{comp}}}{n} = \frac{10{,}000}{5} = 2{,}000 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 3: System reliability at t = 2000 hr (2 marks):**

$$\lambda_S = n \cdot \lambda = 5 \times 10^{-4} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ failures/hr}$$

$$R_S(2000) = e^{-5 \times 10^{-4} \times 2000} = e^{-1} = 0.3679$$

**Step 4: System unreliability (2 marks):**

$$F_S(2000) = 1 - R_S(2000) = 1 - 0.3679 = 0.6321$$

**Final Answer:** $\lambda = 10^{-4}$/hr, MTBF_S = **2,000 hr**, $R_S(2000) = 0.3679$, $F_S(2000) = 0.6321$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Series RBDs (Last-Minure KTU Revision)**

- ✅ **Series definition**: System works **only if all** components work — the **AND-gate** of reliability.
- ✅ **Master formula**: $R_S(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)$ — the **product rule** for series reliability.
- ✅ **Identical shortcut**: $R_S = R^n$ — applies **only when all components have the same reliability**.
- ✅ **Exponential specialization**: $\lambda_S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$ — failure rates **add** in series.
- ✅ **MTBF formula**: $\text{MTBF}_S = 1/\lambda_S = 1/(\sum \lambda_i)$.
- ✅ **Identical MTBF**: $\text{MTBF}_S = \text{MTBF}_{\text{comp}} / n$.
- ✅ **Weakest-link principle**: $R_S(t) \leq \min_i R_i(t)$ — always state in definitions.
- ✅ **Unreliability complement**: $F_S(t) = 1 - R_S(t)$.
- ✅ **Independence assumption**: Always declare — KTU examiners reward it.
- ✅ **Order doesn't matter**: The product is commutative; rearranging blocks in the RBD doesn't change $R_S$.
- ✅ **Series systems are "fragile"**: Adding more components in series **reduces** reliability — drive for redundancy (parallel) is the natural next step (next sub-topic in Module 3).
- ✅ **Unit consistency**: Convert all time units to a common base (hours, minutes) before plugging into formulas.
- ✅ **KTU typical numbers**: Failure rates are usually given in $10^{-3}$, $10^{-4}$, $10^{-5}$ /hr; times in hundreds or thousands of hours; expect $R_S$ values between 0.2 and 0.95.
- ✅ **Examiner's favorite mistake to catch**: Forgetting the constant-rate (exponential) assumption when applying $\text{MTBF} = 1/\lambda$ — for non-exponential distributions, MTBF must be computed via $\int_0^\infty R(t)\,dt$.

> **End of Notes — Series Reliability Block Diagrams, Module 3, PEBRT754 (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Components Connected in Parallel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT754 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Components Connected in Parallel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3 — COMPONENTS CONNECTED IN PARALLEL

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the formalism of **Reliability Engineering**, two or more components are said to be connected in **Parallel Configuration** when the overall system performs its intended function provided **at least one** of the parallel branches remains in the operational (success) state. Mathematically, a parallel system is a logical **OR-gate** structure where the system success event $S$ is the union of individual component success events $A_i$.

$$
S = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} A_i
$$

The system fails **only when all components fail simultaneously** — an event that is statistically far less probable than the failure of any single component. This makes parallel configuration the foundation of **hardware redundancy** in safety-critical engineering systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Parallel reliability modeling forms the analytical core of redundancy theory. Students must master the algebraic probability formulation, the binomial summation for $k$-out-of-$n$ systems, and the geometric interpretation of system survival probability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Nomenclature Convention (KTU Standard):**
> - $R_i$ → Reliability (probability of success) of the $i^{th}$ component
> - $Q_i$ → Unreliability (probability of failure) of the $i^{th}$ component, where $Q_i = 1 - R_i$
> - $R_s$ → System reliability
> - $Q_s$ → System unreliability
> - $\lambda$ → Constant failure rate (assumed for most derivations)

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The Car Headlight Problem

Imagine driving at night. Your car has **two headlights** — left and right. The lighting system works as long as **at least one** bulb is glowing. Even if one bulb burns out (fails), the driver can still see the road using the other bulb. The system fails only when **both bulbs are dead** simultaneously.

This is a textbook **2-out-of-2 parallel system** (also called a 1-out-of-2 system, since you need at least 1 working). Now extend this analogy: an aircraft with **four engines** can still fly safely on **three, two, or even one engine**. This is a **1-out-of-4 parallel configuration**, dramatically more reliable than any single engine alone.

The underlying principle is simple: **redundancy buys reliability**, but at the cost of additional weight, expense, and complexity.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Mean Time Between Failures | MTBF | Hours |
| Failure Rate | $\lambda$ | Failures per hour |
| Mission Time | $t$ | Hours |
| Availability | $A$ | Dimensionless ratio (0 to 1) |
| Reliability | $R(t)$ | Probability $\in [0, 1]$ |

For exponential distribution (the most common KTU assumption), $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ and $Q(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$.

## 1.4 Classification of Parallel Systems

KTU 2024 distinguishes the following parallel structures:

1. **Active (Hot) Parallel Redundancy** — All parallel units operate simultaneously from the start. No switching needed.
2. **Standby (Cold/Dormant) Redundancy** — Backup units are kept inactive and switched in only upon failure of the primary unit. Failure rate of dormant units is typically $\lambda_d \ll \lambda_{\text{active}}$.
3. **k-out-of-n Parallel System** — System functions if **at least k** of the n parallel components work. This is a generalization covering both 1-out-of-n and n-out-of-n cases.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pure Parallel vs Series — The Crucial Distinction:**
> In **series**, the system fails if *any one* component fails (weakest-link logic). In **parallel**, the system fails only if *all* components fail. The duality is fundamental to block diagram reduction.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS

## 2.1 Logic of Parallel Operation — Step-by-Step Reasoning

Let us dissect the operational logic of an $n$-component parallel system:

- **Step 1 — Component Modeling:** Each component $i$ has reliability $R_i$ and unreliability $Q_i = 1 - R_i$. The components are assumed **statistically independent** unless otherwise stated.

- **Step 2 — Failure Event Identification:** The system fails only when **every** component has failed. Using the probability axioms for independent events:
$$
Q_s = P(\text{all components fail}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Q_i = \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)
$$

- **Step 3 — Reliability Computation:** By the complement rule of probability:
$$
R_s = 1 - Q_s = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)
$$

- **Step 4 — Interpretation:** Note the elegant symmetry: **Series** reliability uses the *product of reliabilities*, while **Parallel** reliability uses *one minus the product of unreliabilities*. This duality permeates reliability block diagram analysis.

- **Step 5 — The Reliability Amplification Effect:** Since each $Q_i \in [0, 1]$, their product $\prod Q_i$ is smaller than any individual $Q_i$. Hence $R_s > R_i$ for all $i$. Parallel redundancy *always* improves system reliability when components are independent.

## 2.2 Special Case — n Identical Components in Parallel

When all $n$ parallel components share the same reliability $R$, the formula collapses to a beautiful compact form:

$$
R_s = 1 - (1 - R)^n
$$

This is the most commonly tested configuration in KTU examinations. The unreliability of the system is $(1-R)^n$, which decays exponentially with $n$ for a fixed $R$.

## 2.3 The k-out-of-n Parallel Subsystem

A generalized parallel model: a system of $n$ identical parallel components succeeds when **at least k** of them operate. The reliability is the sum of binomial probabilities from $k$ to $n$:

$$
R_s = \sum_{i=k}^{n} \binom{n}{i} R^i (1-R)^{n-i}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The classical 1-out-of-$n$ parallel system is a *special case* where $k=1$. The series system corresponds to $k=n$. This unified binomial formulation is a favorite of KTU examiners.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Parallel configuration is the design cornerstone of:
- **Aviation systems** (multiple engines, redundant flight computers)
- **Nuclear reactor safety** (parallel coolant pumps, redundant control rods)
- **Data centers** (parallel servers, redundant power supplies)
- **Spacecraft electronics** (parallel processing units for fault tolerance)
- **Power transmission grids** (parallel transmission lines to avoid blackout)

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Configuration | System Reliability $R_s$ | System Unreliability $Q_s$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $n$ identical components in parallel | $1 - (1-R)^n$ | $(1-R)^n$ |
| 2 | $n$ non-identical components in parallel | $1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1-R_i)$ | $\prod_{i=1}^{n}(1-R_i)$ |
| 3 | $k$-out-of-$n$ identical parallel | $\sum_{i=k}^{n} \binom{n}{i} R^i (1-R)^{n-i}$ | $\sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \binom{n}{i} R^i (1-R)^{n-i}$ |
| 4 | Two components in parallel | $R_1 + R_2 - R_1 R_2$ | $(1-R_1)(1-R_2)$ |
| 5 | Three identical components in parallel | $3R^2 - 2R^3$ | $(1-R)^3$ |
| 6 | Exponential $R(t)$ for 2 parallel units | $2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t}$ | $(1-e^{-\lambda t})^2$ |
| 7 | Two parallel, unequal failure rates | $e^{-\lambda_1 t} + e^{-\lambda_2 t} - e^{-(\lambda_1+\lambda_2)t}$ | $(1-e^{-\lambda_1 t})(1-e^{-\lambda_2 t})$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Notation Note:** In the table above, vertical bars were deliberately avoided inside table cells. When writing $\vert x \vert$ or conditional expressions inline, always use the LaTeX command $\vert$ or $\mid$ to prevent markdown table corruption.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# ✏️ STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — n Identical Components in Parallel

We start from the first principles of probability. Let $A_i$ denote the event that the $i^{th}$ component is functioning, and $S$ denote the system success event.

**Step 1 — Express System Success as Union:**

The system succeeds if at least one component works. This is the union of all individual success events:

$$
S = A_1 \cup A_2 \cup A_3 \cup \cdots \cup A_n
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the De Morgan's Law:**

The complement of union is the intersection of complements:

$$
\bar{S} = \overline{A_1 \cup A_2 \cup \cdots \cup A_n} = \bar{A}_1 \cap \bar{A}_2 \cap \cdots \cap \bar{A}_n
$$

This means the system fails only when *all* components have failed.

**Step 3 — Apply Statistical Independence:**

Since the components fail independently:

$$
P(\bar{S}) = P(\bar{A}_1) \cdot P(\bar{A}_2) \cdots P(\bar{A}_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Q_i
$$

**Step 4 — Specialize to Identical Components:**

If all components have the same reliability $R$, then $Q_i = 1 - R$ for every $i$:

$$
Q_s = (1-R)^n
$$

**Step 5 — Compute System Reliability via Complement Rule:**

$$
R_s = 1 - Q_s = 1 - (1-R)^n
$$

This completes the rigorous derivation. ∎

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — k-out-of-n Parallel Subsystem (Binomial Expansion)

**Step 1 — Setup the Binomial Distribution:**

Each component either works (with probability $R$) or fails (with probability $1-R$). For $n$ independent identical components, the number of working components follows a Binomial distribution $B(n, R)$.

**Step 2 — Probability of Exactly $i$ Working Components:**

$$
P(X = i) = \binom{n}{i} R^i (1-R)^{n-i}
$$

**Step 3 — Define the k-out-of-n System:**

System succeeds when $X \geq k$, i.e., when exactly $k$, or $k+1$, or $\cdots$, or $n$ components work:

$$
R_s = P(X \geq k) = \sum_{i=k}^{n} \binom{n}{i} R^i (1-R)^{n-i}
$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent Form Using Complement:**

$$
R_s = 1 - P(X < k) = 1 - \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \binom{n}{i} R^i (1-R)^{n-i}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification for Boundary Cases:**

- If $k = n$: $R_s = R^n$ (series configuration — recovered correctly)
- If $k = 1$: $R_s = 1 - (1-R)^n$ (pure parallel — recovered correctly)

This confirms the unified binomial formulation. ∎

---

## 3.3 Solved Numerical Problem — Two Dissimilar Components in Parallel

**Problem Statement (KTU Style):** A system consists of two components in parallel. Component A has reliability $R_A = 0.9$ and Component B has reliability $R_B = 0.8$. Assuming the failures are independent, compute the system reliability and unreliability. Verify that parallel redundancy improves the system reliability above either individual component.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

Step 1 — Identify the parallel configuration and component reliabilities:

$$
R_A = 0.9, \quad R_B = 0.8
$$

Step 2 — Compute individual unreliabilities:

$$
Q_A = 1 - R_A = 1 - 0.9 = 0.1
$$

$$
Q_B = 1 - R_B = 1 - 0.8 = 0.2
$$

Step 3 — Apply the parallel system unreliability formula (system fails only when both fail):

$$
Q_s = Q_A \cdot Q_B = 0.1 \times 0.2 = 0.02
$$

Step 4 — Compute system reliability via complement:

$$
R_s = 1 - Q_s = 1 - 0.02 = 0.98
$$

Step 5 — Verification using the additive parallel formula:

$$
R_s = R_A + R_B - R_A \cdot R_B = 0.9 + 0.8 - (0.9)(0.8)
$$

$$
R_s = 1.7 - 0.72 = 0.98
$$

Step 6 — Compare: $R_s = 0.98 > R_A = 0.9 > R_B = 0.8$. The system is more reliable than its best component — confirming the redundancy benefit. ∎

---

## 3.4 Solved Numerical Problem — Three Identical Components in Parallel (Exponential)

**Problem Statement:** Three identical components are connected in parallel. Each component has a constant failure rate $\lambda = 0.001$ failures/hour. Find the system reliability for a mission time of $t = 100$ hours.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute the individual component reliability using the exponential model:

$$
R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-0.001 \times 100} = e^{-0.1}
$$

Step 2 — Evaluate numerically:

$$
R(t) = e^{-0.1} \approx 0.9048
$$

Step 3 — Apply the three-identical-parallel reliability formula:

$$
R_s = 1 - (1 - R)^3 = 1 - (1 - 0.9048)^3
$$

Step 4 — Compute the unreliability of a single component:

$$
1 - R = 1 - 0.9048 = 0.0952
$$

Step 5 — Cube the unreliability:

$$
(0.0952)^3 = 0.000863
$$

Step 6 — Compute system reliability:

$$
R_s = 1 - 0.000863 \approx 0.9991
$$

Step 7 — Interpretation: A single component has reliability $\approx 0.905$ at $t = 100$ hours, while the 3-component parallel system achieves $\approx 0.999$. The improvement is **9.94 percentage points** in reliability. ∎

---

## 3.5 Solved Numerical Problem — 2-out-of-3 Subsystem

**Problem Statement:** A computer server rack has 3 identical processors. The system works as long as at least 2 processors function (a 2-out-of-3 configuration). If each processor has reliability $R = 0.95$, compute the system reliability.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

Step 1 — Identify the configuration: $k = 2$, $n = 3$.

Step 2 — Write the binomial summation:

$$
R_s = \sum_{i=2}^{3} \binom{3}{i} R^i (1-R)^{3-i}
$$

Step 3 — Expand the summation:

$$
R_s = \binom{3}{2} R^2 (1-R)^1 + \binom{3}{3} R^3 (1-R)^0
$$

Step 4 — Substitute $\binom{3}{2} = 3$ and $\binom{3}{3} = 1$:

$$
R_s = 3 R^2 (1-R) + R^3
$$

Step 5 — Plug in $R = 0.95$:

$$
R_s = 3 (0.95)^2 (0.05) + (0.95)^3
$$

Step 6 — Compute intermediate values:

$$
(0.95)^2 = 0.9025, \quad (0.95)^3 = 0.857375
$$

Step 7 — Final calculation:

$$
R_s = 3 \times 0.9025 \times 0.05 + 0.857375
$$

$$
R_s = 0.135375 + 0.857375 = 0.99275
$$

Step 8 — Interpretation: The 2-out-of-3 system achieves $R_s \approx 0.993$, far better than a single processor ($0.95$) and even better than a 1-out-of-2 system ($1 - 0.05^2 = 0.9975$). Wait — actually compare carefully: 1-out-of-2 gives $1 - (0.05)^2 = 0.9975$, which is *higher* because it requires only 1 working component. The 2-out-of-3 system is a *tighter* requirement, hence lower reliability. ∎

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** Students often confuse $k$-out-of-$n$ as always being more reliable. Higher $k$ demands *more* working components, hence the system reliability **decreases** as $k$ increases (for fixed $n$ and $R$).

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Reliability Table

The following table provides numerical values for $R_s$ as a function of component reliability $R$ for various parallel configurations, useful for exam preparation.

| $R$ (Component) | 2-Parallel | 3-Parallel | 4-Parallel | 2-out-of-3 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.50 | 0.7500 | 0.8750 | 0.9375 | 0.5000 |
| 0.70 | 0.9100 | 0.9730 | 0.9919 | 0.7840 |
| 0.80 | 0.9600 | 0.9920 | 0.9984 | 0.8960 |
| 0.90 | 0.9900 | 0.9990 | 0.9999 | 0.9720 |
| 0.95 | 0.9975 | 0.9999 | 0.99999 | 0.9928 |
| 0.99 | 0.9999 | 0.999999 | $\approx 1$ | 0.9997 |

Notice how the marginal benefit of additional parallel units *diminishes* as $n$ grows. The jump from 1 to 2 parallel units is dramatic; the jump from 3 to 4 is incremental.

## 4.1 Reliability Block Diagram — n Components in Parallel

The standard KTU block diagram representation for parallel configuration:

```mermaid
graph LR
    inputA[Input Source] --> compA1[Component 1]
    compA1 --> junctionA[Output Junction]
    inputB[Input Source] --> compA2[Component 2]
    compA2 --> junctionA
    inputC[Input Source] --> compA3[Component 3]
    compA3 --> junctionA
    junctionA --> outputNode[System Output]
    
    style inputA fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b
    style inputB fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b
    style inputC fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b
    style compA1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style compA2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style compA3 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style junctionA fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style outputNode fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The input signal (or load) is distributed across all parallel branches. As long as at least one branch delivers the output to the junction, the system functions. The junction represents a logical **OR** operation.

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow — Parallel Reliability Computation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([System Initialization]) --> dataIn[Input Component Reliabilities R1, R2, ..., Rn]
    dataIn --> checkIdentical{All Components Identical?}
    
    checkIdentical -- Yes --> identicalPath[Apply R_s = 1 - 1-R to power n]
    checkIdentical -- No --> nonIdenticalPath[Apply R_s = 1 - product of 1 minus Ri]
    
    identicalPath --> exponentialCheck{Exponential Distribution?}
    exponentialPath[Substitute R = e to power -lambda t] --> combineResults
    nonIdenticalPath --> combineResults[Compute Numerical R_s]
    exponentialCheck -- Yes --> exponentialPath
    exponentialCheck -- No --> combineResults
    
    combineResults --> koutCheck{k-out-of-n System?}
    koutCheck -- Yes --> binomialCalc[Apply Binomial Summation from i=k to n]
    koutCheck -- No --> finalResult[Output R_s]
    binomialCalc --> finalResult
    
    finalResult --> validateStep{R_s greater than max Ri?}
    validateStep -- Yes --> successNode([Reliability Improved])
    validateStep -- No --> warningNode([Check Independence Assumption])
    
    style startNode fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b
    style successNode fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20
    style warningNode fill:#ffab91,stroke:#bf360c
    style finalResult fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17
```

This diagram visualizes the sample space of 3 parallel components, each either working (W) or failing (F). The system fails only at the single leaf where all three fail.

```mermaid
graph TD

    rootNode --> branch2[Component 1: F]

    branch2 --> b2f3[Component 2: F]

    b2w3 --> b2w3f3[Comp 3: F — System OK]

```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visual Proof of Parallel Reliability:** Out of $2^3 = 8$ equally likely outcomes, only **1** outcome (all components fail) causes system failure. Hence the system unreliability is $1/8 = (1/2)^3$ — exactly the formula $(1-R)^n$ with $R = 1/2$.

## 4.4 Comparative Architecture — Series vs Parallel

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph seriesBlock[Series Configuration]
        sIn[Input] --> sC1[Component 1]
        sC1 --> sC2[Component 2]
        sC2 --> sC3[Component 3]
        sC3 --> sOut[Output]
    end
    
    subgraph parallelBlock[Parallel Configuration]
        pIn[Input] --> pC1[Component 1]
        pIn --> pC2[Component 2]
        pIn --> pC3[Component 3]
        pC1 --> pJ[OR Junction]
        pC2 --> pJ
        pC3 --> pJ
        pJ --> pOut[Output]
    end
    
    style seriesBlock fill:#ffebee,stroke:#b71c1c
    style parallelBlock fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style sC1 fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#b71c1c
    style sC2 fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#b71c1c
    style sC3 fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#b71c1c
    style pC1 fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20
    style pC2 fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20
    style pC3 fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Structural Contrast:** In the series diagram, the components form a single chain — a break anywhere breaks the chain. In the parallel diagram, multiple independent paths exist — only when all paths are broken does the system fail. This visual duality is the heart of reliability block diagram algebra.

# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK

---

## 📌 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define a parallel system in reliability engineering. Why is parallel configuration also called a redundancy configuration?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **parallel system** is a configuration in which the system performs its intended function as long as **at least one** of the parallel components is operational. The system fails only when all the parallel components fail simultaneously.

Mathematically, the system reliability for $n$ independent parallel components is:

$$
R_s = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)
$$

It is called a **redundancy configuration** because the additional parallel components beyond the first are *redundant* — they are not strictly required for operation but provide backup in case the primary component fails. The presence of redundancy increases the overall system reliability above the reliability of any single component. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: A system has two components in parallel with reliabilities 0.9 and 0.95. Calculate the system reliability assuming independent failures.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Given: $R_1 = 0.9$, $R_2 = 0.95$

Individual unreliabilities:

$$
Q_1 = 1 - 0.9 = 0.1, \quad Q_2 = 1 - 0.95 = 0.05
$$

System unreliability (system fails when both fail):

$$
Q_s = Q_1 \times Q_2 = 0.1 \times 0.05 = 0.005
$$

System reliability:

$$
R_s = 1 - Q_s = 1 - 0.005 = 0.995
$$

The parallel system reliability is **0.995**, which exceeds both component reliabilities. **(3 marks)**

---

## 📌 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Choice Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

### **Question A(a) [7 Marks]:**

Derive the expression for the reliability of $n$ identical components connected in parallel. Explain how this formula reduces to a series configuration for the case $k = n$ in the $k$-out-of-$n$ framework.

### **Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the parallel system event:**

Consider $n$ identical components, each with reliability $R$ and unreliability $Q = 1 - R$, connected in parallel. Let $S$ be the event that the system functions.

**Step 2 — Express complement event using De Morgan's law:**

The system fails when all components fail:

$$
\bar{S} = \bar{A}_1 \cap \bar{A}_2 \cap \cdots \cap \bar{A}_n
$$

**Step 3 — Apply independence:**

$$
P(\bar{S}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(\bar{A}_i) = Q^n = (1-R)^n
$$

**Step 4 — Compute system reliability:**

$$
R_s = 1 - P(\bar{S}) = 1 - (1-R)^n \quad \text{...(final formula)}
$$

**Step 5 — Connection to $k$-out-of-$n$ framework:**

In the $k$-out-of-$n$ formulation, the system succeeds if at least $k$ components work:

$$
R_s = \sum_{i=k}^{n} \binom{n}{i} R^i (1-R)^{n-i}
$$

When $k = n$, the summation has only one term ($i = n$):

$$
R_s = \binom{n}{n} R^n (1-R)^0 = R^n
$$

This is the **series system formula**, confirming that series is a special case of the $k$-out-of-$n$ model with $k = n$. Conversely, setting $k = 1$ recovers the pure parallel formula. This unified framework elegantly bridges the two extreme configurations. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question A(b) [7 Marks]:**

A system has three components in parallel with reliabilities $R_1 = 0.90$, $R_2 = 0.85$, and $R_3 = 0.80$. Calculate (i) the system reliability, and (ii) the system unreliability. Comment on the effect of the weakest component.

### **Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Given values:**

$$
R_1 = 0.90, \quad R_2 = 0.85, \quad R_3 = 0.80
$$

**Step 2 — Compute individual unreliabilities:**

$$
Q_1 = 0.10, \quad Q_2 = 0.15, \quad Q_3 = 0.20
$$

**Step 3 — Compute system unreliability:**

The system fails only when all three components fail:

$$
Q_s = Q_1 \times Q_2 \times Q_3 = 0.10 \times 0.15 \times 0.20
$$

$$
Q_s = 0.003 = 0.3\%
$$

**Step 4 — Compute system reliability:**

$$
R_s = 1 - Q_s = 1 - 0.003 = 0.997
$$

**Step 5 — Comment on weakest component effect:**

The weakest component is Component 3 with $R_3 = 0.80$. If we remove Component 3 (i.e., use only two parallel components with $R_1$ and $R_2$):

$$
Q_{s,2} = 0.10 \times 0.15 = 0.015
$$

$$
R_{s,2} = 1 - 0.015 = 0.985
$$

The 3-parallel system gives $R_s = 0.997$ versus the 2-parallel $R_{s,2} = 0.985$ — an improvement of 1.2 percentage points. The weakest component still contributes meaningfully because its unreliability $0.20$ is the largest, but the product nature of parallel unreliability means the overall system remains highly reliable. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Choice Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

### **Question B(a) [7 Marks]:**

Explain the $k$-out-of-$n$ parallel configuration with a suitable example. A communication system has 4 parallel transmitters. The system works if at least 2 transmitters are functional. If each transmitter has a reliability of $0.9$, find the system reliability.

### **Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition of $k$-out-of-$n$ system:**

A $k$-out-of-$n$ system consists of $n$ parallel components, and the system functions successfully as long as **at least $k$** of the $n$ components are operating. The system fails if fewer than $k$ components work.

**Step 2 — Engineering example — Communication System:**

A satellite ground station has 4 redundant transmitters. To maintain communication with the satellite, at least 2 transmitters must be operational (so that signal diversity and redundancy are preserved). This is a **2-out-of-4** parallel configuration.

**Step 3 — Apply the binomial formula with $k = 2$, $n = 4$, $R = 0.9$:**

$$
R_s = \sum_{i=2}^{4} \binom{4}{i} (0.9)^i (0.1)^{4-i}
$$

**Step 4 — Expand the summation:**

$$
R_s = \binom{4}{2}(0.9)^2(0.1)^2 + \binom{4}{3}(0.9)^3(0.1)^1 + \binom{4}{4}(0.9)^4(0.1)^0
$$

**Step 5 — Compute each term:**

$$
\text{Term 1} = 6 \times 0.81 \times 0.01 = 6 \times 0.0081 = 0.0486
$$

$$
\text{Term 2} = 4 \times 0.729 \times 0.1 = 4 \times 0.0729 = 0.2916
$$

$$
\text{Term 3} = 1 \times 0.6561 \times 1 = 0.6561
$$

**Step 6 — Sum the terms:**

$$
R_s = 0.0486 + 0.2916 + 0.6561 = 0.9963
$$

The 2-out-of-4 communication system has reliability **$R_s = 0.9963$**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B(b) [7 Marks]:**

A system consists of two components A and B connected in parallel. Component A has a constant failure rate of $\lambda_A = 0.002$ per hour, and Component B has $\lambda_B = 0.005$ per hour. Calculate the system reliability for a mission time of $t = 100$ hours. Compare it with the reliability of each individual component.

### **Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute individual reliabilities at $t = 100$ hours:**

For Component A:

$$
R_A(100) = e^{-\lambda_A \cdot t} = e^{-0.002 \times 100} = e^{-0.2} \approx 0.8187
$$

For Component B:

$$
R_B(100) = e^{-\lambda_B \cdot t} = e^{-0.005 \times 100} = e^{-0.5} \approx 0.6065
$$

**Step 2 — Compute individual unreliabilities:**

$$
Q_A = 1 - 0.8187 = 0.1813
$$

$$
Q_B = 1 - 0.6065 = 0.3935
$$

**Step 3 — Compute system unreliability (parallel — fails only when both fail):**

$$
Q_s = Q_A \times Q_B = 0.1813 \times 0.3935 = 0.07134
$$

**Step 4 — Compute system reliability:**

$$
R_s = 1 - 0.07134 = 0.9287
$$

**Step 5 — Comparison:**

$$
R_A = 0.8187, \quad R_B = 0.6065, \quad R_s = 0.9287
$$

The parallel system reliability $R_s = 0.9287$ is **higher** than the better of the two components ($R_A = 0.8187$). The improvement is approximately **11 percentage points** over Component A and **32 percentage points** over Component B. This dramatic improvement demonstrates why parallel redundancy is heavily used in safety-critical systems such as aviation, nuclear power, and spacecraft. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Series and Parallel Formulas:** The most common error is applying the *series* formula $R_s = R_1 \times R_2 \times \cdots \times R_n$ to a parallel problem, or vice versa. Always check the system description carefully.
> 2. **Forgetting the Complement Rule:** Many students compute $Q_s$ correctly but forget the final $R_s = 1 - Q_s$ step, losing 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Ignoring Independence:** The formula $\prod Q_i$ requires statistical independence. If the problem states dependent failures, the conditional probability formula must be used instead.
> 4. **Sign Errors in $k$-out-of-$n$:** Students often include the $i < k$ terms by mistake. The summation must run from $i = k$ to $i = n$, *not* the reverse.
> 5. **No Units in Exponential Models:** When using $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, ensure that $\lambda$ and $t$ use *consistent units* (e.g., both in hours, or both in years).
> 6. **Skipping the Verification Step:** Always verify that $R_s > \max(R_i)$. If not, check for arithmetic errors or independence violations.

---

## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ **Definition:** A parallel system functions if **at least one** component works. The logical structure is an **OR-gate**.
- ✅ **Core Formula (General $n$ components):** $R_s = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$
- ✅ **Core Formula (Identical $n$ components):** $R_s = 1 - (1-R)^n$
- ✅ **Two-Component Formula:** $R_s = R_1 + R_2 - R_1 R_2$
- ✅ **$k$-out-of-$n$ Formula:** $R_s = \sum_{i=k}^{n} \binom{n}{i} R^i (1-R)^{n-i}$
- ✅ **Series Duality:** Series uses product of reliabilities; Parallel uses one minus product of unreliabilities.
- ✅ **Exponential Form:** For $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, the parallel $R_s(t) = 1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^n$.
- ✅ **Boundary Cases:** $k=1$ gives pure parallel; $k=n$ gives pure series.
- ✅ **Independence Assumption:** The product rule $\prod Q_i$ requires statistical independence among components.
- ✅ **Reliability Amplification:** Parallel configuration *always* improves $R_s$ above $\max(R_i)$ for independent components.
- ✅ **Diminishing Returns:** Each additional parallel unit provides a smaller marginal improvement in $R_s$.
- ✅ **Engineering Applications:** Aviation engines, nuclear safety, data centers, spacecraft, power grids.
- ✅ **Common Pitfall:** Never confuse system reliability with system unreliability — they are complementary events.
- ✅ **KTU Exam Pattern:** Expect derivations in Part A (3 marks) and numerical problems involving 2, 3, or 4 parallel components in Part B (14 marks with two 7-mark sub-parts).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mixed Configuration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT754 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mixed Configuration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mixed Configuration** in Reliability Engineering refers to a non-pure system architecture in which components are arranged using **both series and parallel topologies simultaneously**. A mixed system is one that cannot be reduced to a strictly series or strictly parallel configuration in a single reduction step, and therefore requires hierarchical (block-wise) reliability combination.

The two canonical forms studied under the KTU Module 3 syllabus are:

| S.No. | Configuration Type | Topology Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Series–Parallel (S-P) System** | Parallel sub-blocks connected in **series** |
| 2 | **Parallel–Series (P-S) System** | Series sub-blocks connected in **parallel** |
| 3 | **Complex (Non-Series Non-Parallel) System** | Requires cut-set / tie-set / decomposition methods |

Mathematically, the reliability of any binary coherent system composed of $n$ independent components can be expressed through the **structure function** $\phi(\mathbf{R})$:

$$R_{sys} = E[\phi(\mathbf{X})] = \phi(R_1, R_2, \dots, R_n)$$

where $R_i$ is the reliability of the $i^{th}$ component and $\mathbf{X} = (X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n)$ denotes the binary state vector (1 = working, 0 = failed).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy (Water Distribution Network):**
> Imagine a city's water supply network. Water flows from the main reservoir through **two sequential treatment plants in series** (sedimentation → filtration). However, each plant has **3 identical pumps in parallel** as backup. This is a **Series–Parallel** mixed system: parallel redundancy at each stage, but the stages themselves operate in series because water MUST pass through both.

> Another analogy: An industrial **PLC control system** has two independent redundant controllers (parallel branches). Each controller requires its **CPU, RAM module, and I/O card all working** (series inside each branch). This is a **Parallel–Series** mixed system.

## 1.3 Key Metrics in Mixed Configurations

- **System Reliability $R_{sys}$** — Probability that the system performs its intended function over the mission time $t$.
- **System Unreliability $Q_{sys} = 1 - R_{sys}$**
- **System Failure Rate $\lambda_{sys}$** — Not simply additive or reciprocal in mixed systems.
- **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** — Expected operating time before first failure.
- **Mission Time $t$** — The duration over which reliability is evaluated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Independent Components Assumption:** All derivations in this module assume components are **statistically independent**, with constant (time-invariant) failure rates following the **exponential distribution** unless stated otherwise.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Reduction Logic

### Step 1 — Identify the *innermost* block
Locate the smallest sub-block that is purely series or purely parallel. Compute its equivalent reliability using the appropriate base rule.

### Step 2 — Replace the block with an equivalent component
Substitute the computed equivalent reliability as the "reliability" of a new single virtual component. This step **collapses** the network by one level.

### Step 3 — Repeat until a single equivalent component remains
Iteratively reduce until the entire network reduces to a single reliability value $R_{sys}$.

### Why this works
Boolean algebra of structure functions preserves the **coherent monotonic** property. If all components improve, the system reliability cannot decrease — enabling safe block-wise decomposition.

## 2.2 Base Formulas (Primitives)

For **series** connection of $n$ independent components:

$$R_{series}(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)$$

For **parallel** connection of $n$ independent components:

$$R_{parallel}(t) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - R_i(t))$$

## 2.3 Mixed Configuration Formulas

### A. Series–Parallel (S-P) System
A system with $k$ parallel sub-blocks connected in series. The $j^{th}$ sub-block contains $n_j$ components in parallel.

$$R_{SP}(t) = \prod_{j=1}^{k} \left[ 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n_j} (1 - R_{ij}(t)) \right]$$

### B. Parallel–Series (P-S) System
A system with $k$ series sub-blocks connected in parallel. The $j^{th}$ sub-block contains $n_j$ components in series.

$$R_{PS}(t) = 1 - \prod_{j=1}^{k} \left[ 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n_j} R_{ij}(t) \right]$$

### C. Constant Failure Rate (Exponential) Specialization
If $R_i(t) = e^{-\lambda_i t}$ for each component:

$$R_{series}(t) = \exp\!\left(-t \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i\right) \quad ; \quad \lambda_{sys} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$$

$$MTTF_{series} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i}$$

For **parallel** of two identical components ($\lambda$ each):

$$R_{parallel}(t) = 2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t}$$

$$MTTF_{parallel} = \frac{1}{\lambda} + \frac{1}{2\lambda} = \frac{3}{2\lambda}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Configuration | Reliability Expression | MTTF (Exponential, identical) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Pure Series ($n$) | $\prod R_i$ | $1 / \sum \lambda_i$ |
| Pure Parallel ($n$) | $1 - \prod(1 - R_i)$ | $\sum_{k=1}^{n} (-1)^{k+1} \binom{n}{k} \frac{1}{k\lambda}$ |
| Series–Parallel | $\prod_{j} \left[ 1 - \prod_{i} (1 - R_{ij}) \right]$ | Compute block-wise then series |
| Parallel–Series | $1 - \prod_{j} \left[ 1 - \prod_{i} R_{ij} \right]$ | Compute branch reliability then parallel |
| Decomposition (key component $k$) | $R = R_k \cdot R(\text{cond. on k up}) + (1 - R_k) \cdot R(\text{cond. on k down})$ | Depends on topology |

> [!NOTE]
> The **conditional probability / decomposition method** is the most general technique and is examinable in KTU ESE for complex (non-reducible) configurations.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Mixed configurations are ubiquitous in:

- **Aerospace systems** (redundant flight computers, each containing series-connected processors, in parallel banks)
- **Nuclear power plant safety systems** (parallel redundant coolant pumps with series valve arrangements)
- **Data center power distribution** (UPS units in parallel, each with series battery strings)
- **Automotive braking systems** (dual-circuit hydraulics with parallel wheel cylinders per circuit)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Series–Parallel Reliability

**System Description:** Consider a system with two stages in series. Stage 1 has components A and B in parallel. Stage 2 has components C and D in parallel.

**Step 1 — Compute reliability of Stage 1 (parallel block)**

$$R_{S1} = 1 - (1 - R_A)(1 - R_B) = R_A + R_B - R_A R_B$$

**Reasoning:** The parallel block functions if at least one of A or B is working. Probability of both failing is $(1-R_A)(1-R_B)$. Subtracting from 1 gives survival.

**Step 2 — Compute reliability of Stage 2 (parallel block)**

$$R_{S2} = 1 - (1 - R_C)(1 - R_D) = R_C + R_D - R_C R_D$$

**Step 3 — Combine stages in series**

The system functions only if **both** stages function (series connection requires all stages working).

$$R_{sys} = R_{S1} \times R_{S2}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and expand**

$$R_{sys} = (R_A + R_B - R_A R_B)(R_C + R_D - R_C R_D)$$

**Step 5 — Expand fully**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{sys} =\ & R_A R_C + R_A R_D + R_B R_C + R_B R_D \\
& - R_A R_C R_D - R_B R_C R_D - R_A R_B R_C - R_A R_B R_D \\
& + R_A R_B R_C R_D
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symmetry observation:** The expression contains all $2^4 = 16$ min-terms, but only the **working** ones appear. The expansion enumerates the **minimal path sets** of the system.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Numerical Worked Example

**Problem:** A mixed S-P system has Stage 1 with 2 parallel components ($R_A = 0.9$, $R_B = 0.8$) and Stage 2 with 3 parallel components ($R_C = 0.85$, $R_D = 0.75$, $R_E = 0.95$). Find the system reliability at $t = 100$ hours. Assume exponential distribution with $\lambda_A = 1\times 10^{-3}$ /hr, $\lambda_B = 2\times 10^{-3}$ /hr, $\lambda_C = 1.5\times 10^{-3}$ /hr, $\lambda_D = 2.5\times 10^{-3}$ /hr, $\lambda_E = 0.8\times 10^{-3}$ /hr.

### Step A — Compute component reliabilities at $t = 100$ hr

**Rule:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_A(100) &= e^{-0.001 \times 100} = e^{-0.1} = 0.9048 \\
R_B(100) &= e^{-0.002 \times 100} = e^{-0.2} = 0.8187 \\
R_C(100) &= e^{-0.0015 \times 100} = e^{-0.15} = 0.8607 \\
R_D(100) &= e^{-0.0025 \times 100} = e^{-0.25} = 0.7788 \\
R_E(100) &= e^{-0.0008 \times 100} = e^{-0.08} = 0.9231
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step B — Compute Stage 1 reliability (parallel: A, B)

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{S1} &= 1 - (1 - 0.9048)(1 - 0.8187) \\
&= 1 - (0.0952)(0.1813) \\
&= 1 - 0.01726 \\
&= 0.98274
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step C — Compute Stage 2 reliability (parallel: C, D, E)

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{S2} &= 1 - (1 - 0.8607)(1 - 0.7788)(1 - 0.9231) \\
&= 1 - (0.1393)(0.2212)(0.0769) \\
&= 1 - (0.1393 \times 0.2212 \times 0.0769) \\
&= 1 - (0.03081 \times 0.0769) \\
&= 1 - 0.002369 \\
&= 0.997631
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step D — Compute system reliability (series of stages)

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{sys} &= R_{S1} \times R_{S2} \\
&= 0.98274 \times 0.997631 \\
&= 0.98041
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step E — Compute MTTF of each stage (for parallel of $n$ components with constant $\lambda$)

**Stage 1 (2 parallel, different $\lambda$'s):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MTTF_{S1} &= \int_0^\infty R_{S1}(t)\, dt \\
&= \int_0^\infty \left[ 1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda_A t})(1 - e^{-\lambda_B t}) \right] dt \\
&= \int_0^\infty \left[ e^{-\lambda_A t} + e^{-\lambda_B t} - e^{-(\lambda_A + \lambda_B) t} \right] dt \\
&= \frac{1}{\lambda_A} + \frac{1}{\lambda_B} - \frac{1}{\lambda_A + \lambda_B} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.001} + \frac{1}{0.002} - \frac{1}{0.003} \\
&= 1000 + 500 - 333.33 \\
&= 1166.67 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Stage 2 (3 parallel with different $\lambda$'s):**

For general 3-component parallel:

$$
\begin{aligned}
MTTF_{S2} &= \frac{1}{\lambda_C} + \frac{1}{\lambda_D} + \frac{1}{\lambda_E} - \frac{1}{\lambda_C + \lambda_D} - \frac{1}{\lambda_C + \lambda_E} - \frac{1}{\lambda_D + \lambda_E} + \frac{1}{\lambda_C + \lambda_D + \lambda_E}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&= \frac{1}{0.0015} + \frac{1}{0.0025} + \frac{1}{0.0008} - \frac{1}{0.004} - \frac{1}{0.0023} - \frac{1}{0.0033} + \frac{1}{0.0048} \\
&= 666.67 + 400.00 + 1250.00 - 250.00 - 434.78 - 303.03 + 208.33 \\
&= 1537.19 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step F — System MTTF (series combination)

For a series of independent sub-systems, the system **fails when the first sub-system fails**. Using inclusion-exclusion over competing exponential-like sub-systems (approximation when each stage is treated as having an equivalent constant rate):

$$
\lambda_{sys} \approx \frac{1}{MTTF_{S1}} + \frac{1}{MTTF_{S2}} = \frac{1}{1166.67} + \frac{1}{1537.19} = 0.000857 + 0.000651 = 0.001508
$$

$$
MTTF_{sys} \approx \frac{1}{0.001508} \approx 663.13 \text{ hours}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This MTTF calculation is an **approximation** valid only when each stage's reliability is approximately exponential over the mission profile. For exact mixed MTTF, numerical integration of $R_{sys}(t)$ is required.

## 3.3 Decomposition Method (For Complex Systems)

For a complex bridge-like system, the **conditional probability method** decomposes along a chosen "key" component $k$:

$$
R_{sys} = R_k \cdot R_{sys \vert k \text{ works}} + (1 - R_k) \cdot R_{sys \vert k \text{ fails}}
$$

This reduces a non-reducible mixed network into two simpler sub-networks, which can be further reduced by the standard series/parallel rules.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Series–Parallel (S-P) Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    INPUT([System Input]) --> STAGE1[Stage 1]
    STAGE1 --> STAGE2[Stage 2]
    STAGE2 --> OUTPUT([System Output])

    STAGE1 --> P1A[Component A]
    STAGE1 --> P1B[Component B]
    P1A --- P1B

    STAGE2 --> P2A[Component C]
    STAGE2 --> P2B[Component D]
    STAGE2 --> P2C[Component E]
    P2A --- P2B
    P2B --- P2C
    P2A --- P2C
```

## 4.2 Parallel–Series (P-S) Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    IN([Source]) --> SPLIT{Junction}
    SPLIT --> BRANCH1[Branch 1: Series X-Y]
    SPLIT --> BRANCH2[Branch 2: Series P-Q-R]
    BRANCH1 --> MERGE{Junction}
    BRANCH2 --> MERGE
    MERGE --> OUT([Load])

    BRANCH1 --> X1[Component X]
    X1 --> Y1[Component Y]

    BRANCH2 --> P1[Component P]
    P1 --> Q1[Component Q]
    Q1 --> R1[Component R]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Mixed System Reduction)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Original Mixed System] --> B[Identify innermost block]
    B --> C{Is block purely series or parallel?}
    C -- YES --> D[Apply base formula]
    C -- NO --> E[Apply decomposition method]
    D --> F[Replace block with equivalent R]
    E --> G[Create two conditional sub-networks]
    G --> H[Apply base formula on each]
    H --> F
    F --> I{More than one block remains?}
    I -- YES --> B
    I -- NO --> J[Final R sys]
```

## 4.4 Reliability Block Diagram vs. Physical Layout — Key Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Warning:** In a **Reliability Block Diagram (RBD)**, two components drawn in **parallel** means either one provides the *function*, NOT that they are physically adjacent. **Series in RBD = both must work = sequential dependency of function**, which is **not** the same as physical series wiring. Always draw the RBD based on **functional flow**, not physical placement.

| RBD Connection | Functional Meaning | Boolean Relation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Series | Both A AND B must work | $R = R_A \cdot R_B$ |
| Parallel | Either A OR B works | $R = 1 - (1 - R_A)(1 - R_B)$ |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **PART A — 3 Mark Questions**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define mixed configuration in a reliability system. Differentiate between Series–Parallel and Parallel–Series configurations with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **mixed configuration** is a reliability system architecture where components are arranged using a combination of both series and parallel connections, and cannot be classified as purely series or purely parallel.

**Series–Parallel (S-P):** Sub-blocks of parallel components are connected in series. *Example:* Two parallel pumps in Stage 1 feeding into two parallel pumps in Stage 2 of a water treatment plant.

**Parallel–Series (P-S):** Sub-blocks of series components are connected in parallel. *Example:* Two independent parallel branches, where each branch has a CPU, RAM, and I/O card in series (redundant computers).

> **Marking:** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for S-P and P-S examples (with functional explanation).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State the formula for the reliability of a system consisting of $k$ parallel sub-blocks connected in series, where the $j^{th}$ sub-block contains $n_j$ components in parallel. Mention the assumption.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Assumption:** Components are statistically independent with constant failure rates.

$$R_{SP}(t) = \prod_{j=1}^{k} \left[ 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n_j} (1 - R_{ij}(t)) \right]$$

(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for explanation of each sub-block, 1 mark for assumption).

---

## **PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** A mixed Series–Parallel system has two stages in series. Stage 1 consists of two components A and B in parallel with reliabilities $R_A = 0.9$ and $R_B = 0.85$. Stage 2 consists of three components C, D, and E in parallel with reliabilities $R_C = 0.8$, $R_D = 0.9$, and $R_E = 0.75$. Compute:
  (i) The reliability of Stage 1 and Stage 2.  **(3 marks)**
  (ii) The overall system reliability.  **(2 marks)**
  (iii) The system unreliability.  **(2 marks)**

**(b)** If the system has constant failure rates $\lambda_A = 1\times 10^{-3}$/hr, $\lambda_B = 1.5\times 10^{-3}$/hr, $\lambda_C = 2\times 10^{-3}$/hr, $\lambda_D = 1.2\times 10^{-3}$/hr, and $\lambda_E = 0.8\times 10^{-3}$/hr, determine the MTTF of the overall system over a 1000-hour mission.  **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) (i) Stage 1 reliability (parallel of A, B):**

$$R_{S1} = 1 - (1 - 0.9)(1 - 0.85) = 1 - (0.1)(0.15) = 1 - 0.015 = 0.985$$

**(a) (i) Stage 2 reliability (parallel of C, D, E):**

$$R_{S2} = 1 - (1 - 0.8)(1 - 0.9)(1 - 0.75) = 1 - (0.2)(0.1)(0.25) = 1 - 0.005 = 0.995$$

**(a) (ii) System reliability (series of stages):**

$$R_{sys} = R_{S1} \times R_{S2} = 0.985 \times 0.995 = 0.980075 \approx 0.9801$$

**(a) (iii) System unreliability:**

$$Q_{sys} = 1 - R_{sys} = 1 - 0.9801 = 0.0199$$

**(b) MTTF calculation:**

**Step 1 — Compute component reliabilities at $t = 1000$ hr:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_A &= e^{-0.001 \times 1000} = e^{-1} = 0.3679 \\
R_B &= e^{-0.0015 \times 1000} = e^{-1.5} = 0.2231 \\
R_C &= e^{-0.002 \times 1000} = e^{-2} = 0.1353 \\
R_D &= e^{-0.0012 \times 1000} = e^{-1.2} = 0.3012 \\
R_E &= e^{-0.0008 \times 1000} = e^{-0.8} = 0.4493
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Stage 1 reliability at $t = 1000$:**

$$R_{S1} = 1 - (1 - 0.3679)(1 - 0.2231) = 1 - (0.6321)(0.7769) = 1 - 0.4911 = 0.5089$$

**Step 3 — Stage 2 reliability at $t = 1000$:**

$$R_{S2} = 1 - (0.8647)(0.6988)(0.5507) = 1 - 0.3327 = 0.6673$$

**Step 4 — System reliability at $t = 1000$:**

$$R_{sys}(1000) = 0.5089 \times 0.6673 = 0.3396$$

**Step 5 — MTTF of Stage 1 (parallel of A, B):**

$$MTTF_{S1} = \frac{1}{\lambda_A} + \frac{1}{\lambda_B} - \frac{1}{\lambda_A + \lambda_B} = 1000 + 666.67 - 400 = 1266.67 \text{ hr}$$

**Step 6 — MTTF of Stage 2 (parallel of C, D, E):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MTTF_{S2} &= \frac{1}{\lambda_C} + \frac{1}{\lambda_D} + \frac{1}{\lambda_E} - \frac{1}{\lambda_C+\lambda_D} - \frac{1}{\lambda_C+\lambda_E} - \frac{1}{\lambda_D+\lambda_E} + \frac{1}{\lambda_C+\lambda_D+\lambda_E} \\
&= 500 + 833.33 + 1250 - 312.5 - 357.14 - 500 + 220 \\
&= 1633.69 \text{ hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Approximate system MTTF (series of stages):**

$$\frac{1}{MTTF_{sys}} \approx \frac{1}{1266.67} + \frac{1}{1633.69} = 0.000789 + 0.000612 = 0.001401$$

$$MTTF_{sys} \approx \frac{1}{0.001401} \approx 713.78 \text{ hours}$$

> **Mark split:** 2 marks for sub-part (a), 7 marks for sub-part (b).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** Define the **decomposition method** for non-series non-parallel systems. Show how the system reliability is computed using the conditional probability approach.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the system shown in the figure (2 components A, B in series, this series combination in parallel with component C), compute the system reliability when $R_A = 0.9$, $R_B = 0.8$, and $R_C = 0.7$. If the mission time is $t = 500$ hr and failure rates are $\lambda_A = 1\times 10^{-3}$/hr, $\lambda_B = 2\times 10^{-3}$/hr, $\lambda_C = 1.5\times 10^{-3}$/hr, find the MTTF.  **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Decomposition Method — 7 marks:**

The **decomposition method** is applied to systems that cannot be reduced by simple series/parallel rules. A "key" component $k$ is selected, and the system is split into two simpler conditional configurations.

**Master formula:**

$$R_{sys} = R_k \cdot R_{sys}^{(k \text{ works})} + (1 - R_k) \cdot R_{sys}^{(k \text{ fails})}$$

**Procedure:**
1. Select a component $k$ that participates in both series and parallel paths.
2. **Case 1:** Assume $k$ works (short $k$). Reduce the remaining network using series/parallel rules to get $R_{sys}^{(k \text{ works})}$.
3. **Case 2:** Assume $k$ fails (remove $k$ or open the path). Reduce the remaining network to get $R_{sys}^{(k \text{ fails})}$.
4. Substitute into the master formula.

This converts a non-reducible mixed network into two sub-problems that are each reducible. (4 marks for full derivation, 3 marks for procedure.)

**(b) Parallel–Series system — 7 marks:**

**Topology:** Branch 1 = A series B; Branch 2 = C; both branches in parallel.

**Step 1 — Branch 1 reliability (series of A, B):**

$$R_{B1} = R_A \cdot R_B = 0.9 \times 0.8 = 0.72$$

**Step 2 — System reliability (parallel of Branch 1, Branch 2):**

$$R_{sys} = 1 - (1 - R_{B1})(1 - R_C) = 1 - (1 - 0.72)(1 - 0.7) = 1 - (0.28)(0.3) = 1 - 0.084 = 0.916$$

**Step 3 — Component reliabilities at $t = 500$ hr:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_A(500) &= e^{-0.001 \times 500} = e^{-0.5} = 0.6065 \\
R_B(500) &= e^{-0.002 \times 500} = e^{-1.0} = 0.3679 \\
R_C(500) &= e^{-0.0015 \times 500} = e^{-0.75} = 0.4724
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Branch 1 reliability at $t = 500$:**

$$R_{B1}(500) = 0.6065 \times 0.3679 = 0.2231$$

**Step 5 — System reliability at $t = 500$:**

$$R_{sys}(500) = 1 - (1 - 0.2231)(1 - 0.4724) = 1 - (0.7769)(0.5276) = 1 - 0.4099 = 0.5901$$

**Step 6 — MTTF calculation:**

**Branch 1 MTTF (series):**

$$MTTF_{B1} = \frac{1}{\lambda_A + \lambda_B} = \frac{1}{0.003} = 333.33 \text{ hr}$$

**Branch 2 MTTF (single component):**

$$MTTF_{B2} = \frac{1}{\lambda_C} = \frac{1}{0.0015} = 666.67 \text{ hr}$$

**System MTTF (parallel of two branches):**

$$MTTF_{sys} = MTTF_{B1} + MTTF_{B2} - \frac{MTTF_{B1} \cdot MTTF_{B2}}{MTTF_{B1} + MTTF_{B2}}$$

$$= 333.33 + 666.67 - \frac{333.33 \times 666.67}{333.33 + 666.67}$$

$$= 1000 - \frac{222222.22}{1000} = 1000 - 222.22 = 777.78 \text{ hours}$$

(3 marks for reliability at $t=0$, 2 marks for $t=500$ case, 2 marks for MTTF.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse physical wiring with RBD.** A component physically in series with another in a circuit is NOT always in series in the RBD. RBD follows *function*, not *wiring*. (**−2 marks** if confused)
> 2. **Do not apply $\lambda_{sys} = \sum \lambda_i$ for mixed systems directly.** This is valid only for *pure series* configurations. For mixed/parallel branches, the equivalent rate is NOT a simple sum.
> 3. **Do not forget the MTTF units.** KTU expects answers in hours (or as specified in the problem).
> 4. **Show intermediate $R_{S1}$, $R_{S2}$ values** before combining. Skipping directly to the final $R_{sys}$ loses method marks.
> 5. **For the series-parallel MTTF approximation**, KTU accepts the series-of-blocks approximation $\frac{1}{MTTF_{sys}} \approx \sum \frac{1}{MTTF_{block}}$ if explicitly stated as an approximation. Without the qualifier, the examiner will deduct 1 mark.

---

## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- **Mixed configuration** = hierarchical combination of series and parallel blocks.
- **S-P System:** Parallel sub-blocks → series. Formula: $\prod_j [1 - \prod_i (1 - R_{ij})]$
- **P-S System:** Series sub-blocks → parallel. Formula: $1 - \prod_j [1 - \prod_i R_{ij}]$
- **Hierarchical reduction rule:** Always collapse the **innermost** pure sub-block first, then re-evaluate.
- **Exponential specialization:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$; series: $\lambda_{sys} = \sum \lambda_i$; parallel: must integrate.
- **Pure series MTTF** = $1 / \sum \lambda_i$. **Two-identical-component parallel MTTF** = $\frac{3}{2\lambda}$.
- **Decomposition (conditional) method** is the universal technique for **non-reducible** mixed networks (e.g., bridge systems).
- **Cut-set** = set of components whose **simultaneous failure** causes system failure.
- **Tie-set (path-set)** = set of components whose **simultaneous success** guarantees system success.
- **Always assume component independence** unless the problem explicitly states dependence.
- **RBD ≠ physical layout** — draw RBD based on *functional flow*, not wiring.
- **Units check:** $\lambda$ in /hr, $t$ in hr, MTTF in hr, $R$ dimensionless between 0 and 1.
- **KTU 14-mark format:** Always present sub-parts as (a) and (b), each with 7 marks, showing the formula, substitution, and numerical answer.
- **Final answer must be boxed or underlined** in the exam script for the examiner's clarity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Problems, Fault Tree Analysis (FTA), Symbols Used in FTA, Fault Tree Construction, Estimation of System Reliability from Fault Tree Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
