> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT754 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cost of Reliability, Factors to Be Considered for Optimizing the Reliability Cost, Reliability Allocation - Basic Concepts, Reliability Allocation for a Series System, Proportional to Uniform Failure Rate Method of Reliability Allocation (Other Methods Need Not Be Introduced)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cost of Reliability — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Standard)**
> **Cost of Reliability** is defined as the total expenditure incurred by an organization to ensure that a product or system achieves and maintains a specified level of reliability throughout its operational life. It is the sum of all costs associated with designing, manufacturing, testing, and sustaining a reliable product, balanced against the cost consequences of system failure.

Mathematically, the **Total Life-Cycle Cost (TLCC)** of a system is expressed as:

$$\text{Total Cost } (C_T) = C_{R} + C_{U}$$

Where:
- $C_{R}$ = **Cost of Reliability (Achieving Reliability)** — money spent to *prevent* failures and *appraise* quality.
- $C_{U}$ = **Cost of Unreliability (Cost of Failure)** — money lost due to *internal* and *external* failure consequences.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 scheme specifically asks for a clear understanding of *why* cost optimization matters. The optimal reliability level is **NOT** 100% — it is the point where the marginal cost of adding reliability equals the marginal benefit of avoiding failures.

---

## 1.2 Components of Reliability Cost

The **Cost of Reliability ($C_R$)** is broadly classified into **three sub-categories**:

| Sub-Category | Meaning | Engineering Examples |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Prevention Costs ($C_{P}$)** | Costs of activities to *avoid* defects | Design reviews, training, supplier qualification, reliability engineering itself |
| **Appraisal Costs ($C_{A}$)** | Costs of *detecting* defects before delivery | Incoming inspection, testing, burn-in, field trials |
| **Manufacturing/Realization Costs ($C_{M}$)** | Costs of *building in* reliability | Higher-grade components, redundancy, derating, tighter tolerances |

The **Cost of Unreliability ($C_U$)** consists of:

| Sub-Category | Meaning | Engineering Examples |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Internal Failure Costs ($C_{IF}$)** | Costs *before* shipment | Rework, scrap, re-testing, schedule overruns |
| **External Failure Costs ($C_{EF}$)** | Costs *after* shipment | Warranty claims, recalls, liability, downtime, lost goodwill |

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Car Maintenance Analogy"

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Intuition for First-Time Learners**
>
> Imagine you own a **maruti car** used for daily commute. You can either:
>
> 1. Spend money on **regular servicing, premium engine oil, new brake pads, and good tyres** (this is your $C_R$).
> 2. Or, save money upfront, but face **engine seizures, brake failures, and roadside breakdowns** (this is your $C_U$).
>
> If you double your servicing budget, breakdowns *almost* vanish — but eventually the extra money you spend on servicing exceeds the money you save on breakdowns. That *sweet spot* is the **Optimum Reliability Level**. Beyond it, every extra rupee spent gives very little additional benefit.

This is the core idea behind **Reliability Cost Optimization** in engineering: there is *always* an economic optimum.

---

## 1.4 Reliability Allocation — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition**
> **Reliability Allocation** (also called **Reliability Apportionment**) is the process of assigning reliability *sub-targets* to the subsystems, subassemblies, or components of a complex system such that the overall system reliability requirement is met, while respecting practical engineering constraints such as complexity, criticality, state of the art, environment, and cost.

**Purpose of Reliability Allocation:**
1. Translates a system-level reliability goal into actionable component-level specifications.
2. Identifies *which* subsystem needs *more* reliability investment.
3. Provides a fair basis for design trade-offs early in the product life cycle.

---

## 1.5 Intuitive Analogy — "The Family Budget Analogy"

> [!TIP]
> **Reliability Allocation = Family Budget Allocation**
>
> Suppose your **monthly household income is ₹1,00,000** and you need to allocate it to: *Rent, Groceries, Education, Healthcare, Entertainment*. You will **not** allocate equally (₹20,000 each). You allocate *more to healthcare for a sick family member*, *less to entertainment*. The constraints (family size, illness, school fees) drive the allocation.
>
> Similarly, in a **satellite system** with overall reliability target $R_S = 0.95$, the *power subsystem* (critical, hard to repair) gets a *stricter* target than the *telemetry subsystem* (non-critical, easy to access). This is what reliability allocation does — it is **budgeting reliability** instead of money.

---

## 1.6 Reliability Allocation for a Series System — The Core Problem

A **series system** fails if *any* one component fails. If there are $n$ components in series, the system reliability is:

$$R_{S}(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_{i}(t)$$

The **allocation problem** is: given a target $R_S^*$ (e.g., 0.99), find $R_1, R_2, \dots, R_n$ such that:

$$R_{S}^{*} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_{i}^{*}$$

with each $R_i^*$ being practically achievable and economically reasonable.

---

## 1.7 Proportional to Uniform Failure Rate Method — Overview

> [!NOTE]
> This method is one of the simplest allocation techniques prescribed in the KTU 2024 syllabus. It assumes that if **all** components were to be designed with the *same* failure rate, the system would achieve a certain baseline reliability. The actual allocation then *redistributes* failure rates in proportion to the difficulty of achieving reliability for each component.

The method is best suited for **early design stages** when limited component data is available.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 Anatomy of the Cost of Reliability Curve

To optimize reliability, we study how each cost component varies with the achieved reliability $R$ (or equivalently, with failure rate $\lambda$).

**Behavioural Analysis:**

- As reliability $R$ **increases** (failure rate $\lambda$ decreases):
  - $C_R$ (prevention + appraisal + manufacturing) **increases** — *exponentially* at very high reliabilities (because parts get disproportionately expensive).
  - $C_U$ (failure costs) **decreases** — rapidly at first, then slowly (you cannot eliminate *all* failures).

- The **Total Cost Curve** $C_T(R) = C_R(R) + C_U(R)$ is **U-shaped** with a unique minimum.

- The point of minimum $C_T$ corresponds to the **Optimum Reliability $R^*$**.

---

## 2.2 Mathematical Condition for Optimum Reliability

To find the optimum, we set the derivative of $C_T$ with respect to $R$ to zero:

$$\frac{dC_T}{dR} = \frac{dC_R}{dR} + \frac{dC_U}{dR} = 0$$

This gives the **economic balance condition**:

$$\frac{dC_R}{dR} = -\frac{dC_U}{dR} = \left\vert \frac{dC_U}{dR} \right\vert$$

**Engineering interpretation:** The optimum is reached when the *marginal cost* of achieving an extra unit of reliability equals the *marginal savings* from avoiding one more unit of failure cost.

---

## 2.3 Factors Considered for Optimizing Reliability Cost

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to enumerate and explain the following factors:

| # | Factor | Effect on Cost Decision |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Failure Consequences (Criticality)** | Higher criticality → accept higher $C_R$ to avoid catastrophic $C_U$ (e.g., aircraft, nuclear). |
| 2 | **State of the Art (Technology Maturity)** | Components near technology frontier cost disproportionately more to improve. |
| 3 | **Operating Environment** | Harsh environments (heat, vibration, dust) require derating → higher $C_R$. |
| 4 | **Mission Time / Required Life** | Longer mission time demands higher reliability → higher $C_R$. |
| 5 | **Maintainability & Downtime Cost** | Easily repairable systems tolerate lower reliability; non-repairable (e.g., satellites) need high $R$. |
| 6 | **Production Volume** | High volume → $C_R$ per unit drops (amortized design/test cost); low volume → $C_R$ dominates. |
| 7 | **Availability of Redundancy** | If redundancy is feasible, cheaper than raising individual component $R$. |
| 8 | **Customer / Regulatory Requirements** | Mandated safety standards set a *floor* on reliability regardless of cost optimum. |
| 9 | **Competition & Market Positioning** | Premium products justify higher $C_R$. |
| 10 | **Warranty / Liability Cost** | High warranty exposure pushes the optimum to higher $R$. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Do *not* confuse "factors affecting the design of a reliable system" with "factors for optimizing reliability cost." The KTU board specifically tests the latter — they are *economic* factors, not technical ones alone.

---

## 2.4 The Reliability Allocation Problem — Formal Statement

**Given:**
- A system with $n$ subsystems (or components) connected in **series**.
- A required **system reliability target** $R_S^*$ over mission time $t$.
- Operational, environmental, and economic data for each subsystem.

**Find:** Reliability targets $R_1^*, R_2^*, \dots, R_n^*$ such that:

$$\prod_{i=1}^{n} R_{i}^{*} \geq R_{S}^{*}$$

subject to practical constraints.

**Underlying Constraint Chain:** Because the product of reliabilities decreases rapidly as $n$ grows, the *individual* component reliability target must be **higher** than the system target. For example, with 10 equal components in series and $R_S = 0.90$, each component needs $R_i = (0.90)^{1/10} \approx 0.9895$.

This rapid shrinkage is called the **Reliability Multiplication Problem** and is the *raison d'être* of allocation methods.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **complete formula set** required for solving Module 4 problems in the KTU 2024 ESE.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description / Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Total Life-Cycle Cost | $C_T = C_R + C_U$ | Currency units (₹, $, etc.) |
| 2 | Cost of Reliability | $C_R = C_P + C_A + C_M$ | Prevention + Appraisal + Manufacturing |
| 3 | Cost of Unreliability | $C_U = C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ | Internal + External Failure |
| 4 | Optimum Condition | $\frac{dC_R}{dR} = -\frac{dC_U}{dR}$ | First-order necessary condition |
| 5 | Series System Reliability | $R_S(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)$ | Multiplicative law |
| 6 | Exponential Series System | $R_S(t) = e^{-\lambda_S t}$, with $\lambda_S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$ | $t$ in hours, $\lambda$ in $\text{failures/hour}$ |
| 7 | Equal Allocation (baseline) | $R_{i,equal} = (R_S^*)^{1/n}$ | Naive equal-target method |
| 8 | Uniform Failure Rate | $\lambda_{uniform} = -\frac{\ln R_S^*}{n \cdot t}$ | Common $\lambda$ if all components identical |
| 9 | Proportional Allocation Factor (weight) | $w_i = \dfrac{n \lambda_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \lambda_j}$ | See Section 3 derivation |
| 10 | Allocated Failure Rate (Proportional Method) | $\lambda_i^* = w_i \cdot \lambda_{uniform} = \dfrac{\lambda_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \lambda_j} \cdot \left( -\frac{\ln R_S^*}{t} \right)$ | Final allocation result |
| 11 | Allocated Reliability | $R_i^*(t) = e^{-\lambda_i^* t}$ | Per-subsystem reliability target |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Convention Used in KTU 2024 Board Answers:**
> - $R_S^*$ or $R_S$ → system reliability target
> - $R_i$ → reliability of $i^{\text{th}}$ component
> - $\lambda_i$ → failure rate of $i^{\text{th}}$ component
> - $n$ → number of components in series
> - $t$ → mission time
> - $w_i$ → allocation weight for component $i$

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of These Concepts

| Field | Application |
|:---|:---|
| **Aerospace** | Satellite subsystems (power, comms, propulsion) are allocated reliability targets to maximize mission success probability within cost caps. |
| **Automotive** | Brake, airbag, and ECU subsystems are allocated higher reliability than infotainment due to safety-criticality. |
| **Nuclear Power** | Reactor protection systems are over-engineered because failure cost is *catastrophic and unbounded*. |
| **Consumer Electronics** | Manufacturers compute optimum reliability to balance warranty cost vs. BOM cost for smartphones, laptops. |
| **Defence / Military** | Ruggedized equipment in extreme environments uses cost-reliability models to justify MIL-spec design choices. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Derivation of the Optimum Reliability Condition

We start by assuming the cost of unreliability $C_U$ decreases as reliability $R$ increases, while the cost of reliability $C_R$ increases as $R$ increases.

A common **modelling assumption** (used in many reliability economics textbooks and accepted in KTU answers) is that the cost of unreliability is inversely related to reliability, and the cost of reliability is exponential in $(1-R)$:

$$C_U(R) = \frac{K_1}{R} \quad \text{and} \quad C_R(R) = K_2 \cdot \left( \frac{1}{1-R} \right)$$

where $K_1$ and $K_2$ are positive cost constants.

**Step 1 — Form the total cost:**

$$C_T(R) = C_R(R) + C_U(R) = \frac{K_2}{1-R} + \frac{K_1}{R}$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate $C_T$ with respect to $R$:**

$$\frac{dC_T}{dR} = \frac{d}{dR}\left[ \frac{K_2}{1-R} \right] + \frac{d}{dR}\left[ \frac{K_1}{R} \right]$$

**Step 3 — Apply quotient/power rule to the first term:**

$$\frac{d}{dR}\left[ \frac{K_2}{1-R} \right] = K_2 \cdot \frac{d}{dR}\left[ (1-R)^{-1} \right] = K_2 \cdot (-1)(1-R)^{-2} \cdot (-1) = \frac{K_2}{(1-R)^2}$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate the second term:**

$$\frac{d}{dR}\left[ \frac{K_1}{R} \right] = K_1 \cdot \frac{d}{dR}\left[ R^{-1} \right] = K_1 \cdot (-1) R^{-2} = -\frac{K_1}{R^2}$$

**Step 5 — Sum and set equal to zero for optimum:**

$$\frac{K_2}{(1-R)^2} - \frac{K_1}{R^2} = 0$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the optimum reliability $R^*$:**

$$\frac{K_2}{(1-R^*)^2} = \frac{K_1}{(R^*)^2}$$

Taking the positive square root of both sides:

$$\frac{\sqrt{K_2}}{1-R^*} = \frac{\sqrt{K_1}}{R^*}$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$R^* \sqrt{K_2} = (1-R^*) \sqrt{K_1}$$

$$R^* \sqrt{K_2} + R^* \sqrt{K_1} = \sqrt{K_1}$$

$$R^* \left( \sqrt{K_1} + \sqrt{K_2} \right) = \sqrt{K_1}$$

$$\boxed{R^* = \frac{\sqrt{K_1}}{\sqrt{K_1} + \sqrt{K_2}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The optimum reliability $R^*$ is the ratio $\sqrt{K_1} / (\sqrt{K_1} + \sqrt{K_2})$. If failure cost constant $K_1$ is *much larger* than reliability cost constant $K_2$ (i.e., failures are catastrophic), $R^* \to 1$. If $K_2 \gg K_1$, $R^* \to 0$. This is the **economic balance** the KTU board expects students to articulate.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Equal Allocation (Baseline)

For a series system with $n$ components and target $R_S^*$:

**Step 1 — Series reliability:**

$$R_S^* = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_{i,equal}$$

**Step 2 — Apply equal allocation assumption $R_1 = R_2 = \dots = R_n = R$:**

$$R_S^* = R^n$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $R$:**

$$R = (R_S^*)^{1/n}$$

This is the **naive baseline** — every component gets the same target. The Proportional method improves on this by incorporating *relative difficulty*.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Uniform Failure Rate (Common Baseline)

For a series system with exponential failure distributions and mission time $t$:

**Step 1 — System reliability target:**

$$R_S^*(t) = e^{-\lambda_S t}$$

**Step 2 — Sum of failure rates for series system:**

$$\lambda_S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$$

**Step 3 — Assume all $n$ components have the *same* (uniform) failure rate $\lambda_u$:**

$$\lambda_S = n \cdot \lambda_u$$

**Step 4 — Express the system target in terms of $\lambda_u$:**

$$R_S^*(t) = e^{-n \lambda_u t}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\lambda_u$:**

$$-n \lambda_u t = \ln R_S^*$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_u = -\frac{\ln R_S^*}{n \cdot t}}$$

This $\lambda_u$ is the *common* failure rate that *would* deliver the system target if all $n$ components truly had equal difficulty.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Proportional to Uniform Failure Rate Method

This method assumes the components have **known individual failure rates** $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \dots, \lambda_n$ (from prior data or engineering judgment). The *uniform* failure rate computed above becomes the **baseline**, and each component's allocation is **proportional to its share** of the total $\lambda$.

**Step 1 — Compute total observed failure rate:**

$$\Lambda_{total} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \lambda_j$$

**Step 2 — Define the allocation weight for component $i$:**

The weight represents *how much of the total failure budget* belongs to component $i$:

$$w_i = \frac{\lambda_i}{\Lambda_{total}} = \frac{\lambda_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \lambda_j}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on "Proportional to Uniform Failure Rate" Name:** Some textbooks define the weight slightly differently as $w_i = \dfrac{n \lambda_i}{\sum \lambda_j}$. Both definitions are equivalent and yield the same allocated failure rate. The KTU board accepts either form, provided the final $\lambda_i^*$ is correct.

**Step 3 — Compute the allocated failure rate for component $i$:**

The allocated failure rate is the *uniform* failure rate scaled by the weight:

$$\lambda_i^* = w_i \cdot \lambda_u = \frac{\lambda_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \lambda_j} \cdot \left( -\frac{\ln R_S^*}{t} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Simplify using $\lambda_S = -\frac{\ln R_S^*}{t}$:**

$$\boxed{\lambda_i^* = \frac{\lambda_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \lambda_j} \cdot \lambda_S = \frac{\lambda_i \cdot \lambda_S}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \lambda_j}}$$

**Step 5 — Verify the constraint** (sum of allocated failure rates equals system failure rate):

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i^* = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\lambda_i \cdot \lambda_S}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \lambda_j} = \frac{\lambda_S \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \lambda_j} = \lambda_S \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 6 — Compute the allocated reliability target for each component:**

$$R_i^*(t) = e^{-\lambda_i^* t}$$

**Step 7 — Verify the system target is met:**

$$\prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i^* = \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{-\lambda_i^* t} = e^{-t \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i^*} = e^{-t \lambda_S} = R_S^* \quad \checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The mathematical consistency check in Steps 5 and 7 is what guarantees the method is **internally valid** — the KTU examiner often awards partial credit for showing this verification.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Worked Example (Step-by-Step)

> **Problem:** A system consists of **3 components in series** with known failure rates $\lambda_1 = 0.001$, $\lambda_2 = 0.002$, $\lambda_3 = 0.003$ failures per hour. The mission time is $t = 100$ hours. The required system reliability is $R_S^* = 0.90$. Using the **Proportional to Uniform Failure Rate Method**, allocate reliabilities.

**Step 1 — Compute the system failure rate from the target:**

$$\lambda_S = -\frac{\ln R_S^*}{t} = -\frac{\ln 0.90}{100} = -\frac{(-0.10536)}{100} = 0.0010536 \text{ failures/hour}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the sum of known failure rates:**

$$\Lambda_{total} = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \lambda_3 = 0.001 + 0.002 + 0.003 = 0.006 \text{ failures/hour}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the uniform failure rate (baseline for cross-check):**

$$\lambda_u = \frac{\lambda_S}{n} = \frac{0.0010536}{3} = 0.0003512 \text{ failures/hour}$$

**Step 4 — Compute weights:**

$$w_1 = \frac{0.001}{0.006} = 0.1667 \quad w_2 = \frac{0.002}{0.006} = 0.3333 \quad w_3 = \frac{0.003}{0.006} = 0.5000$$

**Step 5 — Compute allocated failure rates:**

$$\lambda_1^* = w_1 \cdot \lambda_S = 0.1667 \times 0.0010536 = 0.0001756$$

$$\lambda_2^* = w_2 \cdot \lambda_S = 0.3333 \times 0.0010536 = 0.0003512$$

$$\lambda_3^* = w_3 \cdot \lambda_S = 0.5000 \times 0.0010536 = 0.0005268$$

**Step 6 — Compute allocated reliabilities at $t = 100$ hours:**

$$R_1^* = e^{-0.0001756 \times 100} = e^{-0.01756} = 0.9826$$

$$R_2^* = e^{-0.0003512 \times 100} = e^{-0.03512} = 0.9655$$

$$R_3^* = e^{-0.0005268 \times 100} = e^{-0.05268} = 0.9487$$

**Step 7 — Verify:**

$$R_1^* \times R_2^* \times R_3^* = 0.9826 \times 0.9655 \times 0.9487 \approx 0.9000 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 8 — Engineering Interpretation:**

Component 3 (highest $\lambda$) is given the *largest* portion of the failure budget (50%), meaning it is *allowed* the *lowest* reliability target. This correctly reflects engineering reality — we don't punish a low-$\lambda$ component by demanding superhuman reliability from it; we allocate *in proportion* to its existing difficulty.

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** Always show the verification step in Step 7. Examiners reward it generously.

---

## 3.6 Comparison Matrix — Equal vs. Proportional Allocation

| Aspect | Equal Allocation | Proportional to Uniform Failure Rate |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Data Required** | Only $R_S^*$ and $n$ | $R_S^*$, $n$, individual $\lambda_i$ |
| **Realism** | Low — ignores component differences | High — respects prior failure data |
| **Computational Effort** | Trivial | Simple (2–3 extra steps) |
| **Best Used When** | Early design, no failure data | Preliminary design, some data available |
| **Limitation** | May over-spec low-criticality parts | Requires reliable $\lambda_i$ estimates |
| **System Target Met?** | Yes, by construction | Yes, by mathematical verification |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Cost of Reliability `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define "Cost of Reliability" and "Cost of Unreliability". List any four factors considered for optimizing reliability cost.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Cost of Reliability ($C_R$):** It is the total expenditure incurred in *preventing* failures, *appraising* quality, and *building* reliability into a product during design, development, and manufacturing stages. It includes prevention costs (design reviews, training), appraisal costs (testing, inspection), and realization costs (better components, redundancy).

> **Cost of Unreliability ($C_U$):** It is the total cost consequence of *failures*, both internal (rework, scrap, re-test) before delivery and external (warranty, recall, downtime, customer dissatisfaction) after delivery.

> **Four factors for optimizing reliability cost:**
> 1. **Failure consequences / criticality** of the system (catastrophic → justify higher $C_R$).
> 2. **State of the art** of the technology (mature tech is cheaper to improve).
> 3. **Operating environment** (harsh environment needs derating and higher $C_R$).
> 4. **Mission time / required operational life** (longer life → higher $R$ target → higher $C_R$).
>
> *(Other acceptable factors: maintainability, production volume, redundancy feasibility, warranty cost, regulatory requirements.)*

---

### Question 2: Reliability Allocation `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is reliability allocation? Why is it necessary in the design of complex systems?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Reliability Allocation** is the process of *apportioning* an overall system reliability target among the subsystems, subassemblies, or components such that the system requirement is met, while each subsystem target is practically achievable and economically reasonable.

> **Why it is necessary:**
> 1. **Translates a system-level goal into actionable component-level specifications** for designers.
> 2. **Ensures fair distribution** — critical components get higher targets, less critical ones get lower ones.
> 3. **Drives design trade-offs** early in the product life cycle when changes are cheapest.
> 4. **Avoids over-specification** of low-criticality components (which wastes cost) and **under-specification** of critical ones (which risks system failure).
> 5. **Forms a baseline** for testing, qualification, and acceptance decisions.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain with a neat diagram the cost-reliability trade-off. How is the optimum reliability determined? State the factors that influence the optimization of reliability cost. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A series system has **4 components** with failure rates $\lambda_1 = 0.0005$, $\lambda_2 = 0.0010$, $\lambda_3 = 0.0015$, $\lambda_4 = 0.0020$ failures/hour. The mission time is $t = 50$ hours and the system reliability target is $R_S^* = 0.95$. Using the **Proportional to Uniform Failure Rate Method**, allocate the failure rate and reliability to each component. Verify that the system reliability target is met. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution A(a):

**Cost-Reliability Trade-off Diagram:**

> *(Refer to the schematic in Section 4.4 of these notes — the U-shaped $C_T$ curve with minimum at $R^*$.)*

**Explanation:**

The total cost $C_T$ of a system is the sum of $C_R$ (cost of achieving reliability) and $C_U$ (cost of unreliability). As reliability $R$ increases from a low value, $C_U$ drops rapidly while $C_R$ rises slowly; the total $C_T$ first decreases. Beyond an optimum $R^*$, the marginal cost of additional reliability rises faster than the savings from avoided failures, so $C_T$ begins to increase again. The curve is therefore **U-shaped**.

**Optimum Reliability Determination:**

The optimum is found by minimizing $C_T(R) = C_R(R) + C_U(R)$. Setting $\dfrac{dC_T}{dR} = 0$:

$$\frac{dC_R}{dR} = -\frac{dC_U}{dR} = \left\vert \frac{dC_U}{dR} \right\vert$$

This is the **economic balance condition**: marginal cost of adding reliability = marginal savings from avoided failures.

**Factors Influencing Reliability Cost Optimization (any 4-6):**

1. **Failure consequences (criticality)** — catastrophic failures justify high $C_R$.
2. **State of the art** — frontier-technology components cost disproportionately more.
3. **Operating environment** — harsh conditions need derating.
4. **Mission time** — longer mission → higher required $R$ → higher $C_R$.
5. **Maintainability** — easily repairable systems tolerate lower $R$.
6. **Production volume** — high volume amortizes $C_R$ per unit.
7. **Warranty and liability cost** — high exposure shifts optimum upward.
8. **Redundancy feasibility** — may be cheaper than raising individual $R$.

---

#### Solution A(b):

**Given:** $n = 4$, $\lambda_1 = 0.0005$, $\lambda_2 = 0.0010$, $\lambda_3 = 0.0015$, $\lambda_4 = 0.0020$ per hour, $t = 50$ hours, $R_S^* = 0.95$.

**Step 1 — Compute system failure rate from target:**

$$\lambda_S = -\frac{\ln 0.95}{50} = -\frac{(-0.05129)}{50} = 0.0010259 \text{ failures/hour}$$

**Step 2 — Compute sum of known failure rates:**

$$\Lambda_{total} = 0.0005 + 0.0010 + 0.0015 + 0.0020 = 0.0050 \text{ failures/hour}$$

**Step 3 — Compute weights $w_i = \lambda_i / \Lambda_{total}$:**

$$w_1 = \frac{0.0005}{0.0050} = 0.10 \quad w_2 = \frac{0.0010}{0.0050} = 0.20$$

$$w_3 = \frac{0.0015}{0.0050} = 0.30 \quad w_4 = \frac{0.0020}{0.0050} = 0.40$$

**Step 4 — Compute allocated failure rates $\lambda_i^* = w_i \cdot \lambda_S$:**

$$\lambda_1^* = 0.10 \times 0.0010259 = 0.00010259$$

$$\lambda_2^* = 0.20 \times 0.0010259 = 0.00020518$$

$$\lambda_3^* = 0.30 \times 0.0010259 = 0.00030777$$

$$\lambda_4^* = 0.40 \times 0.0010259 = 0.00041036$$

**Step 5 — Compute allocated reliabilities $R_i^* = e^{-\lambda_i^* \cdot t}$:**

$$R_1^* = e^{-0.00010259 \times 50} = e^{-0.005130} = 0.9949$$

$$R_2^* = e^{-0.00020518 \times 50} = e^{-0.010259} = 0.9898$$

$$R_3^* = e^{-0.00030777 \times 50} = e^{-0.015389} = 0.9847$$

$$R_4^* = e^{-0.00041036 \times 50} = e^{-0.020518} = 0.9797$$

**Step 6 — Verify system reliability:**

$$R_S^{computed} = R_1^* \times R_2^* \times R_3^* \times R_4^*$$

$$= 0.9949 \times 0.9898 \times 0.9847 \times 0.9797$$

Computing sequentially:

$$0.9949 \times 0.9898 = 0.9848$$

$$0.9848 \times 0.9847 = 0.9697$$

$$0.9697 \times 0.9797 = 0.9500$$

$$R_S^{computed} \approx 0.9500 = R_S^* \quad \checkmark$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Component 4 (originally highest failure rate) is allocated the largest *share* of the failure budget (40%) and therefore the lowest reliability target (0.9797). Component 1 (lowest original failure rate) is held to the highest standard (0.9949). This is consistent with the engineering reality that demanding a 0.9999 target from an already-reliable component is wasteful.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Define the following terms with suitable examples: (i) Prevention cost (ii) Appraisal cost (iii) Internal failure cost (iv) External failure cost. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A series system has **3 components** with current individual reliabilities $R_1 = 0.98$, $R_2 = 0.95$, $R_3 = 0.92$ over a mission time of $t = 200$ hours. The overall system reliability target is $R_S^* = 0.90$. Discuss whether the current design meets the target, and if not, use the **Proportional to Uniform Failure Rate Method** to re-allocate reliability targets. Assume the components have exponential failure distributions. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution B(a):

**(i) Prevention Cost ($C_P$):** Costs of activities undertaken to *prevent* defects from occurring in the first place. Examples: reliability design reviews, quality training programs, supplier qualification audits, design FMEA workshops, robust design methodologies.

**(ii) Appraisal Cost ($C_A$):** Costs of activities to *detect* defects *before* the product is shipped to the customer. Examples: incoming material inspection, in-process inspection, final product testing, environmental stress screening (burn-in), field trial evaluations.

**(iii) Internal Failure Cost ($C_{IF}$):** Costs of defects discovered *after* manufacture but *before* shipment to the customer. Examples: scrap and rework, re-testing of repaired units, schedule overruns, investigation of defects, production line downtime.

**(iv) External Failure Cost ($C_{EF}$):** Costs of defects discovered *after* the product reaches the customer. Examples: warranty repairs, product recalls, legal liability claims, customer compensation, loss of goodwill, downtime cost to the customer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quality Insight:** Joseph Juran's classic principle (and the basis of KTU 2024's cost-of-quality framework) states: *As prevention and appraisal costs rise, internal and external failure costs fall faster, lowering total cost up to the optimum.*

---

#### Solution B(b):

**Step 1 — Compute current failure rates from given reliabilities:**

Using $R_i = e^{-\lambda_i t}$, we have $\lambda_i = -\dfrac{\ln R_i}{t}$:

$$\lambda_1 = -\frac{\ln 0.98}{200} = -\frac{(-0.02020)}{200} = 0.0001010 \text{ per hour}$$

$$\lambda_2 = -\frac{\ln 0.95}{200} = -\frac{(-0.05129)}{200} = 0.0002565 \text{ per hour}$$

$$\lambda_3 = -\frac{\ln 0.92}{200} = -\frac{(-0.08338)}{200} = 0.0004169 \text{ per hour}$$

**Step 2 — Check current system reliability:**

$$R_S^{current} = 0.98 \times 0.95 \times 0.92$$

$$= 0.98 \times 0.8740 = 0.8565$$

$$R_S^{current} \approx 0.8565 < R_S^* = 0.90$$

> **Conclusion:** The current design **does NOT meet** the target. Reliability must be improved.

**Step 3 — Compute system target failure rate:**

$$\lambda_S = -\frac{\ln 0.90}{200} = -\frac{(-0.10536)}{200} = 0.0005268 \text{ per hour}$$

**Step 4 — Compute sum of current failure rates:**

$$\Lambda_{total} = 0.0001010 + 0.0002565 + 0.0004169 = 0.0007744 \text{ per hour}$$

**Step 5 — Compute weights:**

$$w_1 = \frac{0.0001010}{0.0007744} = 0.1304 \quad w_2 = \frac{0.0002565}{0.0007744} = 0.3312$$

$$w_3 = \frac{0.0004169}{0.0007744} = 0.5383$$

(Check: $0.1304 + 0.3312 + 0.5383 \approx 1.000$ ✓)

**Step 6 — Compute allocated failure rates:**

$$\lambda_1^* = 0.1304 \times 0.0005268 = 0.0000687$$

$$\lambda_2^* = 0.3312 \times 0.0005268 = 0.0001745$$

$$\lambda_3^* = 0.5383 \times 0.0005268 = 0.0002836$$

**Step 7 — Compute allocated reliabilities:**

$$R_1^* = e^{-0.0000687 \times 200} = e^{-0.01374} = 0.9864$$

$$R_2^* = e^{-0.0001745 \times 200} = e^{-0.03490} = 0.9657$$

$$R_3^* = e^{-0.0002836 \times 200} = e^{-0.05672} = 0.9449$$

**Step 8 — Verify:**

$$R_S^{allocated} = 0.9864 \times 0.9657 \times 0.9449$$

$$= 0.9864 \times 0.9657 = 0.9526$$

$$0.9526 \times 0.9449 = 0.9000$$

$$R_S^{allocated} \approx 0.9000 = R_S^* \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 9 — Improvement Required:**

| Component | Current $R$ | Allocated $R^*$ | Improvement |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 0.9800 | 0.9864 | +0.0064 |
| 2 | 0.9500 | 0.9657 | +0.0157 |
| 3 | 0.9200 | 0.9449 | +0.0249 |

Component 3 needs the *largest* improvement — this is the weakest link, and the Proportional method correctly identifies it.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Board Exams**
>
> 1. **Formula Mis-application (–2 marks):** Students often confuse the *Equal Allocation* formula $R_i = (R_S^*)^{1/n}$ with the *Proportional* method. The proportional method requires *individual $\lambda_i$ values* as input; it is **not** simply an $(R_S^*)^{1/n}$ computation.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Mission Time (–1 to –2 marks):** In exponential failure distributions, $R_i = e^{-\lambda_i t}$ — omitting $t$ in the exponent is a frequent error. Always explicitly state $t$ in your computation.
>
> 3. **No Verification Step (–1 mark):** Examiners reward students who *verify* that $\prod R_i^* = R_S^*$. Skipping this loses an easy mark.
>
> 4. **Confusing Cost of Reliability with Selling Price (–1 to –2 marks):** Cost of Reliability is an *internal engineering cost*, not the market price. Do not include marketing, distribution, or profit margins.
>
> 5. **Mixing Series and Parallel Formulas (–2 marks):** This module is *exclusively* about series systems for allocation. Do not write parallel-system formulas (e.g., $R_S = 1 - \prod(1 - R_i)$) in this context.
>
> 6. **Skipping Units (–0.5 mark):** Always state units for failure rates (failures/hour or $\text{hr}^{-1}$) and reliability (dimensionless).
>
> 7. **No Diagram for Cost Trade-off (–1 to –2 marks):** For 7-mark questions on the cost-reliability trade-off, a labelled U-shaped curve is **mandatory**. Examiners explicitly allocate marks for the diagram.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**
>
> **Core Concepts:**
> - **Cost of Reliability ($C_R$):** Prevention + Appraisal + Manufacturing cost.
> - **Cost of Unreliability ($C_U$):** Internal + External failure cost.
> - **Total Cost Curve** is **U-shaped** with unique minimum.
> - **Optimum** occurs when $\dfrac{dC_R}{dR} = -\dfrac{dC_U}{dR}$.
> - **Reliability Allocation** = budgeting reliability to subsystems in proportion to their difficulty / failure rate.
>
> **Key Formulas (memorize verbatim):**
> - Series system: $R_S(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)$.
> - Exponential series: $\lambda_S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$ and $R_S(t) = e^{-\lambda_S t}$.
> - Uniform failure rate: $\lambda_u = -\dfrac{\ln R_S^*}{n \cdot t}$.
> - Proportional weight: $w_i = \dfrac{\lambda_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \lambda_j}$.
> - Allocated failure rate: $\lambda_i^* = w_i \cdot \lambda_S$.
> - Allocated reliability: $R_i^* = e^{-\lambda_i^* t}$.
>
> **Key Principles:**
> - The Proportional method *redistributes* the failure budget in proportion to existing $\lambda_i$.
> - Higher original $\lambda_i$ → higher weight → larger share of system failure budget → lower allocated $R_i^*$.
> - Always verify: $\sum \lambda_i^* = \lambda_S$ and $\prod R_i^* = R_S^*$.
> - Allocation is an *early-design* activity — design changes are cheapest at this stage.
>
> **Factor Categories for Cost Optimization (memorize 4–6):**
> 1. Failure consequences (criticality)
> 2. State of the art (technology maturity)
> 3. Operating environment
> 4. Mission time
> 5. Maintainability & downtime
> 6. Production volume
> 7. Warranty / liability cost
> 8. Redundancy feasibility
> 9. Regulatory requirements
>
> **Don't Confuse:**
> - Reliability **Allocation** (this module) vs. Reliability **Prediction** (Module 2/3) — allocation goes *top-down*; prediction goes *bottom-up*.
> - Equal allocation vs. Proportional allocation — equal ignores $\lambda_i$; proportional uses them.
> - Cost of **Reliability** vs. Cost of **Unreliability** — first is investment, second is loss.
>
> **Numerical Problem Workflow (always follow this order):**
> 1. Identify series system and target $R_S^*$, mission $t$, $\lambda_i$.
> 2. Compute $\lambda_S = -\ln(R_S^*)/t$.
> 3. Compute $\sum \lambda_i$.
> 4. Compute $w_i = \lambda_i / \sum \lambda_j$.
> 5. Compute $\lambda_i^* = w_i \cdot \lambda_S$.
> 6. Compute $R_i^* = \exp(-\lambda_i^* t)$.
> 7. Verify $\prod R_i^* = R_S^*$.
> 8. Interpret the allocation result.
